## Page 4

PREFACE
Three censuses of population have been taken since the Government of Northern
Ireland was established.
The first enumeration took place in 1926,and incorporated
questions relating to occupation and industry, orphanhood and infirmities.
The second
enumeration made in 1937 was of more limited scope and was intended to bridge the gap
between the census of 1926 and the census which it was proposed to take in 1941, but
which had to be abandoned owing to the outbreak of war.
The census taken as at mid-
night of 8th-9th April, 1951, forms the "basis of this report and like that in 1926
question's were asked as to the occupations and industries of the population.
The length of time required to process the data collected at an enumeration before
it can be presented in the ultimate reports is necessarily considerable.
In order to
meet immediate requirements, however, two Preliminary Reports on the 1951 census were
published.
The first of these gave the population figures by administrative areas and
towns and villages, and by Counties and County Boroughs according to religious pro-
fession.
The Second Report, which was restricted to Counties and Countj^ Boroughs,
gave the population by age groups.
The present Volume completes the series of seven county volumes (the Belfast
County Borough and Counties of Antrim, Down, Londonderry, Armagh and Tyrone volumes
having been published already) and has been prepared (as were also the Preliminary
Reports) in accordance with the provisions of the Census Act (Northern Ireland) 1951o
The statistics now published supersede those shown in the Preliminary Reports and may
be accepted as final.
A General Report for the whole of Northern Ireland will be published as soon as
possible.
This will bring together, and in some respects show in greater detail, the
information contained in the county volumes.
Additional features will also be intro-
duced particularly in regard to the distribution of the people industrially by age and
marital condition.
Following the 1926 census a topographical index was published dealing with each
type of area for which statistics were supplied in the county series.
A supplement to
this index was published after the 1937 census and a further supplement in respect qf
the 1951 census will be prepared in due course.
Census publications are obtainable either directly from H.M. Stationery Office,
Chichester House, Chichester Street, Belfast, or from any book-seller.
L. C. MULLIGAN,
REGISTRAR-GENERAL
General Register
Office,
Fermanagh House,
Belfast.
June, 1954.
(18156)

## Page 8

PART I
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF TERMS
USED IN THIS VOLUME
AREA
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey (foreshore being
excluded), and relate to the several administrative areas as constituted at the date
of the 1951 census.
The acreage covered by the larger rivers and lakes is not
included in the body of Tables 4 and 5 but is given in footnotes thereto.
POPULATION
The population comprises all persons on land or in vessels on inland waters on
census night.
Members of the Forces of the Crovm are also included.
ADMINISTRATIVE DIVISIONS
The County is divided into four county districts (one municipal borough and three
rural districts) comprising 79 district electoral divisions, three of which are
coterminous with the wards of the municipal borough (see table 4).
Wards, in muni-
cipal boroughs or urban districts, are the units for the purpose of municipal
elections.
In rural districts the unit is the district electoral division.
The
County is also divided into 20 county electoral divisions, which are the units for the
purpose of county council elections (see table 5).
Owing to the abolition of the Boards of Guardians, details of the Poor Law Union
and Dispensary districts are not given in this volume.
These areas, however, form
respectively, superintendent registrars* and registrars* districts for the registration
of marriages, births and deaths, and consequently the census tabulations have been pre-
pared in a manner suitable for the extraction of data according to these areas should
such be required.
There are three parliamentary constituencies for elections to the Northern Ireland
Parliament.
For elections to the United Kingdom Parliament South Tyrone with County
Fermanagh forms one constituency for which there is one Member of Parliament (see
note (c) below and Table 9).
The Tyrone Education Area is coterpiinous with the County.
The changes made in the constitution or boundaries of administrative divisions of
the County during the last intercensal period are as follows:-
(a) The boundary of Enniskillen Urban District was extended (1st April, 1945 and

    1st April, 1947) and made a Municipal Borough (30th May, 1949).
(b) A portion of Enniskillen Rural District was transferred to Enniskillen Urban
District (1st April, 1945 and 1st April, 1947).
(c) Under the provisions of the Representation of the People Act, 1948 South
Tyrone and Fermanagh were' created a single constituency for elections to the
United Kingdom Parliament.
HOUSES
1.
The term "House" means
(a) each structurally separate dwelling (except institutions) having distinct
access to the street, common stair, or landing.
(18156)


---

## Page 9

(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanitorium, nursing home, asylum, religious,
welfare or charitable community, residental school, barracks or other premises
under Naval, Military, Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other place of
detention), regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts.
(c) each boat, caravan, tent or other movable dwelling if occupied on census
night but not otherwise.
2.
The term ^Trivate House^- means a dv\jelling other than an institution, hotel,
boarding house, premises occupied by a caretaker, vessel, movable dwelling, barn,
stable or outhouse occupied on census night.
The term includes premises comprising a
shop with dwelling attached, unless occupied by a caretaker.
3o
The terms ^^Boarding House'^ and "Lodging House^' cover habitations described as
such, and any private house containing three or more boarders or lodgers.
4.
The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons on census
night.
"Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those temporarily vacant through
the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Enumerators were
instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in such an advanced state of
decay that there was little likelihood that they would again be made fit for habitation,
ROOMS
1.
The rooms counted are the usual living rooms including bedrooms and kitchens but
excluding sculleries (i.e., compartments used only for washing, cleaning, or
cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets, bathrooms, or any warehouse, office
or shop rooms.
2.
For the classification of households according to rooms occupied (see table 7),
shared rooms - i.e., those used in common by two or more households - have been
included in the number of rooms occupied by the main household in the dwelling.
In
cases of one room occupations, all persons living therein, whether related or not, have
been enumerated as one census household.
HOUSEHOLDS
lo
"Non-private Households" comprise all persons enumerated in hotels, boarding
houses, lodging houses, shops and institutions (see para. l(b) under "Houses"
definition above).
2.
"Private Households" are comprised of single persons or groups of persons occupying
separately the whole, or part of any private dwelling (see para. 2 under "Houses"
definition above).
Visitors and domestic servants are included in the household with
which they were erjumerated.
Lodgers having or sharing separate accommodation were
enumerated on separate returns and consequently are regarded as separate private
households.
It should be specially noted that a household or private household for census
purposes includes any group of persons, whether related or not, who live together in
separate house or room accommodation.
RATEABLE VALUATION
The figures shown are those for the year ended 31st March, 1952, and have been
supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Subject to the provisions of the Local Government (Eating and Finance) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1929, agricultural land and agricultural buildings are completely
derated and industrial hereditaments and freight-transport hereditaments are partially
derated, the consequent deficiency in the yield of rates being made good by way of
General Exchequer contributions.
The figures in Tables 4 and 5 are the totals of the valuations in the various
areas before reduction on account of derating, but exclude in certain cases the valua-
tions of electricity, gas and water mains, telegraphs, telephones, fisheries, water-
works and tramways.
The total of such excluded valuations for the county is given in
a foot-note to the table.
(18156)


---

## Page 10

Vll
USUAL RESIDENCE
The persons who have been regarded as having their usual residence in the area of
enumeration (i.e., the County) include:-
(1) Those enumerated in their own houses;
(2)
Persons having settled residence in private lodgings;
(3) Resident domestic servants;
(4) Scholars, students, etc., Living during term time in residential schools,
etc.;
(5) Inmates of institutions when the nature of the institution suggested
permanent residence;
(6) Crews of vessels if usually living on shipboard.
BIRTHPLACES
The birthplaces of a small number of persons were not stated on the census return;
it was assumed that these persons were born in the county or county borough of enumera-
tion, except when they were stated to be visitors to the household, or bore foreign
names.
After further enquiry in the latter cases it was found possible to classify
them under definite headings.
RELIGIONS
The classification of religions includes a separate heading for each religion,
the adherents of which number 10 or more (see Table 16).
The heading ^Presbyterian"
includes those who call themselves by this name without qualification, together with
those who describe themselves as members of the Church of Scotland.
Other
Presbyterian denominations are shown at the foot of Table 16.
Members of the Church
of England and the Episcopal Church of Scotland have been included under the heading
"Church of Ireland".
The l a t t e r group includes all persons returned as "Church" or
"Episcopalian".
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
There are two categories into which the population may be grouped in their
working capacity, namely, occupation and industry.
The sole criterion for determining
the occupational classification is the type of work performed.
On the other hand,
industry has to do with the kind of business with which the occupation is associated.
Thus a carpenter working for a firm of shipbuilders would be classified under
"Carpenter" for occupation but under "Shipbuilding" for industry.
The 1951 Censuses were carried out simiiltaneously in all parts of the British
Isles and this afforded the first opportunity for Northern Ireland to adopt both
occupational and industrial classifications* on exactly the same lines as those used
for the rest of the United Kingdom.
In the preparation of Tables 19 and 21 the range of Orders, Sub-Orders and
Occupational and Industrial unit groups have been modified by combining closely related
groups where the numbers were small and the preservation or usefulness of the data was
not harmed.
TABLES CONTAINING DOTS ( . )
AND DASHES
( - )
Throughout this volume dots (.) arc used in tabular matter to indicate "None", and
dashes (-) are used to indicate that figures are not available or that the column
headings are not relevant to the lines in which the dashes appear.
* Classification of Occupations, 1950.
H.M.S.O.
Price £1 10s. Od. net.
Census 1951, Classification of Industries.
H.M.S.O.
Price 8s. 6d. net.
(18156)

## Page 11

CORRECTION OF CENSUS DATA
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the census data.
The process of correction began with the enumera-
tors, who received full instructions as to the action to be taken in cases of error or
omission, and it was continued by a system of check and further enquiry where necessary
applied at each stage of the office work.
The process was completed by an intensive
scrutiny and inter-comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny and comparison
revealed a certain number of incongruities, which, when material, were investigated and
corrected in the light of the facts disclosed; trifling errors were adjusted
arbitrarily.
(18156)


---

## Page 12

PART II
STATISTICAL NOTES
The definitions, etc., given in the preceding section apply equally to these notes
and to the tables in Part III.
AREA
Descriptions and figures are given for areas as constituted at the date of the
1951 census unless otherwise stated.
It will be seen from Table 1 (Part III) that the total area of Fermanagh County
exclusive of the larger rivers and lakes, and of land on the shores of Loughs Erne,
Castlehulme and Macnean is 653 square miles or 12.5 per cent of the area of Northern
Ireland.
POPULATION
The total enumeration for the County was 53,044, a decrease of 1,525 or 2.8 per
cent since 1937.
For each intercensal period between 1841 and 1951 a decrease in
population was shown and the 1951 figure is the lowest on record.
Table 2 shows that
censuses were taken decennially between 1821 and. 1911 and thereafter at irregular
intervals; this should be borne in mind when comparing data of one enumeration with
another.
During the whole range of enumerations the highest point was reached in 1841
when the figure was 156,852 or almost three times as high as in 1951.
The two periods

    between 1821 and 1841 showed a combined increase of 25,855 persons but in the following
    period (1841-1851) there was a decrease of 40,411 and although this was the greatest
    intercensal rate of decrease (25.8) the decreases for the periods 1861-1871 and
    1881-1891 (the next highest) were 12.3 and 12.6 respectively.
Natural Increase: The following table sets out figures of the natural increase
and actual decrease of population in each of the intercensal periods since 1871, and
shows the net loss by migration.

Comparable figures of natural increase due to the excess of births over deaths are
not available for periods prior to 1871, but i t is evident that in each of the inter-

    censal periods from 1841 to 1871, as well as from 1871 to 1951, there was a net outward
movement of such dimensions as not only to counterbalance the natural increase, but to
cause a marked decrease in population.

* The figures given relate to each area as constituted at the date of the 1951 Census^
i Estimated Figure.
As the administrative divisions of the County are constituted at present, 46»'?22
live in rural areas and 6,322 in the one urban area.
The urbeoi population constitutes

    11.9 per cent of the whole as compared with 8.9 in 1937, 8.4 in 192&^ 7.8 in 1911, and
8.3 in 1901 - the areas being as constituted at the date of each census.
It will be
seen that all rural districts show decreases during the fourteen yesrs 1937-1951:-
Enniskillen (3.5 or 564 persons), Irvinestown (4.8 or 611 persons) and Lisnaskea (5.3
or 1,046 persons).
The total increase in the urban population ig 696 or 12.4 per cent
and the overall decrease in the rural population is 2, 221 or 4.5 per cent *• the areas
being the same for both censuses.
Scanning the whole course of fifty years (1901-1951) the urban population shows an
increase of 565 persons or 9.8 per cent, while the rural population shows an overall
decrease of 12,951 persons or 21. 7 per cent.
All rural districts show substantial

    decreases:- Enniskillen (4,093 or 20.6 per cent), Irvinestown (5,801 or 23.8 per cent)
and Lisnaskea (5,057 or 21.2 per cent).
The density of population in Enniskillen Municipal Borough is 7.0 persons per acre
and in each rural district the rate is 0.1 persons per acre.
For the whole County the
rate is 0.1 persons per acre.
Tables 4 and 5 provide detailed information regarding area, population, housing
and accommodation for the various sub-div.islons of the County and incorporate figures
of rateable valuation.

    The rateable valuation of .the County in 1951 was £284, 759
before reduction on account of derating and £85,230 after reduction on account of
derating.
In 1937 the corresponding valuations of the Cotinty were £274,239 and
£72,415 respectively.
Table 4 includes information regarding towns and villages containing 50 houses and
upwards with no legally defined boundaries.
Parliamentary Constituencies; Table 9 sets out the Parliamentary Constituencies
of the County for the Parliaments of both the United Kingdom and Northern Ireland
together with the figures of population, number of electors and electors per 1,000 of
the population, distinction being shown in respect of electors with residence
qualification.
Under the provisions of the Elections and Franchise Act (Northern Ireland), 1946,
a person is entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any constituency
returning a member to the Parliament af Northern Ireland if he or she is a British
subject, 21 or more years of age, not subject to any legal incapacity, and was born in
(18156)


---

## Page 14

Northern Ireland (or alternatively, has resided continuously in the United Kingdom for
a period of seven years ending on the qualifying date) and either:-
(a) has the requisite residence qualification of three months; or
(b) has occupied for three months prior to the qualifying date business premises
of at least £10 valuation; or
(c) is the wife or husband of a person entitled to be registered in respect of
the above business premises qualification; or
(d) was on the qualifying date a member of the Forces or a merchant seaman and
who, but for such service, would have been residing in Northern Ireland.
The electorate in the Queen*s University constituency comprises all graduates of
the University who are British subjects of full age, not subject to any legal
incapacity.
Under the provisions of the Representation of the People Act (l949), a person is
entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any Northern Ireland con-
stituency returning a member to the Parliament at Westminster if that person is a
British subject or citizen of the Irish Republic of twenty-one or more years of age
and not subject to any legal incapacity to vote, is resident at an address in that
constituency on the qualifying date, and has resided at that address (or at any other
address or addresses in Northern Ireland) for a period of three months ending on the
qualifying date.
In addition, a person who would ordinarily have been resident at a specified
address in a constituency in Northern Ireland, may be registered in respect of a
Service qualification if -
(i) he or she is a member of the Forces; or
(ii) he or she is a person employed in the service of the Crown in a post outside
the United Kingdom; or
(iii) she is the wife of a person having a Service qualification and is residing
outside the United Kingdom to be with her husband.
Education Areas; The Fermanagh County Council is the Education Authority for the
whole area under its control, and all figures given in this Volume for the County
apply equally to the respective Education Areas.
HOUSING • BUILDINGS, ROOMS, HOUSEHOLDS, ETC.
Houses; The total niomber of houses, including hotels, institutions, etc., in the
County normally used for dwelling purposes was 13,487 of which 12,641 houses were
occupied on Census night.
The number of occupied houses at the 1937 Census was
12,855, a decrease therefore of 214, or 1.7 per cent under this category took place in
the intercensal period.
Table III includes all houses, private and non-private.
The number of non-private houses was 114 of which 60 were boarding houses or hotels.
The population figures do not include inmates of institutions, but they do include
other occupants such as staff.

## Page 15

xii
The total number of houses was the lowest to be recorded.
The number of houses
being built ('205) was almost four times as great as the next highest census figure -
that of 52 for the 1937 census.
A similar trend regarding hpuses being built was
observed in respect of Counties covered by the earlier reports.
The average number of persons (exclusive of inmates of institutions) per inhabited

    house, steadily decreased from 1851 (when it stood at 5.64) and now stands at 4.18
being the lowest on record.
Table IV shows the changes in the numbers of houses in each County district during
the intercensal period 1937-1951.
In Enniskillen Municipal Borough there was an

    increase of 29.7 per cent between 1937 and 1951 but each of the rural areas showed a
decrease, viz., Enniskillen 2.4 per cent, Irvinestown 4.5<per cent and Lisnaskea 5.5
per cent.

The figures given relate to each County District as constituted at the date of the 1951 Census.
For the County as a whole there was a decrease of 196 houses (or 1.4 per cent)

    compared with a decrease of 317 houses (or 2.3 per cent) during the 1926-1937
interval.
The number of uninhabited houses was 776 in 1937, which i s the equivalent
of 5.7 per cent of a l l completed houses, and for 1951 a drop to 641, or 4.8 per cent
of a l l completed houses, i s shown and t h i s i s the lowest r a t i o recorded since 1881.
96 per cent of the houses unoccupied on census night in 1951 were in rural areas.
INHABITED PRIVATE HOUSES
Number of Houses and I n h a b i t a n t s :

The decrease in the number of inhabited private houses for the period 1926-1937 was

    13 and for the period 1937-1951 i t was 176, but t h i s must be viewed in the l i g h t of
variations in the population in these dwelling houses - there was a greater decrease
of population (3,107 persons) in the f i r s t period than in the l a t t e r (1,394 persons).
The r e l a t i v e changes are seen more clearly from the figures for "person per house" the

    average having dropped for the period 1926-1937 by 0.24 and for 1937-1951 by 0.05.
    Of the t o t a l of 12,527 inhabited private houses, 12,363 (98.7 per cent) were
occupied by single private households, the remainder of 164 were shared by two or more
households.
This means that 96.9 per cent of the t o t a l private households (12,757)
each occupies a single house.

The number of houses with three or less rooms fell between the 1926-1937 and
1937-1951 periods.
The number of houses with four or five rooms has increased over
the whole period, "while the number of houses with six or more rooms has also increased,
though at a slower rate.
The percentage of shared accommodation fell between 1926 and 1937 in the larger
type of house, but remained the same as regards houses of one to three rooms.
Between
1937 and 1951 it increased slightly in the 1-3 room group, decreased in the 4-5 room
group and remained the same as regards houses with 6 or more rooms.
Rooms: From Table 6 it will be observed that the total number of rooms in

    inhabited private houses was 57,753 (the corresponding figure for 1937 was 56,625).
    The average number of rooms per house was 4.61 compared with 4.46 in 1937.
The following table gives for the County the number of private households occupy-
ing various numbers of rooms (irrespective of the number of houses) for the enumera-

    tions of 1926, 1937 and 1951:-

PRIVATE HOUSEHOLDS
Households:
The population of 51,715 occupying private houses was comprised of
12,757 households within the definitions of "Private Households" given in Part I.
In
1937 there were 12,938 such households.
The decrease of 181 households (1.4 per cent)
was at a lower rate than the rate of decrease in population comprising them (population
decrease was 1,394 - 2 . 6 per cent).
The household factor rather than that of popula-
tion affects the question of housing.
In the period 1926 to 1937 the percentage
decrease in households was 0.4, whereas the population comprising them decreased by
5.5 per cent.
Households of all sizes decreased in number during the period 1937-1951
with the exception of households of one, two, eleven, twelve and fifteen and over persons.
tiai&e)


---

## Page 17

Table 7, in which the measurement of households according to the number of persons
in each household is detailed, shows that the number of households rises from 1,350
households of one person each, to 2,410 households of the two-person group;
from
thence there is a slight decrease to 2,331 in the three-person group from which the
decline is more marked as each group is reached.
The two-person group comprises 18.9
per cent of all households, the three-person group 18.3 per cent and the four-person
group 15.8 per cent - these three categories make up over 5C per cent of the total
number of private households.

    The comparable figures for 1937 were 18.0 for the two-person, 18.3 for three-
person households and 16.2 for the four-person group, making over 50 per cent in all as
in the later period.
The average size of household was 4.1 persons in 1951 and the same in 1937.
DENSITIES
Table 7 gives the composition of private households, the number of persons com-
prising each household, the number of rooms occupied, and the density of occupation.
Private Households: There were 54 households living at densities of more than
four persons per room and 214 households at more than three persons per room. 922
households or 7.2 per cent fall within the over two person per room category which has
been assumed to indicate overcrowding.
As shown earlier in these notes the household of two occurs most frequently; and
the most common combination consists of the household of two occupying three rooms.
Of the 4,554 households in the 1-3 room group, 828 comprise only one person each.
There are 199 households (344 persons) living in one roomed accommodation, but of these

    134 are persons living alone - in 1937 there were 246 households (459 persons) living
in one-roomed accommodation.
The number of households of two or more persons occupy-

    ing one room decreased from 131 in 1926 to 92 in 1937, and to 65 in 1951.
The particulars of households in two and three-roomed accommodation are:-

Of the 12,527 inhabited private houses 164 or 1.3 per cent were found to be
occupied by two or more households - 125 of these accommodated two households and 39
accommodated three or more households.

    Persons; Of the total of 51,715 persons in private houses 85.7 per cent (44,350
persons) were living not more than two persons per room; in 1937 the percentage was
84.7, so that a slight mitigation of general overcrowding is indicated.
Table 7 shows that, taking the total number of persons in private households and
the total number of rooms occupied, there is an average of 0.90 persons for each room.

    In 1926 the corresponding figure was 1.00 and that for 1937 was 0.94, so that there
has been an improvement in room accommodation during both intercensal periods, though
the rate of improvement was higher in the first period.

    Table VIII gives the densities of room occupation for 1926, 1937 and 1951
Censuses for the County.
It will be seen that the proportion of persons living at a density of over two

    persons per room decreased between 1926 and 1937 and further decreased between 1937
and 1951, although the proportion of persons living at densities of over three and over
four persons per room increased during the later intercensal period.
(18156)

It will be noted that in the County as a whole 143 persons per 1,000 live at a

    density of over two persons per room (in 1937 the figure was 153 and in 1926 it was
188 persons per 1,000 of the population).
An increase in the number of persons living
at this density occurred in Lisnaskea Rural District in the 1937-1951 period;
decreases - some substantial - took place in all other districts.
Between 1926 and
1937 all areas recorded a decrease.
The greatest overcrowding is in the rural
district of Irvinestown for which the figure is 169 persons per 1,000.
Enniskillen
Municipal Borough shows least overcrowding (86 persons per 1,000).

* The 1937 figures relate to a Poor Law Institution.
In the 1937 Report attention was dravm to the drop in the number of hotels and
boarding houses, and their occupants, since the 1926 Census.
Since 1937 there has

    been a drop in every area and the total now stands at 60, as compared with 94 in 1937.
Enniskillen Municipal Borough contains the highest number of this class of building
and accounts for 57 per cent of the total.

    Between 1937 and 1951 the number of inmates in institutions decreased by 22, and
the total population (i.e., inmates and staff) of these institutions by 9.
USUAL RESIDENCE
Table 10 discloses that 98.2 per cent of the*enumerated population of the County
were normally resident in the County and of the remainder, 593 were usually resident in
some other part of Northern Ireland.
Allowing for 644 persons usually resident in the
County but enumerated elsewhere in Northern Ireland the adjustment to obtain the usual
residence figure represented 1.0 per 1,000 of the population.
In 1937, when the
^TJsual Residence" question was first included in the Census Return, the adjustment
figure was 0.9 per 1,000 of the total enumeration.
Only 353 persons were usually resident outside the Province.
SEX, AGE AND MARITAL CONDITION
Table 11 shows the population of the County according to sex, age (individual
years and quinquennial groups) and marital condition.
Table XI although restricted
to certain age groups, provides a convenient basis for comparison of the 1937 position
with that of 1951.

Sex Proportions;
Since 1821, when the first Census affecr^ing Northern Ireland
was taken, each enumeration up to and including that of 1881 showed that the number of
females in the County exceeded the number of males.
The greatest disparity was in

    1821 when there were 1,059 females for every 1,000 males*
At each census from 1891
to 1951 (inclusive) males were in the majority.
The number of females per 1,000 males

    decreased steadily from 986 in 1891 to 891 in 1937, but in 1951 there was an increase
to 912 females per 1,000 males.
Males were in the majority in 1951 in all
quinquennial age groups, except 80-89, in which females were nomerically superior, and

    in the age groups 90-94, and 100 and over for which the figures were identical.
It will be noted from Table 12 that in the urban area there is a preponderance of
females over males and in all the rural districts, males outnumber females.
^£es_:
The average age of the population of the County decreased between 1957 and
1951.
At the 1911 Census the average was 32.5 years;
i t fell to 32.0 in 1926 and

    rose to 34.6 in 1937, falling to 33.7 in 1951.
    In 1937 the 10-14 age group accounted for the largest number of the population of
all quinquennial age groups containing 9.2 per cent of the enumerated population, as
compared with 8.2 in 1951.
In 1951 the age group from birth to four years exceeded
all others with 10.5 per cent of the population as compared with 8.4 in 1937.
Since

    1937 decreases are shown in the age groups between 10 and 29 and between 55 and 69.
Increases were recorded for males in the quinquennial age groups comprising 0-9 years,

    30-54, 70-84 and 90-99 and for females in the age groups comprising 0-9 years, 35-44
and 70 and over.
(18156)


---

## Page 21

XVI11
The following chart shows the distribution of the population for the County in

    the four main age groups for 1901, 1937 and 1951.
    CENSUSES (COUNTY OF FERMANAGH) 1901. 1987, 1951.

    I t will be seen that between 1901 and 1937 the age group 0-14 decreased by 1.8 per
cent but between 1937 and 1951 an increase of 1.9 per cent was recorded.
The 15-44
group showed a decrease of 3. 1 per cent in the former period and in the l a t t e r a
decrease of 2. 2 per cent.
The gain of 0.9 per cent in the 45-64 category shown in

    1937 was p a r t l y offset by the drop to 19.6 in 1951.
The 65 years and over group rose

    from 7.9 in 1901 to 11.9 in 1937 and to 12.7 in 1951.
The proportion of the popula-

    tion aged 45 years and upwards i s shown to have increased between 1937 and 1951 by
0. 3 per cent.
Marital Condition:
Since 1937 the number of persons in the County aged 14 and

    over decreased by 2,348 of whom 1,597 were males and 751 females.
The number of

    single persons in t h i s age group decreased by 3,090 (1,869 males and 1,221 females),
    while the number of married persons increased by 827 (349 males and 478 females).
Single males aged 14 and over accounted for 54. 2 per cent of the male population

    of the same age in 1951 (58.7 per cent in 1937) and married men for 40. 6 per cent (36.1
in 1937).
In the same age group, single females represented 41.4 per cent (46.1 in

    1937) and married women 45.9 per cent (41.6 in 1937).
For both sexes the only quinquennial groups of married persons to show a decrease

    during the 1937-1951 intercensal period were males 20-24, 55-69 and 85-89 and females
    15-19, 45-49, 55-69 and 85-89.
Vi/idowers decreased by 85 and widows by 22.
The number of persons who marriages
had been dissolved and who had not remarried was 22 in 1951 - divorced men numbered 8
and divorced women 14.
No persons were returned as divorced in 1937.
(18156)


---

## Page 22

EDUCATION
Table 13 summarises the information furnished regarding attendance at educational
establishments.
It will be seen that for the County of children aged 5-13 inclusive
7.4 per cent are stated not to be attending school.
In the 14-17 age groups, i . e . ,
amongst persons over the age for compulsory school attendance but of normal age for
attending grammar school, 66.4 per cent were not attending educational establishmentSo
Persons of all ages attending educational establishments part-time were shown to
number 20.
As the total number of part-time students enrolled in the institutions of
further education under the management of the County Fermanagh Education Authority

    during the year ended 31st July, 1951, numbered 740, it is apparent that in many cases
the information regarding part-time education was not furnished on the census returns.
Much the same result was disclosed by the earlier census reports.
It would appear
that the significance of the question V\^as not appreciated by the heads of households.
BIRTHPLACES
Table 14 shows, by sex, the number of persons born in each of the counties of
Northern Ireland, other parts of the British Isles, Commonwealth Countries, etc.,
Foreign Countries and at sea.
Of the total population of the County (i.e., 53,044)

    persons born in the County numbered 42,412 (80.0 per. cent), and altogether 46,133
(86.97 per cent) were born in some part of Northern Irelando

    5,328 persons (10.04 per
    cent) were born in the Irish Republic as compared with 5,502 (10.08 per cent) in 1937.
Only 267 persons (0.5 per cent) were born outside the British Isles, of whom 101 were
born in Commonwealth Countries, 162 in Foreign Countries, and 4 at sea.
The percentage representing persons born in the County (80.0) has decreased since
1937 (81.4); the percentage of persons born in the adjoining County of Tyrone
increased from 3. 2 to 3. 5.

## Page 23

It will be noted that except for a slight increase between lff91 and 1901, the per-
centage of the total population born in Northern Ireland decreased steadily between

    1861 and 1926, viz., from 93.83 to 87.19.
Between 1926 and 1937 there was an increase

    to 87.56 but this fell to 86.97 in 1951 which is the lowest recorded.
Over the same

    period 1861 to 1926 the percentage born in the Irish Republic steadily increased (5.54
    to 10.18); the 1926 figure being the highest recorded.
Between 1926 and 1937 there

    was a decrease to 10.08 and a further decrease to 10.04 in 1951.
The percentage born
in England and Wales stands at 1.55 the highest figure reached during the ninety years

    1861-1951; that born in Scotland stood at its lowest point in 1861 (0.19 per cent) and
    at its highest point in 1926 (l.04 per cent) from which it decreased in 1937 to 0.96
per cent and 0.93 per cent in 1951.
Table 15 shows the numbers of persons in each administrative area born outside
Northern Ireland, according to sex, duration of residence, whether resident in, or
visitors to Northern Ireland and divided into five religious groups.
Table XIII gives for the County for purposes of comparison, the total number of
persons born outside Northern Ireland by duration of residence, for Censuses 1937 and

## Page 24

A scrutiny of Table XIV reveals that the percentage of Roman Catholics decreased

    from 56.5 in 1861 to 55.3 in 1901.
    The figure in 1911 was 56.2, falling to 56.0 in
    1926 and to 55.3 in 1937.
An increase to 55.6 was recorded in 1951.
Presbyterians
stood at 1.8 of the total population in 1861 and did not exceed 2.0 until 1926, when
the figure was 2.5.
There was an increase to 3.0 in 1937 and the figure in 1951 (3.4)
is the highest recorded.
The Church of Ireland decreased by 0.6 per cent between

    1937 and 1951 (from 33.4 to 32.8) and at the latter year stood at i t s lowest point.
Methodists remained at 6.4, the same figure as in 1937.
"Other and Not Stated
Denominations" show a percentage of 1.8 which is a decrease of 0.1 on the 1937 figure.
A comparison of the 1951 figures with those for 1937 shows that there were numerical
decreases in four groups, viz., Roman Catholics 735, Church of Ireland, 841,

    Methodists 87, others 45, while the Presbyterian group increased by 183.
The total
of 17,411 for the Church of Ireland is the lowest figure for that denomination.
Table 16 shows the population by sex for each County District under the five
religious groups, viz., Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church of Ireland (including the
Church of England and the Episcopal Church of Scotland), Methodist and all other (or
not stated) denominations.
The l i s t of descriptions of "Other Denominations" given
at the foot of the table refers to the County as a whole;
the names of denominations
are restricted to those having not less than ten adherents.
Of the "Other Denominations" Brethren showed a decrease of 255 since 1937 and

    members of the Church of Christ, not mentioned in 1937, numbered 79 in 1951.
The
number of persons who omitted to state their denomination has increased from 47 to 179>
Table 17 gives details for the County under five religious categories and age
groups (individual years to age 21, and quinquennial age groups up to 69 years).
The
comparative Table in the 1937 Report gave the population by individual ages up to 20,
and by two religious groups - Roman Catholics and other denominations.
I t is not
therefore possible to make a complete comparison between the 1937 figures and those
published in this Report, but i t is observed that the popiilation of Roman Catholics up
to the age of 20 increased by 2, while the population of all other denominations up to
that age decreased by 380.
The following are the percentage increases or decreases
in the various age groups up to 19 years and of the population aged 20 and over
between 1937 and 1951:-
Age Group
Roman Catholics
Other Denominations

15 - 19
20 and over
All Ages

    A comparison of Tables 15 and 16 shows that in the County 11.5 per cent of Roman
Catholics, 23.9 per cent of Presbyterians, 14.7 per cent of members of the Church of
Ireland, 11.2 per qent of Methodists, and 14.7 per cent of other and not stated
denominations were born outside Northern Ireland.
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
I t will be seen from the volumes "Classification of Occupations" and "Classifica-
tion of Industries" referred to in Part I of this Report that the separate terms under
which workers are classified run into many thousands.
This has occasioned modifica-
tions to bring census data within manageable dimensions, and thus generally speaking
the arrangement of headings is according to class of materials worked with, commodities
produced or services rendered.
Special attention is drawn to the following changes since the 1926 Census which
is the last occasion on which occupational and industrial enquiries were included in
the Northern Ireland Census Schedule:-
(l)

    The 1951 Tables deal with persons aged 14 years and over, whereas in 1926 the
age of 12 was adopted as the minimum.
For Table XV the 1926 figures were
adjusted to the 1951 age grouping to allow proper comparison but see footnote to
that table.
See footnote to Table XVI also.

## Page 25

(2) A revision in the Classification of Occupations since 1926 brings together
the bulk of the unskilled workers in a single group - Order XXVI.
Occupations : Table XV sets out the numbers and percentages of persons occupied
(i.e., persons whose normal occupation was stated) in the age group from 14 years and
upwards, for the Censuses 1926 and 1951.
The term "occupied" includes persons temporarily out of worko
The number

    returned as temporarily out of work in 1926 for the County was 802 (613 males and 189
females).

    In 1951, the figure amounted to 1,380 (1,124 males and 256 females).

The above table shows that for both sexes the percentage of occupied to total

    number of persons aged 14 years and over i s lower for 1951 than for 1926.
In 1926,
however, males aged 65 years and over comprised 14.9 per cent of the male population

    aged 14 years and upwards, whereas in 1951 the percentage was 16.9 and of females aged
    14 years and upwards the percentage aged 65 and over increased from 16. 1 in 1926 to
17.5 in 1951.
I t i s probable also that the percentage of occupied persons - both
male and female - of the age group 65 and over decreased considerably between the
Censuses.

    The total number of occupied persons has decreased from 24,854 in 1926 to 20,968
    in 1951 - a decrease of 3,886 persons (2,729 males and 1,157
females).

    In 1926 there were 23 occupied females to every 100 occupied males, while in 1951
there were 2D occupied females to every 100 occupied males.

## Page 27

in 1911 as well as in 1926.
For males the percentage increase was 0.5 between 1911
and 1926 and for females 0.3, but for the subsequent period a decrease of 2.0 per cent

Table 18 gives the full list of occupations of persons of 14 years of age and
over and Table XVII (see previous page) shows the proportions per 1,000 of the occupied
males and females under the numerically important Orders and also for numerically
important single or grouped occupations.
61.1 per cent of the occupied males are engaged in Agricultural Occupations;
5.9 per cent are engaged as workers in Building and Contracting, etc., 5.8 per cent in
Commercial, Finance and Insurance Occupations (excluding Clerical); and 5.3 per cent
in Transport and Communications.
34.0 per cent of occupied females are engaged in
Personal Service; 16.2 per cent in Agricultural Occupations; 14.1 per cent are in
Professional and Technical Occupations (excluding Clerical) and 12.1 per cent in
Commercial, Finance and Insurance (excluding Clerical).
The changes since 1926 in
the Classification of Occupations and especially the introduction of Order XXVI bring-
ing together groups of unskilled workers not provided for elsewhere (formerly included
in various Orders) make it impracticable to give a general comparison between 1926 and
1951 figures.
It is, however, possible to give comparative figures in some instances.
The following are those which show the greatest variation in numbers and which are
broadly similar to the 1926 classification:-

Agriculture and Fishing provide employment for 54 per cent of the occupied
population.
In 1926 the percentage was 68.
The Manufacturing Industries (used in a
wide sense) provide 15 per cent and the Distributive Trades and Finance (including
Insurance and Banking) 10 per cent; in 1926 the percentages were 8 and 7 respectively.
Table XVIII also shows that the males outnumbered the females in all groups except
the Professions and Miscellaneous Services.
The former group includes Teachers and
Nurses and the latter, persons employed in Private Dom.estic Service, iRestaurants,
Entertainment, etc.
The same balance was recorded in respect of earlier Reports for
1951.
For males Agriculture and Fishing provided employment for 62 per cent of the
occupied, and the Manufacturing Industries (mainly Textiles, Building and Contracting

    and Manufacture of Food) 15 per cent; in 1926 the percentage for the former was 75
and 8 for the latter.
The Miscellaneous Services (including Laundries, Domestic Service, etc.) gave
employment to 30 per cent of the occupied females and the Professions to 18 per cent;
in 1926 the percentages were 37 and 7 respectively.
Table 21 gives the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, separate figures being given for each Order and suitable groupings thereunder.
(18156)

## Page 110

GOVERNMENT OF NORTHERN IRELAND
CENSUS OF POPULATION
OF NORTHERN IRELAND
1951
General Report

    Printed & presented pursuant to 14 & 15 Geo. 6, Ch. 6
BELFAST : HER MAJESTY'S STATIONERY OFFICE

## Page 111

PREFACE
Although three censuses of population have been taken since the Government of
Northern Ireland was established the enumeration as at midnight of 8th-9th April, 1951,
which forms the basis of this report, was the first of these to be carried out at the
same time as censuses were taken in the other parts of the United Kingdom.
The various
reports will, therefore, when read in conjunction with one another, provide a picutre of
the United Kingdom as a whole.
The enumeration of 1926 incorporated questions relating to occupation and industry,
orphanhood and infirmities, but that of 1937 was of more limited scope and was intended
to bridge the gap between the census of 1926 and the census which it was proposed to
take in 1941, but which had to be abandoned owing to the outbreak of war.
In the 1951
census questions were asked about the occupations and industries of the population, but
information about orphanhood and infirmities was not sought.
This report has been prepared (as were also the seven county volumes, including a
volume relating to Belfast County Borough, and the Preliminary Reports already
published) in accordance with the provisions of the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1951.
The whole of Northern Ireland is covered by this issue and it brings together, in
some respects in greater detail, much of the information contained in the county
volumes.
Additional features include the distribution of the people occupationally and
industrially by age and marital condition and a set of tables relating to Belfast and
its environs.
Following the 1926 census a topographical index was published dealing with each
type of area for which statistics were supplied in the county series.
A supplement to
this index was published after the 1937 census and a further supplement in respect of
the 1951 census will be prepared in due course...
Census publications are dbtainable either directly from H.M. Stationery Office,
Chichester House, Chichester Street, Belfast, or from any book-seller.
It is desired to acknowledge the valuable assistance given by the various Northern
Ireland Ministries and by some Imperial Departments.
Particular mention must be made
of the work of the Royal Ulster Constabulary which provided the corps of enumerators.
The enumeration was smoothly carried out and it is evident that the tasks of distribu-
ting schedules, collecting completed returns, and recording the required information
were performed with creditable zeal.
The Valuation and Surveys Division of the Ministry of Finance supplied area and
valuation figures for inclusion in the reports and prepared the charts which appeared
in the various publications.
The special map of Belfast and Environs which appears in
this volume was also prepared by the Ordnance Survey Branch.
H.M. Stationery Office discharged efficiently the duty of providing, within the
limited time available, the multiplicity of schedules, forms and other documents
required for the enumeration and arranged for the publication of the various reports.
The Press, B.B.>C., directors of industrial concerns, transport executives, etc.,
made valuable contributions within their own spheres.
There was a gratifying response
from the public generally to furnish complete and accurate information.
The work of census taking and the production of reports could not have been
effectively carried out without the co-operation of everyone whose aid was solicited
and in all cases readily given.
L. C. MULLIGAN,
REGISTRAR-GENERAL
General Register Office,
Fermanagh House,
Belfast.
December, 1954.
(82287)


---

## Page 116

PART T
INTRODUCTION
1.
GENERAL HISTORICAL SURVEY
The first complete census of Ireland was taken in 1821, from which year enumera-
tions were taken decennially until 1911.
The census taken on 8-9 April, 1951, in
Northern Ireland was the third since the establishment of the Government of Northern
Ireland, the two earlier counts having been made in 1926 (the first since 1911) and
1937.
Censuses were taken also on the same date in 1951 in Great Britain, Isle of Man,
Channel Islands and the Irish Republic: the last occasion on which simultaneous
enumerations took place was in 1911.
The 1937 census was restricted in scope as it was intended to launch a complete
enumeration in 1941 in conjunction with the rest of the United Kingdomx.
The war,
however, made a census in that year impracticable.
Accordingly, it was desirable
that the 1951 census should be as wide in scope as was reasonably possible.
There
is a growing demand for statistics in almost every field of science and although
information is collected from many sources and through divers channels the census
remains an indispensable instrument for acquiring knowledge about the nation; it
enables a great variety of facts' to be collected at one point in time about e\Aery
individual in the country and related to the aggregate of persons in any particular
group.
2.
PREPARATIONS FOR THE CENSUS
(a) The Questions.» For the 1951 census as on former occasions the number and
variety of suggestions for questions to be asked in the census return far exceeded
the limits which were considered practicable.
It was, however, agreed that in
general the programme adopted in Great Britain should be accepted here. Consequently,
questions as to age, marital condition, relation to head of household, birthplace,
nationality, number of rooms occupied by household, occupation, industry, and usual
residence were in similar terms throughout the United Kingdom.
The principal
omissions in Northern Ireland were in respect of household arrangements for water
supply, etc.; date of marriage of women under 50 years of age; fertility; age at
which full time education ceased and employer's address.
On the other hand in the
Northern Ireland return, enquiries about religion, and length of residence if born
outside Northern Ireland, were incorporated.
In 1926 enquiries were made about infirmities and representations were made for
a similar course to be taken in 1951 but it was decided to accept the view widely
held by research bodies that it is not possible to obtain accurate and uniform
information about physical or mental infirmities through the medium of the census.
With the facilities now available through Welfare, Hospital and other Authorities
data of a satisfactory standard of reliability could be collected by officers
trained and experienced in the" special branches of medical activity.
The question on education was restricted to persons attend^ing an educational
establishment but it was made clear by the discrepancy between the figures compiled
from the returns and those furnished by the Ministry of Education that the simple
request to state whether full-time or part-time education was being received was
misunderstood in many cases.
Enumerators were required during the issue of returns to note the number of
private households occupying structurally separate dwellings, that is, houses or
other accommodation adapted for separate occupancy and the number of rooms in each
dwelling; particulars of shared dwellings and shared living rooms were noted at the
same time.
The question of religion is customary for censuses taken throughout Ireland and
in 1951, as previously, persons were not legally obliged to furnish this information.
(82287)


---

## Page 117

viii
(b) Legislation.* Under the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1951, the head of
every household, establishment, vessel, etc., was personally responsible for the
completion of the return delivered to him by the enumerator, who, when collecting the
completed forms, was required to make good any defects in accordance with the instruc-
tions set out in the returns, and to assi'st heads of households on points of difficulty,
A copy of the householder's return appears as an appendix to this Report.
As heretofore, the census was taken on a de facto
and not a de jure
basis, i.e.,
it included all persons regardless of nationality or usual place of residence, who were
present in Northern Ireland at midnight of 8th April or who arrived in Northern Ireland
during the following morning, provided they had not been enumerated elsewhere.
Northern Ireland residents who spent the night elsewhere were excluded.
(c) Organisation for Enumeration.- As in the past, members of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary acted as enumerators.
Each District Inspector was responsible for the
enumeration of his own area and as a preliminary step he was required to divide his
area into enumeration districts of a suitable size having due regard to local admini-
strative areas.
A comparison with records maintained in the Census Office and the
mapping of enumeration districts provided a check that the entire country had been
covered.
A total of 1,061 enumerators were engaged in the 33 police districts
contained in Northern Ireland.
(d) The Returns.- As at previous censuses, the greater part of the enumeration
was concerned with the private household, which may be defined as one or more persons
occupying a house, flat, apartment, etc.
The duty of completing the return rested
with the head of the household.
In non-private households this responsibility was
placed on the manager, proprietor, chief resident officer, governor of prison, etc.,
while in Defence Establishments (including naval vessels) all persons present on
census night were enumerated by Commanding Officers, under special arrangements .made
with the Admiralty, Army Council and Air Council.
Provision was made for individuals, who did ilot wish to give details of them-
selves to heads of households, to complete a separate confidential return which was
either handed to the enumerator or left for him in a sealed envelope.
The
particulars given on these confidential returns were subsequently added by the
enumerators to the returns in which they normally would have been included.
3.
PUBLICITY
An essential feature of a successful enumeration is the ready co-operation of
the public.
The standard attained in the tabulations cannot rise above the standard
of the answers set out in the returns and this in turn depends on the extent to which
people have been informed of the benefits accruing from a census of population.
For
this purpose the advantage of a nation-wide count is obvious.
Not only was the
census given wide publicity in the national and local press but there was also a flow
of articles in weekly and other periodicals.
The British Broadcasting Corporation also made a substantial contribution
through the various stations in the United Kingdom from which special talks were
given to schools, etc.
Local publicity included a booklet entitled "The Coming Census"- and display
posters were prepared and distributed through Public Libraries, Chambers of Commerce,
Young Farmers^ Clubs, Clergymen of various denominations, school teachers, heads of
large industrial concerns, etc.
The posters were displayed at Police Barracks, Post
Offices and Transport Depots.
4.
THE ENUMERATION
The returns were delivered during the week commencing 2nd April and collected
not later than 21st April.
People move frequently from place to place and every
contingency had to be foreseen so that a complete record of the population in each
area could be obtained; special provision had to be made for itinerants, persons
living in portable dwellings, etc.
The enumerator had also to make on his rounds
a note of all buildings and the number of rooms occupied by each household.
Efeich
return had to be examined to see whether it was properly filled up, and to have any
necessary amendment made.
The enumerator prepared population summaries and
forwarded them with returns, and other census material to the superintendent not
later than 28th April. Before forwarding the documents to the Census Office each
superintendent had to satisfy himself that the instructions had been properly carried out.
(82287)


---

## Page 118

It was from the sixmmaries that preliminary figures of population of the counties
and county boroughs were prepared and announced in Parliament on 23rd May, 1951.
5.
PRELIMINARY REPORTS
In Great Britain, pending the preparation of full-scale reports, it was decided
to produce an advance conspectus of the main features of the information gained from
the census and in due course a report giving one per cent sample tables was published.
Consideration was given to the question of publishing similar tables for Northern
Ireland, but, when regard was had to the size and distribution of the population of
Northern Ireland, it was decided that the work involved in the preparation of the
material would not be justified.
It was considered that the better course was to
give as much information as could be made available in a reasonable time in the form of
Preliminary Reports.
Consequently in August, 1951, an Advance Statement of Population
was presented to Parliament; this gave the 1951 figures by administrative areas and
compared them with the 1937 figures.
A table for Belfast City and Environs was also
given.
A Preliminary Report followed in October, 1951.
This volume included a table
on religions for counties and county boroughs.
This information had to be extracted
by hand direct from the returns and caused some delay in the publication of the report.
It became apparent that there was an urgent demand in connection with medical and
sociological investigations for reliable and up-to-date figures of ages of the
population and in September, 1952, a Second Preliminary Report was published.
This
involved the punching of a special series of cards for the whole country followed by
the usual machine processes of sorting and counting.
The result of these two exceptional operations was to delay the programme for the
main reports, but it was considered that the usefulness of the information given in
advance of the main publications made the operations worth while.
6.
TABULATIONS
(a) Scope and Design.« The work of compiling statistics for the reports was
carried out from the individual returns.
The return, excluding those that applied
to the household taken as a whole, contained 14 questions as against 9 in 1937.
The
replies to each particular question had to be variously classified by themselves and
the classifications of each reply had often to be associated with replies to other
questions involving a mass of different groupings.
Taking age as an example replies
had to be dealt with in single years, involving over 100 groups for each sex; again
each age was sub-classified according to marital status, and yet again according to
hundreds of occupations and industries.
Likewise information on housing and
accommodation was analysed by areas as small as townlands in the rural and wards in
the urban areas.
The whole field of possible tabulations is enormous but for general use the
demand for statistics seems to have been met by the tables given in the county
volumes.
Generally therefore the General Report follows the lines of the county
series and presents a complete picture of Northern Ireland.
Several additional
features have, however, been included, e.g., (a) occupations and industries by age
and marital condition, (b) married couples enumerated together by quinquennial age
groups of husbands and wives, and (c) a series of tables for Belfast and Environs.
(b) Methods.- The only satisfactory way of compiling the bulk of census
statistics is by machine tabulation.
A contract was therefore placed with the
British Tabulating Machine Co., Ltd.., for the hire of machines which included
punches, counters and sorter - counters of the ^Hollerith' type.
Certain tabulation work does not lend itself to machine operation - notably
that relating to housing - and here a Hicking* process was used after the
enumeration books had been compared with the returns completed by heads of
households.
(c) Coding particulars on Returns.- This operation consisted of translating
the information in the returns into a series of alphabetical and numerical codes. In
certain cases it was found more convenient to code in groups such as "Usual
Residence", "Birthplace"-, and "Nationality"^
The coding of occupations, industrial
status and industries was at first performed at one operation, but it was found that
it was more rapid and accurate to code the first two headings together and to code
industries separately.
All coding was 100 per cent, checked.
(82S87)


---

## Page 119

(d) Card Punching. - After the returns had been coded they were passed to the
card punching staff who transferred the information to cards, one card being used for
each person.
The position of the holes punched in the cards indicated the appropriate
codings for the particulars of the individual given in the return.
The person's name
did not come into the operation at any point and the details about one individual were
simply used in conjunction with details from other cards to build up totals for the
various tables.
An illustration of the card used is shown below.

The actual card measured 9.3 c/m by 8.2 c/m.
(®) Machines.> Two types of punching machines -were used: the gang punch was
used for punching batches of cards appropriate to columns 1-6 on the cards where the
information was common to many cards, and hand key punches were used for the remaining
columns 12-38.
It is through the holes punched in these cards that the electrical
brushes of the sorting and counting machines make contact.
Eight key punches were used and, to provide a 100 per cent, check, six mechanical
verifiers with electrical sensing were required.
The cards having been punched and
verified were passed to the staff operating the sorting and counting machines.
One sorting machine with counter attached was used; this was capable of sorting
into 12 groups and simultaneously counting on any one pre-arranged column.
This
machine was adapted to feed automatically and sort up to 24,000 cards per hour.
Two sorting machines with a three-bank counter (i.e., a machine which counts on
three columns of the card at one time) were also in service, although one three-bank
counter was only in use as such for a short period.
These machines too were capable
of dealing with up to 24,000 cards per hour.
To avoid unnecessary operations, a programme was devised reducing the number of
"card passages"' to a minimum.
This programme worked satisfactorily for the tables
of the county volumes, but, in this report the more comprehensive tables for
occupations and industries by age and marital condition necessitated the breaking
down of the cards into very small groups, with the result that a good deal of hand
sorting or needling in conjunction with the machines was found to be quicker.
Not
only were several hundred groups of occupations involved, but each had to be broken'
into three groups of marital condition and each sub-group further divided into
fourteen age groups.
For industries similar processes were required but with a
different grand total of cards as in this tabulation persons unemployed were excluded.
Altogether about 1,500,000 cards were used; for the County and General Reports
one card was required for each person enumerated and for the Ages Preliminary Report
one card was normally sufficient for about 12 persons as the information punched on
it was restricted.
Altogether there were about 32,000,000 passages through the
machines.
(82E87)


---

## Page 120

7.
THE MAIN REPORTS
The first of the county reports to be published was that for Belfast County Borough
in October, 1953, while the final county volume (County Fermanagh) issued in September,
1954.
A topographical index was published after the 1926 census and a supplement followed
the 1937 reports.
It is proposed to bring these indices up to date by means of a
further supplement.
8.
COST OF THE CENSUS
The total expenditure on the 1951 census under the Census Vote up to 30th September,

    1954, amounted to £46,989 to which must be added further expenditure, estimated at £2,850
in respect of salaries, printing, etc., to complete the work, making a total of £49,839
which is equivalent to a cost of 8.7d. per head of the population enumerated.
Details
of the total expenditure are shown in the following statement:-

It will be observed that the above figures for 1926 and 1937 do not include
expenditure for rent, fuel, etc., nor cost of printing and stationery for 1937.
It will
also be seen that in 1926 machine operators' wages, etc., are included in cost of machine
work.
The higher cost of the 1951 census as compared with the previous enumerations is
attributable to - (a) general increase in salary and wage levels; (b) increase in
enumerators' expenses; (c) tabulation by hand of information as to religion for the
First Preliminary Report; (d) the production of a Special Ages Report; (e) the compre-
hensive tables on occupations and industries provided in this Report at the request of
Government Departments; and (f) in regard to 1937 that information was not sought at that
enumeration as to occupations and industries.
;82887)
