## Page 121

PART II
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME
AREA
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey (foreshore being
excluded), and relate to the several administrative areas as constituted at the date of
the 1951 census.
The acreages covered by the tidal water, larger rivers and lakes are
not included in the body of Tables 4 and 29 (see footnotes thereto).
POPULATION
The population comprises all persons on land, in vessels on inland waters or in
port or at anchorage on census night, and also persons on coastwise voyages who arrived
in port within a brief period not having been enumerated elsewhere.
Members of the
Forces of the Crown are also included.
ADMINISTRATIVE DIVISIONS
Northern Ireland is comprised of six counties and two county boroughs.
The
counties are divided into 65 county districts (9 municipal boroughs, 24' urban districts
and 3S rural districts) comprising 703 district electoral divisions, 90 of which are
coterminous with entire urban districts or wards thereof.
The counties are also
divided into 129 county electoral divisions.
Owing to the abolition of the Boards of Guardians, details of Poor Law Unions and
Dispensary districts are not given in the 1951 census volumes.
These areas, however,
form respectively, superintendent registrars' and registrars' districts for the
registration of marriages, births and deaths*, and consequently the census tabulations
have been prepared in a manner suitable for the extraction of data according to these
areas should such be required.
Each Education Area is coterminous with its respective county or county borough.
The changes made in the constitution or boundaries of administrative divisions
during the last intercensal period (except as regards county and district electoral
divisions), are as follows:-
(a) The following Urban Districts were made Municipal Boroughs in the years shown

    in brackets:- Ballymena (1939), Carrickfergus (1949), Enniskillen (1949), Larne
    (1939), Lurgan (1949), Newtownards (l938), and Portadown (1947).
(b) xA. portion of Larne R. D. with a population of 1,613 in 1937 was transferred

    to Carrickfergus U.D. in 1946, and a portion with a population of 22 in 1937^ to
Larne M.B. in 1948.
(c) A portion of Armagh R. D. with a population of 1,605 in 1937 was transferred

    to Armagh U. D. in 1945 and 1946 and a portion uninhabited in 1937, to Keady U. D.
in 1948.
(d) A portion of Lurgan R. D. with a population of 606 in 1937 was transferred to

    Lurgan U. D. in 1938, and a portion with a population of 2,363 in 1937 to
Portadown U. D. in 1947.
(e) A portion of Newtownards R.D. with a population of 515 in 1937 was
transferred to Bangor M.B. in 1950, and a portion with a population of 739 in
1937 to Newtownards M.B. also in 1950.
(f) A portion of Castlereagh R.D. with a population of 404 in 1937 was
transferred to Holywood U.D. in 1946.
(82287)


---

## Page 122

(g) Kilkeel, formerly a town under the Towns Improvement (Ireland) Act, 1854, and
forming part of Kilkeel R.D., was made an Urban District in 1937 subsequent to the
Census of that year.
(h) A portion of Enniskillen R. D. with a population of 746 in 1937 was transferred
to Enniskillen U. D. in 1945 and 1947.
(k) The number of Wards of Londonderry C.B. was reduced from five to three in
1938.
(l) A portion of Cookstown R. D. with a population of 449 in 1937 was transferred
to Cookstown U. D, in 1947.
(m) A portion of Dungannon R.D. with a population of 1,023 in 1937 was transferred
to Dungannon U. D. in 1949.
(n) Various changes in Parliamentary Constituencies for elections to the United
Kingdom Parliament under the* provisions of the Representation of the People Act,
1948.
BELFAST AND ENVIRONS
Attention was drawn in the Preliminary Report to the very slight change which had
taken place in the population of Belfast County Borough since 1937 as compared with the
steady growth of the Borough since the earliest census was taken in 1821.
Reference
was also made to the phenomenal growth of the population of the rural areas adjoining
the City, which indicated that the development of what may be termed the Belfast
district had been fully maintained.
Time did not permit at the Preliminary Report stage to examine the position
closely, but Table 4' of that report gave figures for an approximate area around Belfast

    which showed that the population had increased by 36,-969 as compared with 5,585 within
the Borough itself.
It has since been possible to examine the matter in detail and a
line has been drawn round a more or less continuously built-up area, i.e., one that can
conveniently be thought of' as the * Brick-and-Mortar' area, though it -was necessary,
except in one instance, to adhere to townland boundaries as that was the smallest area
used at the census.
The population of the revised area increased by 35,744 between
1937 and 1951.
A separate section of this report has been allocated to Belfast and its Environs,
giving tables relating to housing, accommodation, ages and religion of the population.
A statement is included in the section showing in detail the composition of the area
and a sketch map is provided in addition.
HOUSES
1.
The term '^Hous£" means -
(a) each structurally separate dwelling (except institutions) having distinct
access to the street, common stair, or landing;
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanitorium, nursing home, asylum,
religious, welfare or charitable community, residential school, barracks or
other premises under Naval, Military, Air Force, or Police discipline, prison
or other place of detention), regardless of the number of buildings within its
precincts;
(c) each boat, caravan, tent or other movable dwelling if occupied on census
night but not otherwise.
2.
The term ^Trivate House" means a dwelling other than an institution, hotel,
boarding house, premises occupied by a caretaker, vessel, movable dwelling, barn,
stable or outhouse occupied on census night.
The term includes premises comprising
a shop with dwelling attached, unless occupied by a caretaker.
3.
The terms "Boarding House" and "Lodging House" cover habitations described as
such, and any private house containing three or more boarders or lodgers.
(82287)


---

## Page 123

4.
The term ^^Inhabited^^ denotes actual residence of a person or persons on census
night.
"Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those temporarily vacant through the
absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Enumerators were
instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in such an advanced state of
decay that there was little likelihood that they would again be made fit for habitation.
ROOMS
1.
The rooms counted are the usual living rooms including bedrooms and kitchens but
excluding sculleries (i.e., compartments used only for washing, cleaning, or cooking)
pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets, bathrooms, or any warehouse, office or shop
rooms.
2.
For the classification of households according to rooms occupied (see table 7),
shared rooms - i.e., those used in common by two or more households - have been
included in the number of rooms occupied by the main household in the dwelling.
In
cases of one room occupations, all persons living therein, whether related or not, have
been enumerated as one census household.
HOUSEHOLDS
1.
"Non»private Households" comprise all persons enumerated in hotels, boarding
houses, lodging houses, shops and institutions (see para. l(b) under "Houses"
definition above).
2.
"Private Households" are comprised of single persons or groups of persons
occupying separately the whole, or part of any private dwelling (see para. 2 under
"Houses" definition above).
Visitors and domestic servants are included in the house-
hold with which they were enumerated.
Lodgers having or sharing separate accommodation
were enumerated on separate returns and consequently are regarded as separate private
households.
It should be specially noted that a household or private household for census
purposes includes any group of persons, whether related or not, who live together in
separate house or room accommodation.
RATEABLE VALUATION
The figures shown are those for the year ended 31st March, 1952, and have been
supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Subject to the provisions of the Local Gk)vernment (Rating and Finance) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1929, agricultural land and agricultural buildings are completely
derated and industrial hereditaments and freight-transport hereditaments are partially
derated, the consequent deficiency in the yield of rates being made good by way of
General Exchequer contributions.
The figures in Tables 4 and 29 are the totals of the valuations in the various
areas before reduction on account of derating, but exclude in certain cases the
valuations of electricity, gas and water mains, telegraphs, telephones, fisheries,
water-works and tramways.
USUAL RESIDENCE
The persons who have been regarded as having their usual residence in the area
of enumeration include:-
(1) Those enumerated in their own houses;
(2) Persons having settled residence in private lodgings;
(3) Resident domestic servants;
(4) Scholars, students, etc., living during term time in residential schools,
etc. ;
(82^87)


---

## Page 124

(5) Inmates of institutions when the nature of the institution suggested
permanent residence;
(6) Crews of Vessels if usually living on shipboard,
BIRTHPLACES
The birthplaces of a small number of persons were not stated on the census return;
It was assumed that these persons were born in the county or county borough of enumera-
tion, except when they were stated to be visitor^ to the household, or bore foreign
names.
After further enquiry in the latter cases it was found possible to classify
them (with five exceptions) under definite headings.
RELIGIONS
The classification of religions includes a separate heading for each religion, the
adherents of which number 10 or more (see Table 19).
The heading "Presbyterian"
includes those who call themselves by this name without qualification, together with
those who describe themselves as members of the Church of Scotland.
Other Presbyterian
denominations are shown at the foot of Table 19.
Members of the Church of England and
the Episcopal Church of Scotland have been included under the heading "Church of Ireland".
The latter group includes all persons returned as "Church" or "Episcopalian".
CX:CUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
There are two categories into which the population may be grouped in their working
capacity, namely, occupa1;ion and industry.
The sole criterion for determining the
occupational classification is the type of work performed irrespective of the purpose
for which it is performed.
On the other hand, industry has to do with the kind of
business with which the occupation is associated.
Thus a carpenter working for a firm
of shipbuilders would be classified under "Carpenter" for occupation but under
"Shipbuilding"' for industry.
Some occupations are exclusively related to particular
industries, e.g., Tobacco Pre^s Filler, Grain Miller;
others are employed in various
industries, e.g.. Clerk, Packer.
The 1951 Censuses were carried out simultaneously in all parts of the British Isles
and this afforded the first opportunity for Northern Ireland to adopt both occupational
and industrial classifications* on exactly the same lines as those used for the rest of
the United Kingdom.

    In the preparation of Tables 23, 24 and 28 the range of Orders, Sub-Orders and
Occupational and Industrial unit groups has been modified by combining closely related
groups where the numbers were small and the preservation or usefulness of the data was
not harmed.
SCXriAL CLASS AND SOCIO-ECONOMIC GROUP
Social Class.-
Social Class grouping provides a convenient arrangement of the unit
groups of the Occupational Classification into five Social Classes based on general
standing within the community, economic circumstances not being taken into account
except in so far as they are reflected in the Occupational Classification.
It is not a
classification of individuals, nor is there a separate assessment of individual position
in the light of personal circumstances apart from the details of occupation.
Assignment
to an occupational group on the basis of the statement of occupation automatically
attracts the Social Class grading appropriate to that occupational group.
In England and Wales grouping of the occupied population by Social Classes has been
a feature of certain census publications since 1911 and, in the expectation that the
information will prove of some value to persons engaged on research work of a medical or
sociological nature, it was decided to include in Table 22 of this Report the allocation
of each occupational unit to the appropriate Social Class.
*Classincatlon of Occupations, 1950. H.M.S.O. Price £1. 10s. Od. net.
Census 1951, Classification of Industries. H.M. S.O. Price 8s. 6d. net.
(82287)


---

## Page 125

There are five Classes in the Social Class grouping, whose nature will be
generally understood from the following broad descriptions:-
Class I
Professional, etc., Occupations
Class II
Intermediate Occupations
Class III
Skilled Occupations
Class IV
Partly Skilled Occupations
Class V
Unskilled Occupations
Socio"Economic Group.» For certain investigations the Social Class grading
described in the preceding paragraph may be said to be inadequate and in Table 22 of
this Report a further socio-economic allocation has been made.
As in the case of
social classes this grading is no more than a convenient re-arrangement of occupational
unit groups and must not be considered as if it were a separate classification of
Individuals.
Its structure is as follows:-
A
Agricultural
1. Farmers.
2. Agricultural Workers.
B
Non>Agricultural
I.
Non-Manual
3. Higher administrative, professional and managerial (including large employers).
4. Intermediate administrative, professional and managerial (including teachers
and salaried staff).
5. Shopkeepers and other small employers.
6. Clerical Workers.
7. Shop assistants.
8. Personal service.
II. Manual
9. Foremen.
10. Skilled Workers.
11. Semi-skilled Workers.
12. Unskilled Workers.
C
Special Group not included elsewhere
13. Armed Forces (Other Ranks)
TABLES CONTAINING DOTS (.) AND DASHES (-)
Throughout this volume dots (.) are used in tabular matter to indicate "Kone",
and dashes (-) are used to indicate that figures are not available or that the column
headings are not relevant to the lines in which the dashes appear.
(82287)


---

## Page 126

PART III
STATISTICAL NOTES
The definitions, etc., given in Part II apply equally to these notes and to -the
tables in Part IV.
AREA
Descriptions and figures are given for areas as constituted at the date of the
1951 census unless otherwise stated.
The area of Northern Ireland, exclusive of larger rivers, lakes and tideways, and
of land on the shores of Loughs Erne, Castlehulme and Macnean, is 5,238 square miles or
about 16 per cent of the area of Ireland.
The distribution of land is approximately:
crops and pasture, 68 per cent; woods and plantations, 2 per cent; mountain, turf
bog and marsh, 24 per cent; and buildings, roads, etc., 6 per cent.
POPULATION
The total population of Northern Ireland was 1,370,921 of which 667,819 were
males and 703,102 were females.
During the intercensal period 1937-1951 there was an
increase of 91,176 persons or 7.1 per cent, representing an average annual increase of
6,463 persons, which was three times as great as the average yearly increase between
1926 and 1937.
Table 2 shows that censuses were taken decennially between 1821 and 1911 and
thereafter at irregular intervals; this should be borne in mind when comparing one
enumeration with another.
Throughout the whole range of enumerations the highest
point was attained in 1841 when the population was 1,648,945 and the lowest point in
1891 when it numbered 1,236,056.
Increases were recorded for the two periods between

    1821 and 1841 but these were followed by a shrinkage of 12.5 per cent for the inter-
censal period 1841-1851.
A. more or less steady decline set in at an average decennial
rate of nearly 4 per oent until 1891 and from that point each intercensal period showed
an increase, the greatest being that of 1937-1951.
The increase between 1926 and 1937
was 1.8.
The increase of 7.1 per cent during the latest intercensal period was only

    once exceeded, viz., 1821-1831 for which a 14;0 per cent increase was shown.
The twenty-six counties which now form the Irish Republic likewise recorded an
increase for each period between 1821 and 1841, but the decline which set in at the

    latter year continued until 1946; for the five-year period 1946-1951 a slight recovery
was shown, the increase being 0.1 per cent.
Comparing Northern Ireland with other parts of the United Kingdom, the following

    figures show the increases for the whole span 1911 to 1951 (1911 being the last
occasion on which censuses were carried out simultaneously throughout the Kingdom):-
Increase for Northern Ireland
9.6 per cent
"
" England (excluding Monmouthshire) 22.3 "
"
"
" Wales and Monmouthshire
7.3 "
''
" Scotland
7.0 "
Natural Increase and Intercensal Migration; The natural increase of a population
is represented by the excess of births over deaths.
The following table shows the
natural increase and the actual intercensal changes (increase or decrease) in popula-
tion for each of the intercensal periods since 1871; it also shows the net loss by
migration.
The tendency to a decline in the average annual excess of births over deaths,
mentioned in the 1937 Census Preliminary Report, has been arrested and it is interesting
to note that the average yearly excess for the latest intercensal period is the highest
recorded since birth and death registration was introduced in 1864.
(82287)

    During the eighty years 1871-1951, births exceeded deaths by 676,771, but the
actual increase in population in that period was only 11,731.
In the 1926 Report it
was pointed out that migration losses between 1871 and 1926 were such that in the latter

    year the population was actually 102,629 less than in 1871: in 1937 the population was
79,445 less than in 1871.

    In the 1937-1951 intercensal period the net loss from migration amounted to 67,267
or about 4', 768 persons per annum which is the lowest average figure on record.
No
allowance is made in these figures for the deaths of Northern Ireland citizens overseas
during World War II and of course, emigration to Commonwealth and Foreign countries was
largely suspended during the period 1939-1945.
Although the net outward movement has
diminished considerably during the present century it still accounts for a substantial
loss of the natural increase in population.
An analysis of the intercensal changes in the population of counties and county
boroughs is given in Table 3.
The figures of net outward movements shown in that table
contain movements between counties and county boroiaghs within the Northern Ireland area,
but the extent of such movements is not revealed; it should of course be understood
that movements to or from other parts of the British Isles and other countries are also
included.
These movements may have varied in direction and intensity in each of the
fourteen years under review.
In earlier volumes for the 1951 census the very slight change in the population of
Belfast County Borough since 1937 was mentioned; this is in marked contrast with the
previous intercensal growth of the borough extending over its whole history since the
earliest censuses.
From an examination of the population figures of the rural areas
adjoining the city boundary it became evident, however, that the development of the
Belfast District was fully maintained and an "Outer Ring" provided accommodation which
was not available within the legal perimeter of the city.
Table II sets out the migratory movements of Commonwealth citizens between
Northern Ireland and non-European countries for each calendar year from 1926 onwards
excepting the years 1939-1945 for which figures are not available.
The figures were
obtained from returns published by the Board of Trade, London.
Relatively heavy emigration occurred during the period 1923 - 1930, but between
1930 and the outbreak of the second world war the world wide economic depression more
or less put an end to emigration from this country and the war itself continued the
standstill until 1946.
In 1932 emigration to non-European countries fell to 610 and
remained under 1,000 up to 1938.
A considerable increase has taken place since the
end of the war and for 1950 a figure of 3,370 was recorded, which is the highest since
1930 when it was 9,217.
By 1953 the figure had increased to 6,300.
In 1932 the
number of immigrants from non-European countries amounted to 3,219 and continued at over
the 1,000 mark until 1938.
Since the end of the war the figure has varied between 787
and 1,152 per annum.
From Table I it was seen that the net movement outward was 67,267 persons for the

    1937 - 1951 period and allowing for a net outward migration by sea of 9,934 to non-
European countries a figure of 57,333 is obtained in respect of all other outward
migration together with losses in H.M. Forces and Mercantile Marine outside Northern
Ireland during the second world war.
(82287)

Intercensal Population Estimates; The populatfon at the middle of each year is
estimated for the purpose of calculating rates of marriages, births, and deaths, and
deaths from certain diseases.
With the population figure of the preceding census as
the starting point and taking the number of births and deaths since registered, the
task of compiling a new population estimate would be a comparatively simple matter,
were it not for migration.
Unfortunately, no information is available about
migration between Northern Ireland and Europe generally, including the other parts of
the British Isles.
The following table shows the estimated population of Northern Ireland as at the
middle of each year from 1937 to 1951.
It will be seen tliat the figures for the period
1940 - 1950 are for civilians only and serve as an index to the steady estimated growth
for those years.

Density of Population; The density of the 1951 population of Northern Ireland was
0.41 persons per acre of 262 persons per square mile.
For England and Wales the
figure was 750 persons per square mile, which is higher than that of nearly every other
country of the World; for Scotland it was 171 and the Irish Republic 111.
1926.

    In 1937 there were 244' persons to the square mile in Northern Ireland and 240 in
Populations of Aggregates of Urban and Rural Areas; The urban and rural distribu-
tion of the population of Northern Ireland is given in Table IV.
36 per cent of the
population live in the two county boroughs, about 26 per cent in the remaining towns
and villages and about 38 per cent in purely rural areas.

Excluding towns and villages whi\3h do not possess urban district status it will
be seen from Table V that since the beginning of the present century there has been a
continued increase in urbanisation rising from 42. 9 per cent of the total population

    in 1901 to 53.1 per cent in 1951.
Although the rural areas showed a percentage
decrease for each of the four intercensal periods between 1901 and 1951, an increase of

    40,424' in actual population occurred between 1937 and 1951.
The rate of gain for
urban areas has, however, been declining throughout the fifty years: in the period
1901 - 1911 the urban population increased by 4>0 per cent of the total population and

    the gain between 1911 and 1926 was practically the same, but between 1926 and 1937 it
had fallen to 2.1 per cent and in 1937 - 1951 to 0.2.
This balance would be disturbed
if some built up sections of rural districts were given urban status.
Large new housing schemes were still in progress at the time of the 1951 census
and movements of population were still continuing and will continue for some time. The
redistribution of population will by now have changed a good deal further since 1951,
and the next census will probably show considerable changes compared with 1951.

## Page 131

Populations of Counties and County Boroughs; Since 1901 the population of Belfast
County Borough increased by 27.1 per cent although the increase in the latest period
1937-1951 was only 1.3 per cent but as already pointed out this is not a true index to
the growth of the city which has far extended beyond its legal bounds and merged into
the several adjoining towns with unrecognised boundaries.
Londonderry County Borough

    increased by 25.6 per cent since 1901: the increase between 1937 and 1951 was 4.8 per
cent.
Antrim and Down are the only counties that had a greater population in 1951
than in 1901.
During the last intercensal period, however, all the counties recorded
an increase with the exception of Fermanagh which had a decrease of 2.8 per cent; the
greatest increases were in Antrim, Down and Londonderry (excluding the County Borough)

    being 17.2 per cent, 14.5 per cent and 11.1 per cent respectively.

Rateable valuations of counties and county boroughs are given in Table 4.
The
total rateable valuation of Northern Ireland in 1951 was £8,508,597, which, after
reduction on account of derating, amounted to £5,987,580.
The corresponding figures

    for 1937 were £7,262,692 and £4,921,575.
Populations of Boroughs and Urban and Rural Districts: The increase of
38,577 persons in urban population as seen in Table VII is made up as follows:- the
two county boroughs, 7,864; the nine municipal boroughs, 16,516; and the twenty-
four urban districts, 14,197.
It should be noted that the comparisons in this table
are for areas as constituted at the date of the 1951 census whereas in Table V the
figures are for the districts as constituted at the date of each census.
Six urban areas exceeded 25 per cent intercensal increase - of these
Whitehead U.D. and Carrickfergus M.B, were the most outstanding being 45.7 and
44.0 per cent respectively.
In the 1926>-1937 period Port Stewart U.D. showed the
highest increase, viz., 55.5 per cent, the next highest was Bangor M.B. at 18.5 per
cent; in the same period decreases were recorded for ten urban areas whereas for
1937-1951 (as indicated above) no urban area registered a decrease.
In both inter-
censal periods the greatest relative increases took place in the seaside urbans.
For 1937-1951 (excluding Belfast County Borough which showed an increase of only
1.3 per cent (5,585 persons)), Ballymoney registered the lowest increase, viz.,
2.5 per cent (80 persons).
The net increase in the population of the thirty-two rural districts (again as
constituted at the 1951 census) amounted to 52,599.
Increases amounting to
(82287)

## Page 132

56,516 persons were recorded in twenty-two districts and decreases totalling
3,917 persons in the remaining ten districts.
As was the case in the 1926-1937
interval increases were greatest for 1937-1951 in Castlereagh, Belfast, Lisburn and

    Londonderry, being 134.4 per cent (25.2 in earlier period), 86.6 (l5.3), 34.9 (l7.4)
and 32.5 (8.2) respectively.
In the 1926--1937 period increased occurred in six rural districts only and there
was an overall net decrease of 2.5 per cent for rural areas whereas in the 1911-1926
period the decrease was 5.8 per cent, but between 1937 and 1951 the overall variation
was arl increase of 8.9 per cent.

* The figures given relate to each area as constituted at the date of the 1951 Census.
The population figures for wards of the county boroughs are included in Table 4.
In Belfast County Borough seven wards show increases and eight show decreases during
the fourteen years 1937-1951.
The most marked increases were in Clifton and Victoria
being 4,954 (lC.6 per cent) in the former and 3,318 (9.8 per cent) in the l a t t e r .
Dock Ward had the greatest decrease, i . e . , 2,771 or 15.9 per cent.
In Londonderry County Borough the population increased between 1937 and 1951 by
2,279 persons or 4.8 per cent.
Each of the three wards showed an increase but the
(B2287)

## Page 133

greatest increase was in South ward (1,909 persons or 8.4 per cent) which is the most
densely populated ward (29.8 persons per acre).
Table 4 also gives detailed information, including rateable valuation for boroughs
and other county districts and wards of the two county boroughs.
Populations of Towns other than Boroughs and Urban Districts; Table 5 gives
information in regard to population, housing, etc., of towns created under the Towns
Improvement (Ireland) Act, 1854, and towns with a population of 1,000 or more but
having no legally defined boundaries.
Three of these towns have populations exceeding
3,000 each and four others exceed 2,000 persons each.
Parliamentary Constituencies; Table 11 sets out the Parliamentary Constituencies
of Northern Ireland for the Parliaments of both the United Kingdom and Northern Ireland
together with figures of population, number of electors and electors per 1,000 of the
population.
Under the provisions of the Elections and Franchise Act (Northern Ireland), 1946,
a person is entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any constituency
returning a member to the Parliament of Northern Ireland if he or she is a British
subject, 21 or more years of age, not subject to any legal incapacity, and was born in
Northern Ireland (or alternatively, has resided continuously in the United Kingdom for
a period of seven years ending on the qualifying date) and either:-
(a) has the requisite residence qualification of three months; or
(b) has occupied for three months prior to the qualifying date business premises
of at least £10 valuation; or
(c) is the wife or husband of a person entitled to be registered in respect of
the above business premises qualification; or
(d) was on the qualifying date a member of the Forces or a merchant seaman and
who, but for such service, would have been residing in Northern Ireland.
The electorate in the Queen's University constituency comprises all graduates of
the University who are British subjects of full age, not subject to any legal
incapacity.
Under the provisions of the Representation of the People Act, 1949, a person- is
entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any Northern Ireland
constituency returning a member to the Parliament at Westminster if that person is a
British subject or citizen of the Irish Republic of twenty--one or more years of age
and not subject to any legal incapacity to vote, is resident at an address in that
constituency on the qualifying date, and has resided at that address (or at any other
address or addresses in Northern Ireland) for a period of three months ending on the
qualifying date.
In addition, a person who would ordinarily have been resident at a specified
address in a constituency in Northern Ireland, may be registered in respect of a
Service qualification it -
(i) he or she is a member of the Forces; or
(ii) he or she is a person employed in the service of the Crown in a post outside
the United Kingdom; or
(iii) she is the wife of a person having a Service qualification and is residing
outside the United Kingdom to be with her husband.
Education Areas: Northern Ireland is divided into eight education areas, one for
each county and county borough.
HOUSING - BUILDINGS, ROOMS, HOUSEHOLDS, ETC,
Houses " Private and Non«Private; The total number of houses, including hotels,
institutions, etc., in Northern Ireland normally used for dwelling purposes was
352,815 of which 331,610 were occupied on Census night.
The number of occupied houses

    at the 1937 Census was 300,065 (in 1926 it was 268,834), an increase therefore of
31,545, or 10.5 per cent under this category took place in the intercensal period.
(82287)

The

    The total number of houses increased from 323,183 in 1937 to 352,815 in 1951 - an
    increase of 29,632 or 9.2 per cent for the 1937-1951 intercensal period as compared with
    an increase of 36,341 or 12.7 per cent between 1926 and 1937 and a decrease of 1.7 per
cent for the period 1911-1926.
The increase is a net figure and represents the excess
of new houses over those demolished (by enemy action or in the normal way) and those
converted to purposes other thafi dwellings for which no data is available.
The number
of uninhabited houses (14,138) is the lowest since 1871.
In view of the demand for
houses which existed in 1951 it was considered advisable to make some enquiries into the
position and a special investigation was made in the case of Belfast County Borough and
Counties Antrim and Down, and in regard to the two counties it was learned that (a) in
about 40 per cent of the cases the usual occupiers we're temporarily absent on holiday,
etc.; (b) a further 40 per cent of the houses uninhabited on census night were used as
summer residences; and (c) 15 per cent were affected by changes in occupancy (or new
houses awaiting occupants).
It is assumed that, broadly speaking, the explanations at
(a) and (c) apply to the other counties.
The number of houses in the course of

    erection - 7,067 - is the highest on record, the previous highest being 1,413 in 1926.
In considering the housing position as disclosed by this report, it should be
borne in mind that every building of whatever type, used as a permanent or occasional
habitation, has been included in the total number of houses.
Contained in this figure
are many temporary structures, which, judged by normal standards, are not suitable, nor
originally intended, for permanent residences, but no information was collected at the
enumeration which would enable even an approximate estimate of the number of such
buildings to be made.
It will be seen that the average number of persons per inhabited house has
steadily decreased throughout the last hundred years, the average standing at 5.66 in

    1851 and 4.09 in 1951.
The size of the census household has also fallen considerably

    since 1851 when it was 5.18 - in 1951 it had decreased to 3.99.
Table IX shows the variations in the number of houses in each county during the
fourteen years 1937-1951.
Table 4 contains details as to housing and accommodation for counties, county
boroughs, county districts and wards of county boroughs.
The increase in the population of 91,176 (or 7.1 per cent) since 1937 and in the
number of households of 33,733 (ll.O per cent) compares with an Increase of 29,632
(or 9.2 per cent) in the number of houses.
The most outstanding increases in the
number of houses were in Counties of Antrim and Down and County Borough of Londonderry,

    the figures being 10,984 (21.6 per cent), 9.135 (l5. 6 per cent) and 1,524 (l7.8 per
(82287)

In the County Antrim figure Belfast and Lisburn Rural Districts
accounted for 5,910 houses of the increase and in that for County Down, Castlereagh
Rural District accounted for 3,903 houses.
All three rural districts adjoin Belfast
County Borough.
Rural districts as a whole show an increase of 13,927 houses (or 8.9 per cent)
while the urban areas (including county borqughs) increased by 15,705 (or 9.4 per cent),
The increase in population of the rurals was 8. 9 per cent exactly the same as the
increase in number of houses: urban population increased by 5. 6 per cent.
Of the
counties. County Fermanagh was the only area to show an 1937-1951 intercensal decrease
(196 houses or 1.4 per cent);
it was also the only area to show a decrease in
population (l,525 or 2.8 per cent).

    For all Northern Ireland the increase between 1926 and 1937 was 36,341 (or
12.7 per cent) in the number of houses; 23,184 (or 1.8 per cent) in the population,
and 26,846 (or 9.6 per cent) in the number of households.
The following statement summarises the position in regard to increases in the
population, number of households and number of houses during the two intercensal

The fact that the increase in the number of houses fell below the increase in house-

## Page 136

The average annual increase in the number of inhabited private houses for the

    period 1926-1937 was 3,032 and for the period 1937-1951 only 2,340, but it must be
borne in mind that house building was largely suspended for six of the fourteen years
in the latter period.
The average annual increase in the population in these dwelling
houses for the first intercensal period was 3^282 and in the latter had risen to 6,562.
The relative changes may be seen more clearly from the figures for "persons per house"

    the ratios having dropped for the period 1926-1937 by 0.39 and by 0.14 for 1937-1951.
Of the total of 329,050 inhabited private houses, 322,743 were occupied by single
private households, 5,005 were shared by two private households and the remaining
1,302 were shared by three or more private households.

    Table X shows the position as at each census 1926, 1937 and 1951, for inhabited
private houses of various sizes, for Northern Ireland, and the extent to which they
were shared by two or more households.

The number of houses with three or less rooms fell (proportionately as well as

    numerically) for both the intercensal periods 1926-1937 and 1937-1951 and the number
and proportion of houses with four or five rooms increased over the whole period,
while the number of houses with six or more rooms also increased in number (but
decreased as a ratio of the total) though at a much slower rate.
The number of inhabited houses containing 1-3 rooms in rural areas as a whole
decreased considerably during both census periods while the number with 4-5 rooms
increased substantially.

For all Northern Ireland the percentage of shared houses with six or more rooms

    fell from 3.6 in 1926 to 2.3 in 1937 but rose again to 4.0 in 1951.
The percentage
of shared houses with 4-5 rooms fell substantially during the period 1926-1937, i.e.,
by 3.1, but the decrease during the later period was only 0.2.
Shared houses with
1-3 rooms decreased from 1.1 per cent in 1926 to 0.4 per cent in 1937 and rose to 0.5
in 1951.
The percentage of shared houses as a whole decreased considerably between

    1926 and 1937 (l.8) but increased during 1937-1951 by 0.3 - this was to be expected
in a course of years yielding a high increase in population and including several
years when building of private houses was almost at a standstill.
In Counties Antrim, Armagh, Down and Tyrone less than one per cent of the total
number of inhabited private houses were shared; in Fermanagh 1.3 per cent were
shared, and in Belfast County Borough and Londonderry County (including the County
Borough) 3.4' per cent and 3.5 per cent respectively were shared.
The information provided in Table X for 1951 is readily available for counties
and county boroughs from Table 6.
From Table 6 it will be observed that the total number of rooms in inhabited

    private houses was 1,498,139 (the corresponding figure for 1937 was 1,341,943 and for
1926 it was 1,176,071).
(82287)

    The average number of rooms per house was 4.55 in 1951 as compared with 4.53 in
    1937 and 4; 47 in 1926.

    houses) for the enumerations 1926, 1937 and 1951:-

The population of 1,320,863 occupying private houses was comprised of
337,581 households within the,definition of "Private Households" given in Part II. In

    1937 there were 302,631 households and in 1926 there were 273,668.
The increase of

    34,950 households (11.5 per cent) between 1937 and 1951 was at a somewhat greater rate
than the rate of increase in population comprising them (population increase was
7.5 per cent).

    In the period 1926-1937 the percentage increase in households was 10.6
and that of population comprising them 3.0.
The disparity, therefore, between growth
in population and in households was much greater in the earlier intercensal period than
in the period 1937-1951.
Households of all sizes increased with the exception of those
of seven, eight, nine and ten persons each.
The household factor rather than that of population affects the question of housing,
but in considering the demand for household accommodation regard must be had to the one
person per household group.

    This group, which rose from 25,781 in 1937 to 31,537 in
1951, formed 9.3 per cent of the total private households as compared with 8.5 in 1937.
In 1951 28 per cent occupied one or two rooms which suggests that they were lodgers
having their meals apart: in 1937 the figure was 36 per cent.
On the other hand over
one half of this group was occupying accommodation consisting of four rooms or more but
it is not possible to say from the census returns to what extent the position is
affected by persons temporarily absent on census night.
For example, there are
undoubtedly widows providing accommodation for boarders who spend week-ends at their
homes in another area.
The position in areas other than Belfast remained fairly steady between 1937 and
1951, but in Belfast County Borough the percentage of persons living alone increased
from 6.1 to 8.5..
As regards the Belfast area the matter will be dealt with in the
section relating to Belfast and Environs.
Table 7, in which the measurement of households according to the number of persons
in each household is detailed, shows that the number of households rises from 31,537 of
one person each, to 66,560 of the two-person group, to 67,526 of the three-person group
and to 61,078 households of the four-person group.
A gradual decline continued from
that point.
The three-person group comprises 15.3 per cent of the population of all households,
the four-person group 18.5 per cent and the five-person group 16.4 per cent - these
three categories make up over 50 per cent of the total number of persons in all private
households.
The two-person group comprises 19.7 per cent of all households, the three-
person group 20.0 per cent and the four-person group 18.1 per cent - these three
categories make up 57.8 per cent of the total number of private households.
The

    comparable figures for 1937 were 18.9 for this two-person group, 19.5 for the three-
person group and 17.2 for the four-person group, making a total of 55.6 per cent.
The household of three persons occupying four rooms was the most common, and this
applied not only to the country as a whole but to the aggregates of urban areas and
(82287)


---

## Page 138

rural areas.
4 similar position was recorded in respect of Belfast County Borough,
Down County and Londonderry County (excluding the County Borough): in Antrim County
and Londonderry County Borough the household of four occupying four rooms was the most
common combination; in Armagh County the two person household with four rooms and in
Fermanagh the two person household in occupation of three rooms was most evident;
Tyrone had the most common combination in the four roomed occupancy by the two person
and three person households which were numerically equal.
The average size of a
private household was 3.9 persons in 1951 and 4.1 persons in 1937.
Densities: In order to determine the measure of accommodation essential if over-
crowding is to be avoided, a knowledge of many factors such as size of household,
number, ages, and sex of children and number and size of rooms is necessary.
Moreover,
it is a matter of individual judgment as to what weight should be given to each factor.
In dealing with the information about accommodation recorded at the census, the size of
the census household has been expressed by the number of persons, irrespective of sex or
age, while the rooms comprising the accommodation have been limited to the usual living
rooms.
The resulting rates of "persons per room" have been regarded as an approximate
measure of density of room occupation, and a rate of over two persons per room has been
assumed to indicate overcrowding.
Table 7 gives the composition of private households, the number of persons
comprising each household, the number of rooms occupied and the density of room
occupation.
There were 1,343 Private Households living at densities of more than four persons
per room and 4', 682 at over three persons per room; 20,155 households or 6. 0 per cent
fell within the over two per room category.

    Of the 86,552 private households in the 1-3 room group, 15, 024" were comprised of
only one person each.
There were 5,936 households (13,779 persons) living in one-

    roomed tenements: in 1937 there were 4,845 such households (10,280 persons) and in
    1926 there were 7,229 (18,095 persons).
The 5,936 households in one-roomed tenements
were distributed as follows:- Belfast County Borough 2,677; Londonderry County

    Borough 580; other urban areas 551; rural districts 2,128.
The number of households

    of two or more persons in this type of accommodation for 1926, 1937 and 1951 were 4; 626,
    2,517, 3,471 respectively: of the 3,471 households in 1951 there were 1,744 in Belfast
alone (an Increase of 984 since 1937) and doubtless this group is one of the sources
from which a demand for new houses emanates.
The particulars of households in two and three-roomed accommodation are:-

As already stated, of the 329,050 inhabited private houses 6,307 or 1.9 per cent
were occupied by two or more households - 5,005 accommodated two households and
1,302 accommodated three or more households.

    Private houses accommodated 1,320,863 persons and of these 1,163,715 or 88.1 per
cent were living at not more than two persons per room: in 1937 the figure was
1,065,768 or 86.7 per cent, so that a slight mitigation of general overcrowding is
indicated.
Table 7 shows that, taking the total number of persons in private households and
the total number of rooms occupied, there was an average of 0.88 persons per room. In

    1926 the corresponding figure was 1.01 and that for 1937 was 0.92, so that there has
been an improvement in room accommodation during both intercensal periods, though the
rate of improvement was higher in the first period.

    Table XII gives the densities of room occupation for 1926, 1937 and 1951 Censuses
for the whole Country.
It will be seen that the proportions of persons living at various densities of

    over two persons per room decreased from 216,495 in 1926 to 163,223 in 1937 and further
decreased to 157,148 in 1951, while the proportion of persons living at densities of
two persons or less per room increased during both periods. In 1926 the figure for this

    latter group was 976,392, it rose to 1,065,768 in 1937 and to 1,163,715 in 1951.
(82287)

Table XIII shows, by Coxinties, the population in private households living at two
persons and less per room and at over two persons per room, also persons per 1,000
living at these densities, and for comparison, the 19S6 and 1937 figures for persons
per 1,000 living at a density of over two persons per room are included.
In Northern

    Ireland as a whole in 1951 there were 119 persons per 1,000 living at a density of
over two persons per room.
Londonderry County and County Borough had the highest
figure in the country recording 198 persons per 1,000 (looked at separately the county
(82287)


---

## Page 140

    had 215 and the county borough 163 per 1,000), whilst Tyrone came next with 187 persons
per 1,000.
The smallest overcrowding proportions were in Antrim County (90 persons

    per 1,000) and Belfast County Borough (92 persons per 1,000).
Comparison with previous censuses shows that in the Province as a whole there has
been a reduction in the number of persons per 1,000 living at a density of over two

    persons per room from 182 in 1926 to 133 in 1937 and 119 in 195.1, so that the rate of
improvement has been slower in the» more recent intercensal period.
Only Belfast

    County Borough showed an increase (from 85 in 1937 to 92 in 195.1) over the 1937 figures
for persons living at this density.
Other counties showed a decrease in the proportion
of overcrowding (i.e., in persons living at over two persons per room) for both
intercensal periods.

In 1926 the population of the 4,014 hotels and boarding houses enumerated was
30,42S, i . e . , about one in every forty of the total population.
In 1937 there were

    only '2,569 such i n s t i t u t i o n s with a population of 17, 953'persons, i . e . , about one in
every seventy of the total population, and in 1951 there was a further drop to 1,423
hotels and boarding houses with a population of 12,622 or about one in every hundred
and ten of ttfe total population.

    Between 1926 and 1937 the number of inmates in institutions increased from 12,293
    to 13,676 ( i . e . , by 11.3 per cent) and increased further Xo 14,642 in 1951 (an increase
of 7.1 for the 1937-1951 intercensal period).

    In 1937 the number of inmates in hospitals was 11,111, and in 1951 13,557.
(82S87)

## Page 141

USUAL RESIDENCE
The enquiry as to *TFsual Residence" of each individual irrespective of the address
at which he or she was enumerated disclosed that only l. 6 per cent of the enumerated
population were normally resident elsewhere than in the urban or rural area of enumera-
tion and less than one half of these resided outside Northern Ireland.
Detailed
figures are contained in Table 12 on a county and county borough basis.
The following statement sets out the position in respect of persons enumerated in
Northern Ireland but usually resident in some other part of the British Isles, for the

SEX, AGE AND MARITAL CONDITION
Table 13 gives the population for the whole of Northern Ireland according to sex
and age by individual years and marital condition, and gives also aggregates of urban
and rural areas by quinquennial age groups and marital condition;
a county distribu-
tion by single ages is contained in Table 11 of the county volumes.
Sex Proportion!^:
Table 2 shows that since 1821 when the first census of popula-
tion was taken affecting Northern Ireland the number of females exceeded the number of
males at every enumeration - variations of from 5.5 to 10.'2 per cent were recorded.
The proportion of females to every 1,000 males stood at its highest point in 1881 when

    the figure was 1,102 and at its lowest in 1951 at 1,053.
In 1901 there were 1,097

    females to 1,000 males, but between that year and 1951 males increased by 77,864 and
females by only 56,105, which accounts for the reduced preponderance of females.
The
gain was particularly noticeable during the period 1901-1926, males having increased by
18,133 and females by only 1,476;

    in the 1926-1951 span males increased by 59,731 and
females by 54,629.
The counties provide greater contrasts than the country as a whole in this matter.

    In 1951 Fermanagh had the lowest figure at 912 females to every 1,000 males whereas
Belfast County Borough stood at the other end of the scale and registered a proportion
of 1,117 females to every 1,000 males.
Since 18'21 Fermanagh recorded the lowest

    figure (891 in 1937) and Belfast C. B. the highest (1,194 in 1881).
As there are always more male than female births, the ratio is less than 1,000
females per 1,000 males in the early ages of life and under one year of age there were

    932 girls to every 1,000 boys in 1951.
    In 1937 the figure was 966 and 951 in 19>26.
Generally, the preponderance swings in favour of the girls in adolescence and it will be

    seen from Table 13 that at each age up to 19 males were in the majority in 1951 while
from age 20 onwards the number of females exceeds males except at age 101 when the
number was equal.
In 1937 and 1926 boys outnumbered girls at each age up to and
including 15.

    In the quinquennial groups lying between '20-24 and 45--49 (inclusive) the females
never exceeded 108 to every 100 males;

    while between the 45-^49 group and the 80-84
group the highest rate per 100 was 128.
From the latter point the differential
increased steadily.
(88287)


---

## Page 142

xxxii
In the urban areas females outnumbered males by -45,109 but in the rural areas males
outnumbered females by 9,826.
In urban areas there were more boys than girls in each
quinquennial group under 15 years of age, thereafter females were numerically superior
in each group: in rural areas the number of males exceeded females in all groups under
50 years of age but thereafter females were in the majority.

The Increase In the average age has been steady since 1881.
Each county volume for the 1951 census gave the average ages of the population at

    the censuses of 1911, 1926, 1937 and 1951.
The figures for the censuses prior to 1951
quoted in the county volumes were taken from previous publications, but it became evi-
dent as the work on the 1951 census progressed that a different method had been used in
calculating the average age in each county at the 1937 census to that employed at other
censuses, which resulted in the average age for each county in 1937 being stated exactly
1.0 above' the figure which would have been arrived at if the normal method had been
adopted.

From these figures i t will be seen that Londonderry County and County Borough and
Tyrone County were the only areas to record a decrease during the 1937-1951 period, the
decrease in the former being 0.'2 and in the latter 0.5 years.
All "^but Tyrone County
had a higher average in 1951 than in 1911, and the figure for that County was down by
only 0.1.
It is interesting to note that the 1951 census report for Edinburgh (which city
compares with Belfast in regard to size) shows the average age of the population as
35.9 years.
At the 1931 Census the average was 33.0.
The average age for the whole

    of Scotland in 1951 was 34.0 as compared with 31.3 in 1931, a rise of *2.7 in twenty
years as compared with a rise of 0.6 in Northern Ireland in fourteen years.
The Second Preliminary Report contained a table showing the percentage distribu-
tion by age groups from the census of 1841 onwards which is reproduced "here (Table XV).
The main features are (a) the percentage increase in the number of persons aged 65 and
over from -4.3 in 1841 to 9.9 in 1951 and (b) the regularity of decline in the number of
children londer 15 years of age.
This trend was arrested in 1951 for the first time,
though the improvement between 1937 and 1951 is slight.

Tiie continuing increase in the proportion of older persons has created social and
economic problems not only in Northern Ireland but throughout the whole of the
United Kingdom.

    In the 36 years between 1901 and 1937 persons aged 65 and over
    increased at the rate of 1,121 per annum but between 1937 and 1951 the annual rate of
increase was 1,383.
In Northern Ireland the proportion of people over 65 was 81 per

    1,000 in 1926; 90 in 1937 and 99 in 1951.
In Scotland the figures were - 73 in 1931
and 99 in 1951.
The information in the One per cent Sample Peport shows that for

    England and TlHales the figure in 1951 was 109 per 1,000 as compared with 74 in 1931.
Although the population in Northern Ireland is ageing, the process is more gradual
than in Great Britain, due principally to the higher birth rate in Northern Ireland.

    During the twenty years 1931-1951 the population in England and lales aged 65 and
upwards increased by 62 per cent (the total population increased by 9.5 per cent): in
Scotland by 44 per cent (total population by 5.2 per cent) and over the twenty-five

    years 1926-1951 by 33 per cent (total population by 9.1 per cent) in Northern Ireland.
The following chart shows the proportion of the population at certain age groups
at the 1951 censuses in England and Wales, Scotland, and Northern Ireland.
The total population of Northern Ireland aged 60 and over increased by 12 per cent

    between 1937 and 1951: the number of males of 60 and over increased fromi 79,842 to
85,178 or 7 per cent, while the women in the same group registered an increase of 16 per
cent, rising from 90,007 to 104,340.
This section of the population accounted for
14 per cent of the total in 1951.
It is significant to note that the number of men at

    each individual age between 60 and 68 was fewer in 1951 than in 19;:
The number fell

    by 3,543, from 45,40^2 to 41,859, and the greatest loss was 1,167 at age 60.
The

    of women between 60 and 68 rose by 3,265, from 47,708 to 50,973.
number
As stated earlier in this report, there was an increase of 91,176 in the enumerated
population in the intercensal period, but it will be seen from table XVI that this
increase was not evenly spread over the population in the various age groups.
Apart
from the two groups 0-4 and 5-9 there were losses in all quinquennial groups up to
(82287)

age 29.
The gain in the 0-9 group (-40,887) converts the loss in the other groups up

    to 29 into a total gain of '20,378 from age 0 to 29 (12,649 males and 7,729 females).
Above age 29, an increase was registered in all quinquennial groups with the
exception of that over 100, which is numerically negligible.
The total increase in
these groups numbered 70,798.
Table XVI (which is restricted to certain age groups) shows that the greatest per-
centage losses (7.5) occurred in the 10-14 group in the case of both males and females.
A substantial loss was also recorded in regard to females in the 15-19 group and in the
20-'24 group for both sexes.
These losses are in the main accounted for by the low
birth rates experienced from 1927 to 1941.
In 1942 the number of births increased
considerably and while the high rates attained in the later war years and immediate
post-war years have not been maintained the numbers have not fallen to the level of the
1927-1941 period.
For both sexes the highest percentage gains were recorded in the 75 and over
group.

    Males registered an increase of 6,487 (45.2 per cent) and females 7,331
(38.8 per cent).

    In 1937 the 10-14 quinquennial age group accounted for the largest number of the
population, comprising 61,501 males and 59,243 females or 9.4 per cent of the enumera-
ted population, as compared with 56,904 males and 54,785 females or 8.1 per cent in
1951.
In 1951 the age group 0-4 exceeded a l l other groups with 70,641 males and

    67,20'2 females or 10.1 per cent of the t o t a l population as compared with 57, 131 males
and 54,808 females or 8.7 per cent in 1937.
(82287)

## Page 147

    It will be seen that during the half century 1901-1951 in the groups from 45 yea
of age and upwards there was a steady increase, rising from 22.8 per cent of the whol'
population to 29.'9 per cent.

    Between 15 and 44 years there was a decrease from 46. 5 to 42. 5 per cent and in tt
    lower group of 0-14 years the decrease of 3.6 per cent between 1901 and 1937 was parti
recovered in 1951 when an increase of 0.5 was recorded.
In the whole span from

    15 years to 64 years there was a decrease in the fifty years (1901-1951) of 0.7 per
cent on the group as a ratio of the total population and a corresponding increase in
the higher and lower age ranges.
Within the working age structure, i.e., 15 to

    64 years, the 15-44 group decreased by 4.0 per cent of the whole population and in the
45-64 group there was an increase of 3. 3 per cent.
The following table enables comparisons to be made between the age distributions
of the urban and rural populations of the country at the censuses of 1926, 1937 and

It will be seen that the downward tendency in urban areas in the age groups 0-14

    and 15-'44 between 1926 and 1937 continued during the 1937-1951 period and that the
upward tendency in the other two groups has also continued.
The pattern is more
indefinite in rural areas where each age group except 0-14 suffered a loss since 1937
after registering a gain in the 1926-1937 period.
The 0-14 group having fallen by

    (i.2 per cent between 1926 and 1937 increased by 1.6 per cent between 1937 and 1951.
It is of interest to note that nearly sixty per cent of the increase since 1937 in the
0-14 group is contained in the areas surrounding Belfast County Borough, i.e. Belfast,
Lisburn and Castlereagh Rural Districts.
Age - Accuracy of Census Returns; As in 1926 and 1937 the age question in the
census schedule required the age to be stated in years and months.
The-object of
including the column for months was to encourage a greater concentration on the enquiry
and avoidance of loose or approximate statements.
Despite this precaution it is
apparent from table 13 that there is an unnatural concentration at certain ages,

    particularly those ending with digit 0, e.g. 30, 40, 50, etc., from which it is infer-
red that over certain portions of the age field many of the ages have been returned in
an approximate form.
There is, however, evidence that the 1951 position shows a con-
siderable improvement in this respect on previous enumerations.
This approximation was experienced in all parts of the country, and since the form
of distortion it introduces is minimised, if not eliminated, by the aggregation of
individual years into quinary or other groupings, the aggregation of the figures over
all adult ages in which the error is most prominent may be regarded as essential for
the use of the material either for administrative or statistical purposes.
Marital Condition; If the sex-age Structure of a population is of importance for
calculating-dependency, labour supply, survival trends, etc., and providing information
for sociological, medical, and other fields of research, its usefulness is enhanced
when coupled with the classification according to marital status.
Table 13 gives this sex-age structure with regard to conjugal condition for 1951,
and Table XVI, although restricted to certain age groups, provides a convenient basis
for comparison of the 1937 statistics with those of 1951.
(82287)

## Page 148

XXXVlll
Since 1927 the number of persons in the Province aged 14 years and upwards

    increased by 56,914 of whom 26,486 were males and 30,428 females.
The number of

    single persons in this age group decreased by 34,831 (14,105 males and 20,726 females)
    and the number of married increased by 87,348 (42,098 males and 45,250 females).
Of the total male population aged 14 years and over, 43.-4 per cent were single in

    1951 as against 49.0 per cent in 1937.
Married men comprised 51. 7 per cent of the
same age group and 45.6 per cent in 1937.
Of the female population aged 14 years and

    over 39.4 per cent were single in 1951 and 46.0 per cent in 1937.
Married women

    accounted for 49.2 per cent of the same group; in 1937 the percentage was 43.1
Table XVI shows that the niomber and proportion of married males in all the age
groups increased since 1937 and that all groups of widowed and divorced decreased
except those of 75 years and over; the over all increase of married male§ was 20.1 per
cent and the over all decrease of widowed and divorced was 6.0 per cent.
Between 1926
and 1937 married males increased by 9.1 per cent and widowed and divorced males by
1.4 per cent.
In the female groupings there was an increase in all age categories of

    married persons between 1937 and 1951 - the over all increase being '21.0 per cent.
The widowed and divorced increased by 10.9 per cent.
Between 1926 and 1937 married
females increased by 8.7 per cent and widowed and divorced by 0.'2 per cent.
The number of persons whose marriages had been dissolved and who had not re-

    married increased from 150 in 1937 to 1,303 in 1951 - divorced men numbered 465 and
    divorced women 838 in 1951, the figures in 1937 being 68 men and 82 women.
From Table 13 it will be observed that in each quinquennial age group up to and
including 40-44, females were in the majority amongst the married persons and there-
after males were numerically superior except in the 95 and over group in which there
were more married females than males.
This relativity obtained also for the same age
groups in urban areas; in the rural areas married females outnumbered married males
in all groups up to and including 40-44 years and thereafter married males were in the
majority in all categories.
The following statement shows the proportions married at various age groups in
1937 and 1951.
It will be noted that the highest percentage increases occurred in the

    '25-'29 group for both females (13.2) and males (10.2).
It was only in 1948 that it
became possible to calculate the average age on marriage.
In that year the age was
30.4 for males and 26.8 for females.
By 1951 the average ages had dropped to '29.7 and

    26.3 and for 1953 stands at '29.5 and '26.2.

Table 21 shows the numbers of married couples enumerated together in quinquennial
age groups of husbands and wives.
A scrutiny of the figures in this table shows that
while the average age of all the husbands was higher than that of all the wives such
male seniority did not prevail a.t all ages of either husbands or wives.
In 1951 as in

    1926 and 1937 husbands under 25 years were married to wives whose average age was a
little higher than their own but thereafter in each age group the average age of
husbands was higher than that of their wives.

    In the age group 40-44' for 1951 wives
were approximately 3 years younger than their husbands, in the 65-69 group they were
6' years younger and in 85 and over category they were 13 years junior to their husbands.
Again, this last group shows no change from the position in 1926 and 1937.
The number of marriages registered and the rates - that is the number of marriages
per 1,000 of the population - for the years in which Censuses were taken in and since
1871 in Northern Ireland were as follows:-

EDUCATION
10.6
9.2
8.2

6.9
5.4
In 1926 information was sought as to the type of school attended and whether the
attendance was at day or evening classes, but in 1951 a householder was merely required
to say whether any person enumerated on the schedule was attending an educational
establishment, full-time or part-time.
Notwithstanding the restricted form of
question, it is evident that many heads of households were not conscious of its signifi-
cance as regards part-time attendance.
The total part-time students enrolled in the
institutions of further education under the management of the different education

    authorities during the year ended 31st July, 1951 numbered over 32,000, but the total
for the Province returned at the Census was under '2,600.
The discrepancy may be
accounted for to some extent by a falling off in technical school m.embership as the
Census was taken towards the end of the winter session but a more likely explanation is
that many part-time students were normally following an occupation and regarded the
technical instruction as of relatively minor importance.
Consequently information in
this respect was omitted from the census return.
Table 15 summarises the information furnished in Northern Ireland regarding educa-
tion.
Of children aged 5-13 years inclusive, 4.4 per cent were not attending any
educational establishment.
The great majority of these, however, were aged 5 years
and although legislation provides for a compulsory lower limit school age of 5 years,
education authorities are empowered to raise this limit to 5|^ or 6 years - a provision
that had to be adopted in some areas partly because of the shortage of accommodation
and partly on account of the scarcity of qualified teachers.
In the 14-17 years group, i.e., young persons over the compulsory school age but
of normal age for attending grammar schools, 72.'2 per cent were returned as not attend-
ing any educational establishment.
BIRTHPLACES

    Table 16 shows that of the 1,370,921 persons enumerated, 1,237,950 (90.30 per cent)
    were born in Northern Ireland, 61,056 (4.45) in the Irish Republic and 62,046 (4.53) in
Great Britain; in other words 99. 28 per cent of the population were born in the
United Kingdom, Islands of the British seas and the Irish Pepublic.
The figures for

    1937 respectively are 1,162,329 (90.83); 59,329 ('4.64) and 48,905 (3.82), a total of
99.29 per cent.
The number of persons born abroad increased by 0.01 per cent, i.e.

    from 9,105 in 1937 to 9,829 in 1951, while in the previous intercensal period the
    figure jumped by 1,333 from 0.62 to 0.71 per cent.
Of the 9,869 persons born outside

    the British Isles (including 40 born at sea) 4,401 (44.6 per cent) were born in other
Commonwealth countries.
(82287)

•Persons born In the Isle of Man and the Channel Islands have been Included under this
heading.

    In 1951 they numbered 168 persons born In the Isle of Man and 105 In the
Channel Islands.
I t will be noted that the percentage of the t o t a l population actually born in

    Northern Ireland fell steadily between 1881 and 1926, v i z . , from 94.39 to 90.05;
    between 19S6 and 1937 there was an increase to 90.83 but this fell again to 90.30 in
19 51.
The percentage born in the Irish Republic increased during each intercensal period

    between 1881 and 1926, viz., from 3.41 to 5.09 but fell to 4.64 in 1937 and fell further
    to 4.45 (the lowest since 1901) in 1951.
As regards actual numbers, the 1951 figure

    stands higher at 61,056 than at any census since 1881, except that of 1926 when a
figure of 63,919 was recorded.

    The increase between 1937 and 1951 amounted to 1,727
    persons as compared with a decrease of 4,590 during the 1926-1937 period.
.The figures
quoted include visitors: in 1937 - 1,400 persons were enumerated who gave^ their usual
residence as within the twenty-six counties and in 1951 - 1,726 persons were so returned.
Taking Northern Ireland as a whole, the position of the sexes is most interesting.
Between 1926 and 1937 the loss was divided almost equally between the sexes, but in the

    1937-1951 period the number of females increased by 1,979 and the males decreased by
252.
In the case of females for the latter period the greatest variations occurred in
Antrim and Down which showed gains of 1,345 and 1,091 respectively and in Belfast which
showed a decrease of 986.
As regards males, Antrim and Down also recorded the highest

    increases of 678 and 634 respectively and Belfast a decrease of 1,382.
Of the 61,056 persons born in the Irish Republic 33,966 were enumerated in Belfast
County Borough and the adjoining Counties of Antrim and Down; 9,998 were enumerated in
Londonderry County and County Borough.
The percentage born in England and Wales was 2.97 which is the highest figure for
the ninety years 1861-1951.
For Scotland the percentage was 1.56 being the lowest for
fifty years.

    In Commonwealth Countries abroad 1,646 of a total of 4,*401 were born in Canada
(including Newfoundland and Labrador) and 965 were born in India.
Of the 5,428 persons born in Foreign Countries (including 5 for whom the country of
birth could not be ascertained) '2,819 were born in the United States of America, 434 in
Germany, '285 in Italy and 210 in Russia.
(82287)

There was in the interval an increase of 12,856 or 11.5 per cent in residents.
The .increase of visitors was much greater as a percentage but numerically the figures
have a negligible influence on those for the total population.
In the absence of
figures for mortality amongst this section of the community and also for migration
outwards during the intercensal years it becomes difficult to locate persons of the
1937 groups in the appropriate 1951 categories.
Table XVIII shows that the number of persons born outside Northern Ireland whose
duration of residence is less than 10 years is 4'2,086 and if, to cover the entire inter-

    censal period of fourteen years from 1937 to 1951, two-fifths of the 10-20 year resi-
dence group is added, a figure of approximately 50,000 is arrived at.
Therefore, of
the total increase in population, which amounted to 91,176, over 55 per cent is
accounted for by persons who entered the province since the date of the 1937 census and
who regard themselves as permanent residents.

## Page 152

xlli
RELIGIONS
The question on "Religion" in the return asked for the name of the particular
religious denomination or body - with as precise a description as possible - in respect
of each person enumerated.
The Census Act, however, provided that a person should not
be subject to any penalty for refusing to state his religious profession; altogether
5,865 persons (or 0.4 per cent of the total population) failed to furnish information

    on this subject - in 1937 there were 2, 274 persons (or 0.2 per cent) and in 1926. there
were 2,304 (or 0.2 per cent) who failed to answer the question.
No enquiry was made
to ascertain the reasons for the increase since 1937 but the evidence available sug-
gests that the following factors accounted for it in some measure:-
(1) a large number of omissions was in respect of persons born in Great Britain
who were not accustomed to this question in census returns;
(2) a growing consciousness that it was not obligatory to answer the question;
and
(3) the interchange of shipping census forms between Northern Ireland and
Great Britain - the forms issued in Great Britain and collected in
Northern Ireland did not include the enquiry on religion.
Table 19 shows the population under five religious groups, i.e. Roman Catholic,
Presbyterian, Church of Ireland, Methodist and "Other and Not Stated Denominations";
this Table sets out the figures according to counties and county boroughs for the
first four of these categories and gives, in addition, for the remaining category for
all Northern Ireland the denominations (under 51 different descriptions) that returned
not less than 10 adherents each.
Table 20 gives the five religious groupings - according to age (by individual
years up to 21 years and by quinquennial groups from 20 years upwards) for the Province
as a whole and Table 14 gives figures by quinquennial groups and marital condition.
The numbers of persons in each of the five religious groups from 1861 to 1951
(inclusive) are given in Table XIX; each group is shown also as a percentage of the
whole population.

* This figure Includes Church of England and Episcopal Church of Scotland.
In 1951 Roman Catholics comprised 34.4 per cent of the total population; the

    figure for 1861 was 40.9 from which year there was a steady decline until 1926 when
33.5 per cent (as in 1937) was registered.
The highest point shown for Presbyterians was in 1861 when the denomination
accounted for 32.7 per cent of the people - the ratio fluctuated somewhat over the

    ninety years until 1951 when the lowest figure (29.9 per cent) was recorded (in 1937
the percentage was 30.5).
(82287)


---

## Page 153

xliii
Adherents of the Church of Ireland represented i23. 0 per cent of the population in

    1861 - this was the lowest figure for the whole span 1861 - 1951.
Increases were
recorded for each succeeding census until 1926 when '27.0 per cent was reached and this

    figure obtained also in. 1937; between 1937 and 1951 however there was a drop to -25.8
per cent.
The two categories 'Methodist" and "Other and Not Stated Denominations" stood at
their lowest percentages of the total population in 1861 when '2.0 and 1.4 respectively
were recorded, and at their peak in 1951 with the respective figures of 4.9 and 5.0.
Turning to the counties and county boroughs for 1951, Roman Catholics were pro-
portionately greatest in Feimanagh (55.6 per cent of the county population),Tyrone
(55.4 per cent) and Londonderry County and County Borough (49.5 per cent); in
Londonderry County Borough, however, this denomination comprised 63.1 per cent of the
population; the county with the smallest proportion was Antrim with 22.1 per cent.
Presbyterians were strongest in Antrim County where they formed 44.9 per cent of
the whole, and Down County came next with 36.4 per cent; their lowest figures were in
Fermanagh (3.4 per cent) and Armagh County (15.0 per cent).
The Church of Ireland recorded their highest ratios in Fermanagh (32.8 per cent),
Armagh County ('29.8 per cent) and Belfast County Borough ('29.7 per cent) and their
lowest ratio in Londonderry County and County Borough (20.3 per cent) - in the County
Borough alone they represented only 15.7 per cent.
Methodists returned the highest percentage in Belfast County Borough at 7.8 and
other Denominations were their greatest in Antrim County, i.e. 6.4 per cent.
Although the religious groups changed as ratios of the whole population since

    1937 all groups showed a numerical increase for the intercensal period 1937-1951,
    whereas during the period 1926-1937 a loss of '2,443 was recorded by Presbyterians.
The following statement shows for the various denominations the numerical changes
during the two intercensal periods and the percentage which each formed of the increase
in the total population.

Amongst the smaller denominations the most marked numerical increases were Baptist

    (+ 2,494), Congregationalist (+ 1,438), Brethren (+ 964), Jehovah's Witness (+418);
the greatest decrease was in the Non-Subscribing Presbyterian or Unitarian Church
(- 483).
An examination of the figures for religions combined with ages reveals that the
Roman Catholic is the most youthful of the denominations.
Although it comprises only
34.4 per cent of the total population it accounts for 41.3 per cent of the children

    under 5 years of age, 38.4 per cent of those under 25 years and 30.8 per cent of those
of 50 years of age and over.
On the other hand Presbyterians represent 26.3 per cent

    of the population under 5 years, '27.3 per cent of those under 25 years and 32.8 per cent
of those of 50 years and over.
Church of Ireland account for 24. 1 per cent of the

    children under 5 years, '25.4 per cent of persons under 25 years and 25.7 of those of
50 years and upwards.
The following statement sets out for the various denominations in 1937 and 1951
the percentage of persons in certain age groups to the total population in those groups.

    Of the Roman Catholics 47. 9 per cent were under 25 years of age in 1951, of
Presbyterians S9.'2 per cent, of Church of Ireland members 42.'2 per cent, of Methodists
39. 5 per cent and of Other Denominations 37.-4 per cent were of this age group.
Regarding the three main groups, therefore, viz. Roman Catholic, Presbyteriaji and
Church of Ireland the Presbyterian group was the most elderly and as already pointed
out the Roman Catholic was the most youthful.
Of the total male population aged 15 and upwards 46.8 per cent were married in

    1937 and 53.0 per cent in 1951: the figures for females are 44.*2 and 50.2 respectively.
In 1937, the percentage of Roman Catholic males in the same age group who were married

    was 40.2 which increased to 44.5 in 1951: the figures for all other denominations are
50.0 and 57.0.
As regards females, the percentages in the case of Roman Catholics

    are 39.4 in 1937 and 43.4 in 1951 while those for other denominations are 46.5 and 53.5.
The general indication that marriage is taking place at earlier ages is proven
by the following figures which express the number of those married and widowed in the
age group 20-29 as a percentage of all persons in that group.
