OCCUPATION AND INDUSTRIES
Tables '22 to *28 classify in a variety of ways the census data regarding the
"Economically Active Population" according to occupation, industry, and industrial
status (e.g., employer, employee, worker on own account), etc.
The occupational and industrial tables include classifications by age groups and
marital condition - factors which have an important bearing on questions as to the
measure and character of the supply of labour.
The collection of data in respect of persons temporarily out of work is only a by~
product of the census enquiry and the manner in which statistics are influenced is indi-
cated in the various tabulations.
The necessity for inclusion of this group in the
active population will be appreciated.
"Unpaid Family Workers" are also included in the active population and in Table '23
appear under the heading ^HJnpaid Assistants" but women engaged in household duties in
their own homes and retired persons not gainfully occupied have been excluded.
As will be seeii from the volumes "Classification of Occupations" and "Classifica-
tion of Industries" referred to in Part II of this Report the separate terms under
which workers are classified run into many thousands.
This has necessitated modifica-
tions to bring census data into manageable dimensions, and thus generally speaking the
arrangement of headings is according to the class of materials worked with, commodities
produced or services rendered.
Special attention is drawn to the following changes since the 1926 census which
was the last occasion on which occupational and industrial enquiries were included in
the Northern Ireland Census Return:-
(82287)

The figures in Table XX include persons temporarily out of work.
Between 1926 and

    and 1951, the total population aged 14 and over increased by 97,70?, but the occupied
population increased by only 152,860.
There appear to be two main contributory

    factors - (a) while the number of women between 25 and 64 years increased by 43,006 the
number of occupied rose by only 4,134, but during the same period the number of married
women between those ages increased by 51,465; and (b) the number of persons of both
sexes aged 55 and over increased during the twenty-five years by 33,177, but there were
10, 354 fewer occupied.
This latter decrease is doubtless due to the introduction of
contributory pensions schones.
The number of occupied males aged 65 and over expressed

    as a percentage of the total of 65 and upwards dropped from 67.9 in 1926 to 39.4 in 1951.
The percentage figures for females are 15. 7 and 7.5.
Table 22 shows that males of all
ages retired from previous gainful occupations numbered 42,116 in 1951 as compared with
20,281 in 1926.
The figures for females are 12,603 and 9,727.

    Snployment for both boys and girls aged 14-19 was higher in 1951 than in 1926.
There was a decrease of 5,096 in the total number of boys of those ages during the
period, but the number of occupied fell by only 240, while in the case of girls the

    total number decreased by 8,611 but the number occupied rose by 3,'275.
The number of occupied males between '25 and 64 almost kept pace with the increase
in the total number in that group.
The numbers of persons returned as out-of-work tin 1926 were 46,668 males and

    17,475 females whereas in 1951 the figures were 29,998 males and 9,826 females.
Table XXI gives percentages of occupied to the total population including
children under 14 years of age.

## Page 157

xlvii
The occupied males and females, as perdentages of the total population of both
sexes, increased between 1911 and 1926 in Northern Ireland as a whole and in each of
the counties, except males in Londonderry (County and County Borough combined) and
Tyrone and females in Tyrone, in which areas there were small decreases.
In 1926, of
the total population, 45.4' per cent were stated to be occupied, 31.1 per cent being
males and 14.3 per cent females.
Compared with this, however, between 1926 and 1951
Northern Ireland as a whole showed a decrease for both sexes.
In 1951 44.0 per cent
of the total population were occupied of which 30.7 per cent were males and 13.3 per
cent females.
Slight increases were recorded in males in Antrim and Belfast and
decreases
in all other counties
and decreases
in females in all
areas.
Occupations - Ordersf: A complete list of occupations of persons aged 14 years and
over is given in Table 22 and Table XXII shows the proportions per 1,000 of the occupied
males and females under the numerically important Orders and also for numerically
important single or grouped occupations; these tables cover all Northern Ireland.
Of the males approximately 35 per cent were to be found under two Orders: II,
Agricultural Occupations; VI^ Workers in Metal, Engineering and Allied Trades.
It
should be noted that Order VI excludes (almost entirely) labourers and other unskilled
workers.
A further '27 per cent were attached to Order XVII, Transport (9 per cent).
Order XVIII, Commerce, Finance, etc. (9 per cent), and Order XXVI, Unskilled Occupations
(9 per cent).
About 67 per cent of the occupied females were comprised of four Orders: > VII,
Textile Workers (18 per cent); IX, Makers of Textile Goods and Articles of Dress
(17 per cent); XXII, Persons engaged in Personal Service (18 per cent); XXIII, Clerks,
Typists, etc. (14 per cent).
A further 9 per cent were occupied in Commerce, Finance,
etc. (Order XVIII).
The wide-scale changes since 1926 in the classification of occupations and
especially the introduction of Order XXVI bringing together groups of unskilled workers
not provided for elsewhere (formerly included in various Orders) make it impracticable
to give a complete comparison between the 1926 and 1951 figures.
Where, however, such
comparison is possible the figures for 1926 and 1951 are shown in Table XXIII.
The Agricultural Occupations, although they give employment to mof^e persons than
any other group, show a very marked change since 1926.
In that year there were nearly

    150,000 persons working on the land against 105,000 in 1951 - 343 males per 1,000
    occupied males and 88 females per 1,000 occupied females were in this category in 1926
    and only 236. and 32 respectively in 1951o
Every county recorded a decrease in the
'total number of agricultural workers since 1926; all counties showed an increase in
population during the 1926-1951 intercensal period with the exception of Fermanagh and
Tyrone and although there was a population decrease in these areas the decrease in the
number of agricultural workers was proportionally much greater.
On the other hand, Table XXIII shows that considerable expansion took place in
occupations linked with Building and Contracting, Transport, the Professions, etc.
Many factors appear to have had a bearing on the changes recorded in agricultural
occupations and some of these may be cited as:- (a) an increase from 83 to 568 persons
engaged on agricultural machinery, tractors, etc., (b) an increase of about 50 per cent
in the proportion of larger farms to the total number of farms; in 1926 about 14.4 per
cent of the holdings in Northern Ireland were above 50 acres whereas in 1951 the per-
centage was 21.7; the larger farms are more economically worked as to labour, being
more suitable for mechanisation.
(It will be noted from Table XXV that Northern Ireland
has still the lowest percentage of larger farms of the countries comprising the United
Kingdom and the Irish Republic - nevertheless the increase during the intercensal period
was marked).
As a percentage of all persons engaged in agricultural occupations,

    farmers (including farm managers) accounted for 50.9 in 1951 whereas in 1926. the figure
was 45.6 - with this should be mentioned that there was a decrease from 28.5 per cent
to 20.1 per cent in farmers' relatives helping (unpaid) in farm work and an increase in
the percentage of all other agricultural workers from 26.0 to 29.0.
Tables XXIV and XXV provide in some detail figures relating to farmers, unpaid
assistants and other workers.
(82287)

## Page 159

xlix
The extent to which females participate in agricultural occupations varies in the
several countries of the British Isles.
In Northern Ireland the number of females thus
engaged was equivalent to 6%0 per cent of the number of males compared with 11.9 per
cent in 1926.

    For the Irish Republic the figure was 15.9 per cent in 1946 and 22.2 per
cent in 1926, while in Great Britain the 1951 percentages were 9.5 for England and Wales
and 9.6 for Scotland - the corresponding percentages in 1921 were 7.1 for England and
Wales 14. 3 for Scotland.
It will be seen, therefore, that of these countries England
and Wales is the only one to show an increase in female activity in agriculture - in
all the other areas the departure of female labour from this field of occupation is
appreciable.
In Northern Ireland occupied females in all occupations represented 45.4' per cent

    of occupied males in 1951 and in 1926- the percentage was 46.2.
From this it is clear
that the widening in the disparity between occupied males and females is particularly
marked in respect of agricultural occupations.
It will be seen also from Table XXIII that since 1926 notable increases in the
number of persons employed took place in occupations connected with quarrying, electri-
cal work (including manufactures), manufacture of foods, drinks and tobacco, and, as
already mentioned, building and contracting, transport and professional and technical
work.
On the other hand the number of skilled forge workers, weavers, and domestic
servants (indoor) decreased considerably.
Table XXVI was prepared from the figures given in Table 24 and it will be seen
that among males under 25 years of age the highest proportions were to be found
(excluding persons employed in the Defence Services) under Textile Workers and Workers
in Wood, Cane and Cork.
Although, as already pointed out, the 1926- Orders were not on
exactly the same lines as those for 1951, nevertheless the age structures related to
both sets of figures and Orders may be compared with reasonable safety in a general way:
in 1926. in the under 25 years group the highest proportions were found among Textile
Workers and Clerks and Draughtsmen.
In the 25-44 years group the highest figures for
1951 were in the Professional and Technical Occupations and Transport.
The highest
proportion of workers aged 45 and over were in Agricultural Occupations - a similar

    position obtained in 1926: 44- per cent were in this age group in 1951 and 45 per cent
in the earlier census.
Among females the greatest proportion of all the age groups in 1951 was in the
group under 25 years in Order XXIII covering Clerical, Typing, etc. and the next highest
was in the same age group in Order IX which comprises Makers of Textile Goods and
Articles of Dress; in 1926- the highest figures for all age groups were found in the
under 25 years of age category and in the groups (l) Warehousemen, Storekeepers,
Packers, (2) Makers of Foods, Drinks, and Tobacco, and (3) Clerks, (not Civil Service
or Local Authority) Typists, etc.
In the 25-44' years range women in the Professional
and Technical Occupations had the highest proportion in 1951 whereas in 1926- Makers of
Textile Goods and Articles of Dress had the highest place.
Table 24 gives the distribution of the occupied male and female populations by
marital condition and 14' age groups and according to occupational Orders and, in
selected cases, Sub-Orders.
Single males aged 35 and over employed in agricultural occupations formed 36 per
cent of total males in that age group in the agricultural industry which is 1 per cent
higher than in 1926^
The percentages of single men of 35 and upwards in the other
large occupational groups are - Unskilled Workers (XXVI) 17: Commercial, Finance and

    Insurance (XVIII) 15: Transport (XXII) 12, and Metal Workers (VI) 11.
One third of the women of all ages recorded as Textile Workers (VII) are married.
The percentage figures of married women to all women for the other groups in which
female labour is employed in large num.bers are - Personal Service (XXII) 23, and
Makers of Textile Goods (IX) 22.
Occupations » Industrial Status: Table 23 gives occupations (by Orders and
selective groupings of Sub-Orders) by Industrial Status and Table XXVII provides a
comparison of the 1951 percentages with those of 1926.
(82287)

    • In bringing the 1926 figures Into line with those for 1951 as to age, the 129 males and
    79 females In age group 12-13 In 1926 recorded as * occupied* have been regarded as
employees.
The most significant change since the 1926 Census is the decrease in the propor-
tion of employers.
The number of employers in all comparable Orders save Professional
and Technical Occupations (XIX) Entertainments and Sport (XXI) and Warehousemen (XXIV),
fell between 1926 and 1951, but the most noticeable fall was in Agriculture.
In 1926.,
35,119 persons were returned as employers of agricultural labour: in 1951 the figure
was 4,403, a decrease of 87 per cent, while the number of persons returned as working
on own account in agriculture and analogous occupations rose by 48 per cent.
In 1926 of the total male population aged 14' and over 9.2 per cent were employers
(this represents 10.3 per cent of the occupied males) whereas in 1951 only 2.6 per cent
of the male population in that age group were employers (or 3.1 per cent of the occupied
males).
On the other hand threre was a marked increase in the proportion of employees,
persons working on own account, and retired persons (in 1926 there were 20,281 males
recorded as retired and in 1951 there were 42,116).
Somewhat similar trends took
place in the case of females except in respect of workers on own account of which there

    was a decrease in the proportion between 19'26 and 1951.
(82287)


---

## Page 161

li
The one per cent sample tables for Great Britain for 1951 Census show that of the
total number of persons aged 15 years and upwards 2.3 per cent were employers which
represents 2.6 of the occupied persons.

    Table 26. shows status aggregates for males aged 60-6& and females aged 55-64.
Social Classes: As this is the first occasion on which Social Class distribution
has been used in a Northern Ireland census report, particular attention is directed to
the explanation in Part I.
Table '22 shows the allocation of each occupational unit to the appropriate Social
Class.
Table 25 was prepared from Table 22, together with the appropriate informa-
tion relating to retired males, who were allocated Social Class gradings according to
their former occupations.

    Table 25 is on the same lines as Table 11.10 of the One
per cent Sample Tables for Great Britain on the 1951 census, but is restricted to
males.
A comparison of the two tables shows that Social Class II comprises a higher
proportion of the total in Northern Ireland than in Great Britain.
This Class
includes persons returned as Farmers, Farm Managers, etc., irrespective of the area of
the farms, and the figures for Northern Ireland reflect the very large part played by
agriculture in the economy as well as the emphasis on the small farm.
Industries; Table 27 provides a full list of industries as classified in the
1951 Census Volume "Classification of Industries"' already referred to, and for refer-
ence, the "Standard Industrial Classification"^ is also shown.
Table XXVIII shows the
distribution for 1951 according to certain main industrial groupings.
The 1951 and
1926 figures are not strictly comparable as in the earlier census the figures for the
occupied population include persons out of work, whereas the 1951 census figures
exclude these groups in accordance with the practice adopted throughout the United
Kingdom.

The Manufacturing Industries (used in a wide sense) gave emplojniient to 42.8 per
cent of the occupied population and Agriculture and Fishing 17.5 per cent.
Comparing
these figures with those for 1926 on broad lines, that is disregarding the element- of
unemployment, there was an increase in the former group of industries and a decrease in
the latter group during the 25 years: in 1926. the Manufacturing Industries accounted
for 38.8 per cent and Agriculture and Fishing for 26*0 per cent.
During the same period males engaged in Agriculture and Fishing decreased as a
percentage of the occupied male population from 34'. 0 to 23.8 and the female population
from 8.9 to 3.5; on the other hand in the Manufacturing Industries males increased

    from 32.5 to 39.3 per cent but females decreased from 52.5 to 50.7 per cent.
(82287)


---

## Page 162

Hi
In the Distributive Trades and Finance group the percentage for males remained at

    13.0 as in 1926 but the female figure increased from 10.6 to 14.3.
In the 1951 figures it will be seen that males outnumbered females in all groups
except Professional Services and Miscellaneous Services.
In the 1926- General Report it was stated that of the occupied population of the
six counties, excluding Belfast County Borough, 40 per cent were engaged in the
Agricultural Industry, while in Belfast 58 per cent were engaged in Manufacturing
Industries.
Again, disregarding the *'out of work"< element, in 1951 the corresponding
percentages were 27 for the counties and 56 for Belfast; to assess the change in the
counties, however, it should be pointed out that the percentage in the Manufacturing

    Industries in the counties increased from 28 in 1926 to 35 in 1951.
Industries » Age and Marital Condition; Further tabulations under industrial
orders and sub-orders are given in Table 28 which shows the distribution according to
marital condition and 14 age groups.
(82287)

## Page 242

19
BELFAST AND ENVIRONS
As may have been inferred from the earlier parts of this report, Belfast County
Borough can justifiably be regarded as the central portion of a continuous and much
greater urban area incorporating sections of several rural administrative areas.
In
this respect, however, it is unlike the area.s in Great Britain designated "conurbations"
and comprised of groups of complete local authority areas.
Belfast County Borough showed an increase in population of 5,585 between the 1937

    and 1951 censuses as compared with 22,935 in the eleven years 1926-1937 and 28,204 in
the fifteen years 1911-1926.
Eight of the fifteen wards showed a decrease in popula-
tion and the development of the surrounding areas appears to have been due to the
natural growth which could not be accommodated in the legally defined County Borough and
to a certain extent a drift from the city.
The key to the map given as an appendix to this report provides a complete list of
the townlands comprising the built-up localities around Belfast - rural in administra-
tion but urban in character: the whole area (Belfast and Environs) is the subject of
the following tables, humbered 29 to 34*.
In restricting the tabulations to the strictly built-up areas it was necessary to
extract the information by hand directly from the household returns; if administrative
districts with legally defined boundaries had been selected large purely rural areas
would have been included.
It was appreciated that the inclusion of whole rural
districts would not affect the general picture materially - and this course was adopted
in order not to delay the issue of the first Preliminary Report.
As, however, the
Belfast area is the only extensive urbanised area in Northern Ireland and the returns
were numerically manageable the more selective area was adopted for this report.
The tabulations relate to population, private houses, private households, density
of occupation, age distribution, marital condition, and religion.
The definitions, etc., given in Part II of this report apply equally to these
notes and tables..
AREA
The total area of Belfast and Environs is 27,943 acres or approximately 43^ square
miles, of which 55 per cent is within the Belfast County Borough boundary.
POPULATION
Table 29 shows that the population of this whole area increased between 1937 and

    1951 by 35,744'whereas the County Borough portion increased by only 5,585.
In the
constituent parts of the environs the County Antrim portion increased by 18,256 or

    103 per cent and the County Down portion by 11,903 or 181 per cent.
Of the four rural
districts affected the relative increase was greatest in the part of Castlereagh
included in the environs at 214 per cent, then came the area in Belfast R.D. at 110 per
cent increase; the Lisburn R.D. portion of the environs increased by 90 per cent and
the part in Hillsborough R.D. by 48 per cent.

    During the half century 1901 to 1951 the County Borough increased by 94,491 or
    27 per cent and the environs by 43,490 (estimated) or 398 per cent.
In the case of

    the County Borough 70 per cent of the increase took place in the period 1901-1926 while
    in the case of the environs 70 per cent of the increase occurred during the 1937-1951
period.
County Antrim section increased to 35,945 which is nearly four times the

    1901 population (9,065) and the County Down section to 18,479 which is nearly ten
times as great as the population in 1901 (l,869).
PRIVATE HOUSES. ROOMS. HOUSEHOLDS
Table 30 deals with Inhabited private houses, population therein and rooms
occupied, and Table 31 gives the composition of private households and the density of
room occupation, for Belfast C.B., and each of the rural areas comprising the environs.
Private houses of one to three rooms formed 12.1 per cent of the total in the County
Borough whereas in the environs area this size house accounted for 9.8 per cent
(82887)


---

## Page 243

80
(combined areas 11.8).
The 4 to 5 roomed house in the Coiinty Borough represented

    68,6 per cent and the 6 to 8 roomed house 17.3 per cent against 68.9 and 17.4 per cent
respectively for the amalgamated areas.
The total number of occupied private houses
in Belfast C.B. was 107,358 and the average number of persons per house was 3.99; in
the whole area - city and environs - there were 121,148 houses with an average
occupation of 3.97 persons per house; in the environs alone there were 13,790 of these
houses and an average of 3.79 persons per house which is lower than the average for any
county.
Of the total number of houses in the course of erection, viz., 2,008, there were
945 in the County Borough and 1,063 in the surrounding districts.
Castlereagh had the
greatest number (593) under construction in the rural areas and Belfast R.D. came next
(385).
In the city 3,673 houses (3.4' per cent) were shared by two or more iauseholds
whereas in the environs only 122 houses (0.9 per cent) were shared.
The number of rooms in inhabited private houses in Belfast C.B. was 499,450 which
is equivalent to 0.86 persons per room; in the environs there were 66,541 such rooms
with 0. 79 persons per room.
In the rural areas the persons per room figures varied
between 0.68 in Hillsborough R.D. and 0.82 in Belfast R.D.
Table 30 shows that for the whole area there were 126,516 households occupying

    121,148 houses - an ostensible shortage of 5,368 houses, but from Table 31 it is seen
that there were 1, 930 people -^ probably lodgers for the most part - living alone in
one or two rooms and it is a safe assumption that many of these persons would not wish
to occupy a separate house if such were available.
The deduction of this figure of
1,930 from the overplus of 5,368 households sharing houses gives a net figure of
3,438 houses apparently required to avoid sharing in 1951.
On census night
2,008 houses were in course of erection which is 1,430 below the required figure for
the whole area to provide a separate house for each household on census night.
New
housing schemes have been developed in the area since the census was taken in April
1951 which may have more than made up the required number, but as the population of a
highly industrialised area is never static the balance which existed between house-
holds and houses in 1951 may have altered considerably since then.
These figures do not take into account overcrowding * 41,061 people in the
combined area were living at room densities above 2 persons per room: or sharing of
houses not disclosed to the census enumerators, e.g., married couples living with
relatives.
It is desired to call attention to the note in the Housing Section of this report
regarding temporary structures used as dwelling houses.
From general observation, it
is felt that the remarks have particular significance to some portions of the area of
Belfast and Environs.
The room density of the people residing in the environs portion of the area shows
a distinct improvement over that for the County Borough, but the development was not
sufficient in 1951 to affect materially the figures as a whole.
The numbers of
houses and households in the environs area form 11 per cent of the total figures. The
following statement shows the position in regard to the whole area, Belfast County
Borough and the environs portion.

## Page 244

81
SEX, AGE AND MARITAL CONDITION
Table 32 gives the aggregate of the populations of the Belfast County Borough and
Environs according to sex, age (individual years and quinquennial groups) and marital
condition.
In the Belfast County Borough volume it was noted that boys were numerically
superior to girls at every year up to and including the age of 17 years.
The same
holds good for the combined figures for Belfast and Environs except that at age 16
there were exactly the same number of boys as girls.
Also, as in Belfast, females
outnumbered males at all ages from 18 years and upwards.
The average age for Belfast itself was shown to be 32.5 years; the average for
the environs was 29.7 years.
As stated earlier in these notes the average for all

    Northern Ireland was 32.5 in 1951 which was the highest of a steady increase since 1881.
Londonderry County (including C.B.) was the lowest in 1951 for the Counties being 31.0.
The low figure of 29.7 for Belfast Environs therefore is not only below the lowest for
the counties but is lower than that of any County area since 1911 except Belfast C.B.

    which registered 26.7 in 1911 and 28.5 in 1926.
The inference is that the population
of the developed areas suirounding Belfast is comprised mostly of young married couples
and their children.
This inference is supported by the fact that although the popula-
tion of the environs represents 12.3 per cent of that of Belfast, the number of children
under 1 year of age in the environs forms 17.6 per cent of children of the same age in
Belfast: children 0-4 years represent 17.8 per cent and the total children under
10 years 15.6 per cent of the City's children of the same ages.
It may also be pointed
out that married persons under 25 years of age in the extension area amounted to 12.9

    per cent of those in Belfast, those aged 25 to 44 amounted to 16.9 per cent and the
total number of married persons of all ages in the former area was equal to 13.7 per
cent of the city proper.
Widowed and divorced persons in the environs represented 8.1 per cent of the
similar category in the County Boroughs
RELIGIONS
Table 33 gives the population by sex for the County Borough and each of the rural
areas forming the environs, under five religious descriptions, viz., Roman Catholic,
Presbyterian, Church of Ireland, Methodist, and all other (and not stated)
denominations.
A list is also given of denominations in the last category each of which numbered
10 or more adherents, but this list does not give the break down of the environs into
constituent parts.
Table 34 gives the population under the five categories stated above according to
ages - individual years'xinder 22 years of age and by quinquennial groups from age 20
upwards - this information is given for Belfast C«B., County Antrim portion of environs.
County Down portion, and the aggregate of all areas.
The following statement shows how the religious groups are distributed in the
various areas - the figures represent percentages of the whole population of each area.

## Page 245

82
The Church of Ireland is the only denomination that shows the same percentage for
the aggregate of the areas as for Belfast C.B. : Roman Catholic shows a decrease and
Presbyterian an increase of 0.5 per cent for the aggregate compared with their respec-
tive figures for the County Borough; likewise Methodist shows a decrease and "Other
Denominations" an increase of 0.1 per cent.
The Area in Belfast R.D. is the only one in which the Roman Catholics are propor«-
tionally the greatest, accounting for 36.5 per cent of the population. Presbyterians
are numerically superior in all other regions except Lisburn in which the Church of
Ireland predominates.
In County Antrim Environs, Roman Catholics, Presbyterians, and "Other
Denominations" exceed their Belfast C.B. percentages and in County Down Environs all
groups, except Roman Catholics exceed their Belfast C.B. proportions.
In rural areas Presbyterians have the highest percentage (40.8) in Castlereagh,
and Church of Ireland have the next highest (39.9) in Lisburn.
The following statement shows on a percentage basis the distribution of the
population according to two religious categories - Roman Catholic and Other Denomina-
tions - by selected age groups in areas, Belfast C.B. , Co. Antrim Environs, Co. Down

From the above figures it will be seen that for each part of the whole urbanised
area Roman Catholics have their greatest percentage of adherents in the 0-4 years
group and the next greatest ratio obtains in respect of the 5-9 years group except in
Co. Down.
On the other hand for each area, without exception, "Other Denominations"
have their lowest percentages in the 0-4 category and except for Co. Down the next
lowest are in the 5-9 group.
The highest percentages for "Other Denominations" are
in the 20 years and over class whereas Roman Catholic percentages are lowest in it.
It may be inferred from the figures for the new built-up areas that the
Co. Antrim side attracted a settlement of Roman Catholics, the proportion of which
exceeds that for Belfast C.B. for that denomination but on the Co. Down side more
than 90 per cent of the population are under "Other Denominations".

CENSUS, NORTHERN IRELAND, 1951
HOUSEHOLD RETURN
!
Form issued by the Registrar-General pursuant'to the
Census Act, 1951.
' j
To be filled up, as applicable, by the Enumerator.
County or County Borough
County District (Urban or Rural)
Registrar's District
(in Co. Boro's, Newry U.D.
and Kilkeel U.D. only)
Townland
Town or Village
Street, etc., with No.
or Name of House

of Household

Parly. Divn. (in C.B.'s. only)
NOTICE
1.
This Return must be filled up and signed by the Head of the Household,
or in his absence by the person in charge of the dwelling. If unable to prepare
the Return himself, the head of the household may allow another person to do so
under his instructions and in his presence.
Lseholds or persons
, fill up a separate
(»t to be considered
3h they board.
ging Houses and
|n charge must fill
r Census purposes
2.
If a house or part of a house be let or sublet to separate ho
then each ,occupier (Head of Household or Lodger) mus
Return applicable to his part of the house. Boarders are m
as separate occupiers but as part of the household with wh:
3.
In the case of Hotels, Boarding Houses, Clubs, Lo<
other similar establishments, the Manager or other person
up a Return in respect of all persons who are to be regarded
as inmates.
|
4.
Each person in a household or establishment is required by law to give to
the head of the household, or the manager of the establishment, such information
as may be necessary to enable the Return to be filled up. But should a person
object to giving the information in this way, he may fill in the particulars on a
separate Return, and hand it under cover to the head of the household (or mana-
ger of the establishment) for delivery to the enumerator. In such a case
the person responsible for making the Return should complepe columns (A) and
(B) only in respect of the person making a separate Return, j Forms for making
separate Returns will be furnished upon claim being made prior to the census
date on a form to be obtained from the enumerator or from the nearest Police
Station.
5. All particulars given in this Return will foe regarded as strictly con-
fidential.
6. This Return must be ready on MONDAY, 9th APRILJ.
Such questions
as may be necessary will be asked by the enumerator to enatjle him to complete
or correct this Return and to compile a Return relating to hcjusing. If the head
of a household, or the manager of an establishment, be unable to deliver the
Return personally to the enumerator he may instruct another person to do so.
7. If any person whose duty it is to give information refuses to do so, or wil-
fully gives false information, or uses otherwise than for the [purpose of making
the Return any information given him for that purpose, lie will be liable on
conviction to a fine not exceeding TEN POUNDS.
W. A. CARSON,
Registrar-General.

Instructions for filling up Various Columns
RELAlSONSHIP-^COLUMJNf B
U A relative visiting the family (#.g., a son o i a visit tdjhift lather's house) should be
described as "VisitorM. A person residing at a hotel, efcq., should be described as
"Resident" or "Boarder".
U S U A L RSSIDENCB-COLUMpf 0
2. For Persons from abroad,
unless they have a sett^d residence in this country
(e.g.; students), give the full postal address in the country where they usually
reside,
3. Hotels, Boarding-houses, etc. Persons enumerate^ at hotels, boarding-houses,
hostels, etc., at which they have a settled residences without a more usual place of
residence elsewhere, should be entered a$ "Here".
4. Resident Domestic Servants, Shop Assistants, etc., who live in should regard the
private house, boarding-house, etc., where they are employed, or the premises where
they live in, as their usual residence. If enumerated aZ their living-in premises,
write "Here". If enumerated elspwhere, give the address of their living-in premises
as their usual residence.
5* Scholars, Students, Undergraduates, €|tc.t of a resid€«itiftl school, college or uni-
versity, if enumerated at home, should give the address of the boarding school br
college at which they usually reside during term.
University students who reside
during term in lodgings in the university town should rigard such lodgings as their
usual residence.
6. In the case of Members of II.M. Forces on leave, iive the name and address
of their barracks, quarters, station or shore establishment.
EDUCATION—COLUMN K
.7. Full-time at an educational establishment means such time as leavest no reasonable
opportunity for substantial regular employment in term-time.
OCCUPATION A N D INDUSTRY-COLUMNS M A N D N
8. Personal Occupation.
An entry must be made in Column M for everyone aged 14 or
over. The occupation should be stated in bolumn M whether the person is at work
or unemployed at the time of the Census. If the job is known in the trade or industry
by a special name, use that name. For managers, foremen, etc., state the approp-
riate branch or department, e.g., "Sales Manager", "Hardware Departmental Man-
ager", "Foreman Baker", "Overlooker, Spinning Room 0.
9. Retired.
For a person retired from an occupation and not following any regular
work, state the former occupation, addink""Retired,, even if occasional jobs are
undertaken. -"But if he has taken up regidar work again his present occupation
should be stated and he should not be described as "Retired".
10. Out of Work.
State usual occupation and industry in Columns M and N even if a
long time unemployed, adding "Out of Work" at (b) in Oolomn M, For a person who
has no prospect of returning to former occupation and is seeking another, and for a
young person who is seeking work but has never had a job, write "None" at (a), and
"Out of Work" at (b) of Column M.
11. Part-time refers to paid employment normally occupying less than 30 hours a week.
12. Unpaid Helpers in a Family Business (including Farming).
For a person chiefly
engaged in helping in the family business or farm of the head of the household (or
other relative) without receiving a definite wage or share in the profits, state the
occupation in Column M adding "Unpaid", and in Column N treat the head of the
business or farm as employer. If helping in private domestic duties and unpaid,
no entry need be made in Column N.
13. Home Duties.
This term refers to unpaid domestic duties—leave Column N blank
in such cases.
14. Other Persons not occupied for payment or profit.
For persons permanently
incapacitated by illness, etc., who have never followed any Paid occupation, write
"None" in Column M and leave Column N blank.
15. Descriptions should not be vague and indefinite.
The following, for example,
are too vague :—Operative, Polisher, Technician, Collector, Inspector, Mill-hand,
Labourer, Contractor, Scientist, Overlooker, Checker, Assistant.
Labourers.
Do not use term "Labourer" alone. State "Dock Labourer"* "Brick-
layer's Labourer", etc., or if working at various jobs write "General Labourer".
See also "Farm Workers" below, (See examples 6-7 below.)
Machine Operators. Always state the kind of machine, giving its recognised name,
if any.
Farm Workers.
For a person following a definite occupation on a farm, such as
Cowman, Shepherd, Carter, Dairymaid or General Farm Worker, state occupation
irrespective of whether or not he or she is a relative of the farmer and whether paid
or unpaid. For a relative assisting in. the general work or management of the farm,
write "Assisting Father (or Brother, etc.) in general farm work".
(See examples
1—2 below.)
Shopkeepers, Retail Dealers, Shop Assistants.
If wholly or mainly engaged in
selling, add "Dealer" (if Principal) or "Shop Assistant" or "Salesman" (if assisting),
and^tate whether the business is wholesale or retail (or both). If mainly making,
though also selling, add "Maker". For shop assistants and salesmen in Co-opera-
tive Stores and Multiple or Departmental Shops, state the particular Department
in which engaged. (See example 14.)
Navy, Army, Air Force.
For members of H.M. Forces on leave, state rank in
Column M and the arm of the Service in Column N, adding (N.S.) at (b), in Column
M if National Serviceman.
Civil Service.
For civil servants, state rank and Department (also Branch) i n
which serving.
Domestic Servants. If in private service, state occupation (e.g., "Cook") in Column
M, but write only "Private" in Column N. Complete Columns M and N if engaged
in Hotels, etc.
16. Employer and Employer's Business.
The particulars about employers are
required solely to assist classification of the industry or service.
No personal
information of any kind about individuals will be published or disclosed.
Describe the Business fully : the following terms are insufficient by themselves :—
Manufacturer, Merchant, Agent, Dealer, Engineering, Iron Works.

## Page 270

iii
PREFACE
Three censuses of population have been taken since the Government of Northern
Ireland was estaolished.
The first enumeration took place in 1926 and incorporated
questions relating to occupation and industry, orphanhood and infirmities.
The second
enumeration made in 1937 was of more limited scope and was intended to bridge the gap
between the census of 1926 and the census which it was proposed to take in 1941, but
which had to be abandoned owing to the outbreak of war.
The census taken as at mid-
night of 8th-9th April, 1951, forms the basis of this report and like that in 1926
questions were asked as to the occupations and industries of the population.
The length of time required to process the data collected at an enumeration before
it can be presented in the ultimate reports is necessarily considerable.
In order to
meet immediate requirements, however, two Preliminary Reports on the 1951 census were
published.
The first of these gave the population figures by administrative areas and
towns and villages, and by Counties and County Boroughs according to religious profes-
sion.
The Second Report, which was restricted to Counties and County Boroughs, gave
the population by age groups.
The present Volume is the fourth of seven county volumes (the Belfast County
Borough and Counties of Antrim and Down volumes having been published already) and has
been prepared (as were also the Preliminary Reports) in accordance with the provisions
of the Census Act (Northern Ireland) 1951o
The statistics now published supersede
those shown in the Preliminary Reports and may be accepted as final.
This volume is consistent in design and scope with the previous volumes and the
remaining County volumes which will be published at intervals as completed will be pre-
pared in like manner.
A General Report for the whole of Northern Ireland will follow the county volumeSo
This will bring together, and in some respects show in greater detail, the information
contained in the county volumeSo
Additional features will also be introduced particu-
larly in regard to the distribution of the people industrially by age and marital
condition.
Following the 1926 census a topographical index was published dealing with each
type of area for which statistics were supplied in the county series.
A supplement to
this index was published after the 1937 census and a further supplement in respect of
the 1951 census will be prepared in due course.
Census publications are obtainable either directly from H.M. Stationery Office,
Chichester Street, Belfast, or from any oook-seller.
L. C. MULLIGAN,
REGISTRAR-GENERAL
General Register Office,
Fermanagh House,
Belfast.
April, 1954o
(14667)

## Page 273

PART I
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME
AREA
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey (foreshore being
excluded), and relate to the several administrative areas as constituted at the date of
the 1951 census.
The acreage covered Qy the larger rivers, lakes and tideways is not
included in the body of Tables 4 and 5 but is given in footnotes thereto.
POPULATION
The population comprises all persons on land, in vessels on inland waters or in
port or at anchorage on census night, and also persons on coastwise voyages who arrived
in port within a brief period not having been enumerated elsewhere.
Members of the
Forces of the Crown are also included.
ADMINISTRATIVE DIVISIONS
The County is divided into seven county districts (one municipal borough, two urban
districts and four rural districts) comprising 86 district electoral divisions, three of
which are coterminous with the municipal borough and the two urban districts (see
table 4).
The County Borough is divided into three wards, each of which is coterminous
with a district electoral division (see table 4).
The district electoral division forms
the unit for the purpose of municipal elections.
The County is also divided into nine-
teen county electoral divisions, which are the units for the purpose of county council
elections (see table 5).
The County Borough is not divided into county electoral
divisions.
Owing to the abolition of the Boards of Guardians, details of the Poor Law Union
and Dispensary districts are not given in this volume.
These areas, however, form
respectively, superintendent registrars' and registrarsV districts for the registration
of marriages, births and deaths, and consequently the census tabulations have been pre-
pared in a manner suitable for the extraction of data according to these areas should
such be required.
There are five parliamentary constituencies for elections to the Northern Ireland
Parliament, and one for elections to the United Kingdom Parliament (see para, (b) below
and table 9).
The County and County Borough Education Areas are coterminous with the County and
County Borough respectively.
The changes made in the constitution or boundaries of administrative divisions of
the County and County Borough during the last intercensal period are as follows:-
(a) In 1938 the number of wards in Londonderry County Borough was reduced from
five to three.
(b) The creation by the Representation of the People Act 1948 of one parliamentary
constituency for the purpose of election to the Parliament of the United Kingdom
and comprising the County and County Borough excepting Magherafelt Rural District
which area under the same Act forms part of the Mid-Ulster constituency.
(14667)


---

## Page 274

HOUSES
1,
The term "House" means
(a) each structurally separate dwelling (except institutions) having distinct
access to the street, common stair, or landing.
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanitorium, nursing home, asylum, religious,
welfare or charitable community, residental school, barracks or other premises
under Naval, Military, Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other place of
detention), regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts.
(c) each boat, caravan, tent or other movable dwelling if occupied on census
night but not otherwise.
2.
The term "Private House" means a dwelling other than an institution, hotel, board-
ing house, premises occupied by a caretaker, vessel, movable dwelling, barn, stable
or outhouse occupied on census night.
The term includes premises comprising a shop
with dwelling attached, unless occupied by a caretaker.
3,
The term "Boarding House" and "Lodging House" cover habitations described as
such, and any private house containing three or more boarders or lodgers.
4.
The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons on census
night.
"Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those temporarily vacant through
the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Enumerators were
instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in such an advanced state of
decay that there was little likelihood that they would again be made fit for habitation.
ROOMS
1.
The rooms counted are the usual living rooms including bedrooms and kitchens but
excluding sculleries (i.e., compartments used only for washing, cleaning, or cook-
ing), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets, bathrooms, or any warehouse, office or
shop rooms.
2.
For the classification of households according to rooms occupied (see table 7),
shared rooms - i.e., those used in common by two or more households - have been
included in the number of rooms occupied by the main household in the dwelling.
In
cases of one room occupations, all persons living therein, whether related or not, have
been enumerated as one census household.
HOUSEHOLDS
1.
"N6n«private Households" comprise all persons enumerated in hotels, boarding
houses, lodging houses, shops and institutions (see para. l(b) xinder "Houses"
definition above).
2.
"Private Households" are comprised of single persons or groups of persons occupy-
ing separately the whole, or part of any private dwelling (see para. 2 under
"Houses" definition above).
Visitors and domestic servants are included in the house-
hold with which they were enumerated.
Lodgers having or sharing separate accommoda-
tion were enumerated on separate returns and consequently are regarded as separate
private households.
It should be specially noted that a household or private household for census pur-
poses includes any group of persons, whether related or not, who live together in
separate house or room accommodation.
RATEABLE VALUATION
The figures shown are those for the year ended 31st March, 1952, and have been
supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Subject to the provisions of the Local Government (Rating and Finance) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1929, agricultural land and agricultural buildings are completely
(14667)


---

## Page 275

derated and industrial hereditanients and freight-transport hereditaments are partially
derated, the consequent deficiency in the yield of rates being made good by way of
General Exchequer contributions.
The figures in Tables 4 and 5 are the totals of the valuations in the various
areas before reduction on account of derating, but exclude in certain cases the valua-
tions of electricity, gas and water mains, telegraphs, telephones, fisheries, water-
works and tramways.
The total of such excluded valuations for the county is given in
a foot-note to the table.
USUAL RESIDENCE
The persons who have been regarded as having their usual residence in the area of
enumeration (i.e., the County and County Borough) include:-
(1) Those enumerated in their own houses;
(2) Persons having settled residence in private lodgings;
(3) Resident domestic servants;
(4) Scholars, students, etc., living during term time in residential schools,
etc.;
(5) Inmates of institutions when the nature of the institution suggested
permanent residence;
(6) Crews of vessels if usually living on shipboard.
BIRTHPLACES
The birthplaces of a small number of persons were not stated on the census return;
it was assumed that these persons were born in the county or county borough of enupiera-
tion, except when they were stated to be visitors to the household, or bore foreign
names.
After further enquiry in the latter cases it was found possible to classify
them (with one exception) under definite headings.
RELIGIONS
The classi^fication of religions includes a separate heading for each religion, the
adherents of which number 10 or more (see Table 16).
The heading "Presbyterian"
includes those who call themselves by this name without qualification, together with
those who describe themselves as members of the Church of Scotland.
Other Presbyterian
denominations are shown at the foot of Table 16.
Members of the Church of England and
the Episcopal Church of Scotland have been included under the heading "Church of
Ireland".
The latter group includes all persons returned as "Church" or "Episcopalian".
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
There are two categories into which the population may be grouped In their work-
ing capacity, namely, occupation and industry.
The sole criterion for determining the
occupational classification is the type of work performed.
On the other hand, industry
has to do with the kind of business with which the occupation is associated.
Thus a
carpenter working for a firm of shipbuilders would be classified under "Carpenter" for
occupation but under "Shipbuilding" for industry.
The 1951 Censuses were carried out simultaneously in all parts of the British Isles
and this affor'ded the first opportunity for Northern Ireland to adopt both occupational-
and industrial classifications* on exactly the same lines as those used for'the rest of
the United Kingdom.
* Classification of Occupations, 1950,
H.M.S.0,
Price £1 10s. Od. net.
Census 1951, Classification of Industries.
H.M.S.O.
Price 8s. 6d. net.
(14667)


---

## Page 276

In the preparation of Tables 19 and 21 the range of Orders, Sub-Orders and
Occupational and Industrial unit groups have been modified by combining closely related
groups where the niombers were small and the preservation or usefulness of the data was
not harmed,
TABLES CONTAINING DOTS (•) AND DASHES (-)
Throughout this volume dots (• ) are used in tabular matter to indicate "None", and
dashes (-) are used to indicate that figures are not available or that the column head-
ings are not relevant to the lines in which the dashes appear.
CORRECTION OF CENSUS DATA
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and inac-
curacies from the census data.
The process of correction began with the enumerators,
who received full instructions as to thq action to be taken in cases of error or omis-
sion, and it was continued by a system of check and further enquiry where necessary
applied at each stage of the office work.
The process was completed by an intensive
scrutiny and inter-comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny and comparison
revealed a certain number of incongruities, which, when material, were investigated and
corrected in the light of the facts disclosed; trifling errors were adjusted
arbitrarily.


---

## Page 277

PART II
STATISTICAL NOTES
The definitions, etc,, given in the preceding section apply equally to these notes
and to the tables in Part III.
AREA
Descriptions and figures are given for areas as constituted at the date of the
1951 census unless otherwise stated.
It will be seen from Table 1 (Part III) that the total area of County and County
Borough exclusive of the larger rivers, lakes and tideways, is 804 square miles or
15.3 per cent of the area of Northern Ireland.
In 1915 the County lost about one-third
of a square mile to Antrim County (see note to table 2).
POPULATION
The total enumeration for the County and County Borough was 155, 540.
highest number recorded since 1881.
This is the
It will be seen from table 2 that censuses were taken decennially between 1821 and
1911 and thereafter at irregular intervals; this should be borne in mind when comparing
data of one enumeration with another.
From 1821 to 1841 each census registered an
increase in population, the population in the latter year being 222,461.
From 1841 to

    1926 every census registered a decline in population; in 1926 it stood at 139,693 the
lowest figure on record.
Since 1926, however, the population has been increasing, the

    rate of increase being much greater between 1937 and 1951 than in the 1926 - 1937 period.
Natural Increase: The following table sets out figures of the natural increase
and actual increase or decrease of population in each of the intercensal periods since
1871, and shows the net loss by migration.

## Page 278

Comparable figures of natural increase due to the excess of births over deaths,
in respect of the County and County Borough as now constituted, are not available for
periods prior to 1871, but it is evident that in each of the intercensal periods from

    1841 to 1871, as well as from 1871 to 1926, there was a net outward movement of such
dimensions as not only to counterbalance the natural increase, but to cause an actual
decrease in population.
During the 1926 - 1937 period, however, the net outward move-
ment dropped to 8,315 from 13,410 in the preceding intercensal period and although the

    latest period 1937 - 1951 shows an increase in population of 12,804 the outward movement
was as high as 11,460.

•f Estimated figure.
In regard to county dist^ricts it will be seen that all divisions show increases
during the fourteen years 1937 - 1951.
The most marked percentage increases are in
Portstewart Urban District (37.7 per cent or 976 persons) and Londonderry Rural District
(32.5 per cent or 5,160 persons).
The total increase in the population of municipal
boroughs and urban districts is 2,948 or 20.3 per cent and the overall increase in the
rural population is 7,, 577 or 9.4 per cent - the areas being the same for both censuses.
Scanning the whole course of fifty years (1901 - 1951) all urban and one rural
areas show an increase since the commencement of the period.
Coleraine Municipal
Borough is the only county district that shows an increase for every .intercensal period
since 1901.
The most marked increases are in Portstewart Urban District (2,629 or 281
per cent) Coleraine Municipal Borough (3,790 or 54.5 per cent) and Londonderry Rural
District 6,051 or 40.4 per cent).
Limavady Urban District had an increase of 484 or
18.0 per cent but the remaining county divisions, viz., the rural districts of
Coleraine, Limavady and Magherafelt all registered decreases during the half century.
The population of the County Borough increased during the period 1937 - 1951 by

    2,279 or 4.6 per cent a somewhat lower increase than that during the 1926 - 1937
    interval (2, 654 or 5, 9 per cent).
As already observed the constitution of the wards
was altered in 1938 but the areas each show an increase since 1937 as is the case also
between 1901 and 1951.
(14667)


---

## Page 279

Taking the County Districts and the County Borough together the 1951 urban popula-

    tion constitutes 43.4 per cent of the whole corapared with 43.7 per cent in 1937, 36.4
    per cent in 1911, and 34.3 per cent in 1901 - the percentages quoted relating to urban
areas as constituted at the date of each census.
Amongst the county urban areas population density is highest in Coleraine (10.7
persons per acre) and lowest in Portstewart (2.6 persons per acre).
In rural districts
the rates vary between 0.1 and 0.3 persons per acre.
For the whole County the rate is
0.2 persons per acre.
In the County Borough the rates vary between 14.4 (in Waterside
Ward) and 29.8 (South Ward).
Tables 4 and 5 provide detailed information regarding area, population, housing
and accommodation for the various sub-divisions of the County and County Borough and
incorporate figures of rateable valuation.
The rateable valuation of the County in

    1951 was £504,034 before reduction on account of derating and £262,103 after reduction
on account of derating, and for the County Borough £238,061 before, and £220,763 after
derating.
In 1937 the corresponding valuations of the County were £448,211 and

    £208,693 respectively and for the County Borough £203,496 before reduction and £188,444
after reduction.
Table 4 includes information regarding towns and villages containing 50 houses and
upwards with no legally defined boundaries.
Parliamentary Constituencies; Table 9 sets out the Parliamentary Constituencies of
the County and County Borough for" the Parli£iments of both the United Kingdom and
Northern Ireland together with the figures of population, number of electors and elec-
tors per 1,000 of the population, distinction being shown in respect of electors with
residence qualification.
Under the provisions of the Elections and Franchise Act (Northern Ireland), 1946,
a person is entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any constituency
returning a member to the Parliament of Northern Ireland if he or she is a British sub-
ject, 21 or more years of a^e, not subject to any legal incapacity, and was born in
Northern Ireland (or alternatively, has resided continuously in the United Kingdom for
a period of seven years ending on the qualifying date) and either:-
(a) has the requisite residence qualification of three months; or
(b) has occupied for three months prior to the qualifying date business premises
of at least £10 valuation; or
(c) is the wife or husband of a person entitled to be registered in respect of
the above business premises qualification; or
(d) was on the qualifying date a member of the forces or a merchant seaman and
who, but for such service, would have been residing in Northern Ireland.
The electorate in the Queen's University constituency comprises all graduates of
the University vdio are British subjects of full age, not subject to any legal incapacity.
Under the provisions of the Representation of the People Act (1949), a person is
entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any Northern Ireland constitu-
ency returning a member to the Parliament at Westminster if that person is a British
subject or citizen of the Irish Republic of twenty-one or more years of age and not
subject to any legal incapacity to vote, is resident at an address in that constituency
on the qualifying date, and has resided at that address (or at any other address or
addresses in Northern Ireland) for a period of three months ending on the qualifying
date.
In addition, a person who would ordinarily have been resident at a specified
address in a constituency in Northern Ireland, may be registered in respect of a Service
qualification if -
(i) he or she is a member of the Forces; or
(ii) he or she is a person employed in the service of the Crown in a post outside
the United Kingdom; or
(14667)


---

## Page 280

X 1 1 1
(iii) she is the wife of a person having a Service qualification and is residing
^outside the United Kingdom to be with her husband.
Education Areas; The Londonderry County Council and the Londonderry County Borough
Council are the Education Authorities for the whole areas under their control, and all
figures given in this Volume for the County and County Borough apply equally to the
respective Education Areas.
HOUSING - BUILDINGS, ROOMS, HOUSEHOLDS, ETC.
Houses: The total number of houses, including hotels, institutions, etc., in the
County and County Borough normally used for dwelling purposes was 35,898 of which 33,295
houses were occupied on Census night.
The number of occupied houses at the 1937 Census

    was 30,233, an increase therefore of 3,062, or 10.1 per cent under this category took
place in the intercensal period.
Table III includes all houses, private and non-
private.
The number of non-private houses was 338 of which 185 were boarding houses
or hotels.
The population figures do not include inmates of institutions, but they do
include other occupants such-as staff.

* The figures given are for the County and County Borough as constituted at the date of each census. A portion
of the county of Londonderry was transferred to the county of Antrim in 1915, consequent on the extension of
Portrush Urban District.
The total number of houses and the number of houses being built were the highest
figures recorded since 1851.
The n\mber of houses, being built (967) was more than six
times as great as the next highest census figure - that of 149 for the 1926 census.
Similar trends were observed in respect of Counties Antrim and Down.
The average number of persons (exclusive of inmates of institutions) per house,

    steadily decreased from 1851 (when it stood at 5.69) to 1911 (4.79) from which point it
fluctuated and now stands at 4.63 being the lowest since 1851.
Table IV shows the changes in the numbers of houses in each County district and
ward of the County Borough during the intercensal period 1937 - 1951.
The increase of
3,553 houses for the County and County Borough should be considered with the figures

    for uninhabited houses, which show a drop from 2,000 in 1937 to 1,63& in 1951.
A comprehensive investigation! for the County of Antrim report showed that of the
uninhabited houses about 42 per cent were temporarily unoccupied at the time of census,
39 per cent were summer residences, 15 per cent were affected by change of occupancy
(or new houses awaiting occupancy) and no information was available about the remaining
4 per cent.
A similar position was noted in respect of County Down.
As the percentage of
unoccupied houses in the County and County Borough of Londonderry is lower than in
either Antrim or Down it has not been considered necessary to make a detailed survey
of the reasons for vacancy on census night.
(14667)

* The figures given relate to each Ward as constituted at the date of the 1951 Census.
In each of the county urban areas there was an increase of not less than 20 per
cent between 1937 and 1951.
In the rural districts the greatest increase was in
Londonderry in which the increase was 19.7 per cent.
The only county division to show
a decrease was Magherafelt Rural District the decrease being by 0.5 per cent.
The
three wards of the County Borough record an increase, North W^rd by 11.0 per cent and
South Ward and Waterside Ward by 20.7 and 20.8 per cent respectively.
Between 1926 and 1937 the County Borough and all the urban areas showed an increase
whereas the only rural area registering an increase was Londonderry Rural District.
The increase for Londonderry County and County Borough was 6.7 per cent between 1926 and

    1937 and 11.0 per cent for the 1937 - 1951 interval.
INHABITED PRIVATE HOUSES
Number of Houses and Inhabitants: The following statement sets out the figures for
inhabited private houses, persons therein and average number per house for censuses,

    1926, 1937 and 1951, for the County and County Borough.
1926
27,671

The average annual increase in the number of inhabited private houses for the period

    1926 - 1937 was 185 and for the period 1937 - 1951 it was 232, but this must be viewed
in the light of increases in the population in these dwelling houses - the average annual
increase of population in the first period was only 308, whereas in the latter it was
873.
The relative changes are seen more clearly from the figures for "persons per

    house" the ratios having dropped for the period 1926 - 1937 by 0.21 ^ d for 1937 - 1951
by 0.08.
Thus the rate of improvement in the second period has been slower than in the
first period.

    Of the total of 32,957 inhabited private houses, 31,791 (96.5 per cent) were occu-
pied by single private households, the remainder of 1,166 were shared by two or more
households.
This means that 92.2 per cent of the total private households (34,468)
each occupies a single house, or to put it another way, there are something more than
104 households accommodated in every 100 inhabited private houses.
(14667)

The number of houses with three or less rooms fell between the 1926 - 1937 and
1937 - 1951 periods.
The number of houses with four or five rooms has increased
steeply over the whole period, while the number of houses with six or more rooms has
also increased, though at a slower rate.
In each of the three categories the percentage of shared accommodation fell between

    1926 and 1937; between 1937 and 1951 it remained the same as regards houses of one to
three rooms, decreased substantially in respect of houses of four or five-roomed houses
and increased in the case of houses with six or more rooms.
Rooms; From Table 6 it will be observed that the total number of rooms in inhabi-

    ted private houses was 147,760 (the corresponding figure for 1937 was 130,757).
    The average number of rooms per house was 4.48 compared with 4.40 in 1937.

PRIVATE HOUSEHOLDS
Households: The population of 148,221 occupying private houses was comprised of
34,468 households within the definitions of ^'Private Households" given in Part I.
In
1937 there were 31,503 such households.
The increase of 2,965 households (9.4 per
cent) was at a somewhat greater rate than the rate of increase in population comprising
them (population increase was 12,226 - 9*0 per cent).
The household factor rather
than that of population affects the question of housing.
In the period 1926 to 1937
the percentage increase in households was 6.3 and that of population comprising them
2*6.
The disparity, therefore, between growth in population and in households was much
greater in the earlier intercensal period than in the period 1937 - 1951.
Households
of all sizes increased in number during the period 1937 - 1951 with the exception of
households of nine persons each which remained the same as for the earlier period and
households of ten persons which showed a slight decrease.
(14667)


---

## Page 283

XVI
Table 7, in which the measurement of households according to the number of persons
in each household is detailed, shows that the number of households rises from 3,110
households of one person each, to 6,023 households of the two person group; from thence
there is a slight decrease to the three person group from which the decline is more
marked as each group is reached.
The two-person group comprises 17.5 per cent of all
households, the three-person group 17.3 per cent and the four-person group 16.0 per
cent - these three categories make up over 50 per cent of the total number of private
households.
The comparable figures for 1937 were 17.4 for the two-^person and also three-person
households and 15.5 for the four-person group, making over 50 per cent in all as in the
later period*
The average size of household was 4.5 persons in 1951 and also in 1937.
DENSITIES
Table 7 gives the composition of private households, the number of persons compris-
ing each household, the number of rooms occupied, and the density of occupation.
Private Households? There were 365 households living at densities of more than
four persons per room and 1,092 households at more than three persons per room.
3,772
households or 10.9 per cent fall within the over two-person per room category which has
been assumed to indicate overcrowding.
As shown earlier in these notes the household of two occurs most frequently; and
the most common combination consists of the household of four occupying four rooms.
Of the 11,341 households in the 1-3 room group, 1,812 comprise only one person each.
There are 1,097 households (2,773 persons) living in one roomed accommodation, but of

    these 426 are persons living alone - the figures in 1937 were 1,447 households (3,516
persons) living in one-roomed accommodation.
The number of households of two or more

    persons occupying one room fell from 1,184 in 1926 to 895 in 1937, and fell further to
671 in 1951.
The particulars of households in two and three-roomed accommodation are:-

Of the 32,957 inhabited private houses 1,166 or 3.5 per cent were found to be
occupied by two or more households - 944 of these accommodated two households and 222
accommodated three or more households*

    Persons; Of the total of 148,221 persons in private houses 80.2 per cent (118,813
persons) were living not more than two persons per room: in 1937 the percentage was
7?e5, so that a slight mitigation of general overcrowding is indicated.
Table 7 shows that, taking the total number of persons in private households and
the total number of rooms occupied, there is an average of 1.00 persons for each room.

    In 1926 the corresponding figure was 1.11 and that for 1937 was 1.04.
There has there-
fore been an improvement in room accommodation during both intercensal periods, though
the rate of improvement was considerably higher in the first period.

    Table VIII gives the densities of room occupation for 1926, 1937 and 1951 Censuses
for the County and County Borough.
It will be seen that the proportions of persons living at various densities of
over two persons per room decreased between 1926 and 1937 and further decreased between
1937 and 1951, while the proportion of persons living at densities of two persons or
less per room increased during both intercensal periods.
(14667)

County Districts; In the county divisions the proportion of households occupying
one room each is greatest in Londonderry Rural District where the percentage is 3»34

    (135 households comprised of 375 persons) and next, Limavady Rural District with 3.23
per cent (114 households r* 355 persons).
In the urban areas the proportions are all
under one per cent.
It is pointed out that the exceptional cases in Limavady Rural District, viz.,
(a) 1 household of 15 persons living in one room;
(b) 1 household of 13 persons liv^ing in one room, and
(c) 1 household of 15 persons living in two rooms,
were correctly returned as obtaining at the time of the Census.
Enquiries made through
the superintendent of the census district concerned reveal that the households affected
are now more suitably accommodated.
County Borough; Households occupying one room each are greatest in South Ward
being 6.4 per cent of the total number of households in the ward.
The number of per-
sons concerned is 828 or 3.4 per cent of the ward population.
Looking at the County
Borough as a whole 5.5 per cent of the total households were living in one room per
houseTiold.
Table IX shows, by districts, the population in private households living, at two
persons and less per room and at over two persons per room, also persons per 1,000
living at these densities, and for comparison, the 1926 and 1937 figures for persons
per 1,000 living at a density of over two persons per room are included.
It will be noted that in the County as a whole 215 persons per 1,000 live at a

    density of over two persons per room (in 1937 the figure was 226 and in 1926 it was 265
persons per 1,000 of the population).
With the exception of Londonderry Rural District
reductions - some substantial - in the number of persons living at this density occurred
in all districts in the 1937-1951 period.
Between 1926 and 1937 all areas recorded a
decrease.
The greatest overcrowding is in the rural districts of Limavady, Londonderry

    and Magherafelt in which the persons per 1,000 are 287, .274 and 227 respectively.
The
smallest proportions of overcrowding are in PortStewart Urban District (36 persons per

    1,000) and Coler^^xne Municipal Borough (87 persons per 1,000).
Owing to the reconstitution of wards in the County Borough comparative figures are
available' only for Waterside Ward; these show a steady decrease since 1926.
For the

    vdiole County Borough there was a marked decrease since 1926; fop the period 1926 - 1937
    the number of persons per 1,000 decreasjed from 252 to 222 and in the subsequent inter-
censal interval to 163.
{U667)

## Page 286

XIX
In the 1937 Report attention was drawn to the drop in the number of hotels and
boarding houses, and their occupants since the 1926 Census, although the number of such

    premises in the seaside resort of Port Stewart had increased from 70 to 117 between 1926
and 1937.
Since 1937 there has been a drop in every area except Coleraine Municipal
Borough where the slight increase was from 26 to 27 premises.
The drop in the County

    Borough was from 158 to 61 and in PortStewart Urban District from 117 to 65.
The lat-
ter area contains the highest number of this class of building and accounts for over
35 per cent of the total.

    Between 1937 and 1951 the number of inmates in institutions increased by 92, and
the total population (i.e., inmates and staff) of these institutions t)y 187.

## Page 287

XX
Sex Proportions; Since 1821, when the first Census affecting Northern Ireland was
taken, the number of females in the County and County Borough has exceeded the number of
males.
The greatest disparity was reached in 1901 when there were 1,090 females for every

    1,000 males. From 1901 to 1937 (inclusive) each census has shown a lessening in the prepon-
derance of females; in the latter year the figure stood at 1,031 per 1,000 males - the

    lowest on record - and in 1951 there was a slight increase to 1,032 females per 1,000
males.
For the County and County Borough as a whole boys were in the majority up to
and including 11 years of age in 1951, except at the age of 7 at which girls were
numerically superior. Under the age of one year for the County and County Borough com-

    bined there were 93 girls for every 100 boys, but the figure increased to 97 to every 100
boys at age of 5. At the age of 7 there were 108 girls to 100 boys but at each year

    between 8 and 13, inclusive, there were less than 100 girls for every 100 boys; the
    figures were almost identical at age 15 when the Doys numbered 1,342 and the girls 1,343.
    Again in the combined areas for the age group 25-34 there were 106 women for every
    100 men and this increased to 112 to 100 men in the 65-74 group. At age 80 there were
111 women for 100 men.
It will be noted from Table 12 that in all the urban areas and wards of the County
Borough there is a preponderance of females over males and in all the rural districts
males outnumbered females.
Ages: As in the Qase of Counties Antrim and Down, though unlike present day
tendencies, the average age of the population of the County and County Borough of

    Londonderry decreased between 1937 and 1951. At the 1911 Census the average was 30.7
    years; it fell to 30.1 in 1926 and rose to 32.2 in 1937, falling to 31.0 in 1951.
    In 1937 the 10-14 age group accounted for the largest number of the population of
all quinquennial age groups containing 9.9 per cent of the enumerated population, as
compared with 9. 2 in 1951. In 1951 the age group from birth to four years exceeded all

    others with 11.3 per cent of the population as compared with 9.7 in 1937. Since 1937
    all quinquennial groups except 20-24, 25-29, 60-64, 65-69 and 95 and over show an
increase in numbers.
Increases were recorded for males except in the age groups 20-24,

    25-29, 55-59, 60-64, 65-69 and 95 and upwards.
Increases were recorded for females

    except in the age groups 20-24, 25-29 and 95 and over.
The following chart shows the distribution of the population for the County and

    County Borough in the four main age groups for 1901, 1937 and 1951*
    CENSUSES (COUNTY AND COUNTY BOROUGH OF LONDONDERRY) 1901. 1937. 1951.
PERCENTAGE
