## Page 288

It will be seen that in the two groups comprising persons aged 45 years and upwards
there has been a steady increase since 1901 - as was seen also in the Belfast County
Borough, County Antrim and County Down Reports.
The increase, however, in both groups

    (45-64 and 65 years and over) was only by 0.1 per cent between 1937 and 1951 in each
case whereas the increase in the 0-14 years group was 1,6 per cent in the same period
and the drop in the 15-44 years group was 1.8 per cent.
Marital Condition; Since 1937 the number of persons in the County and County

    Borough aged 14 and over increased by 6,525 of whora 2,930 were males and 3,595 females.
    The number of single persons in this age group decreased by 2,085 (785 males and 1,300
    females), while the number of married persons increased by 8,390 (3,938 males and 4,452
females).
Single males aged 14 and over accounted for 48.3 per cent of the male population

    of the same age in 1951 (52.6 per cent in 1937) and married men for 47.0 per cent (41.9
in 1937).
In the same age group, single females represented 43.9 per cent (49.2 in

    1937) and married women 45.7 per cent (40.4 in 1937).
For both sexes the only quinquennial groups of married persons to show a decrease

    during the 1937 - 1951 intercensal period were males 60-64, 65-69 and females ages 95
and over; in this last mentioned group there was only one married female in 1937 but
none in 1951.
Widowers decreased by 247 whilst widows increased by 389.
The number of persons
whose marriages had been dissolved and who had not remarried, increased from 14 in 1937

    to 92 in 1951 - divorced men numbered 30 as compared with 6 in 1937, and divorced women
62 as against 8*
EDUCATION
Table 13 summarises the information furnishad regarding attendance at educational
establishments.
It will be seen that for the Coimty and County Borough, of children
aged 5-13 inclusive 4.7 per cent are stated not to be attending school.
In the 14-17
age groups, i.e., amongst persons over the age for compulsory school attendance but of
normal age for attending grammar school, 73. 3 per cent were not attending educational
establishments.
Persons of all ages attending educational establishments part-^-time were shown to
number 149.
As the total number of part-time students enrolled in the institutions of
further education under the management of the Londonderry County and County Borough

    Education Authorities during the year ended 31st July, 1951, numbered 3,216, it is
apparent that in many cases the information regarding part-time education was not
furnished on the census returns.
Much the same result was disclosed by the earlier
census reports.
It would appear that the significance of the question was not
appreciated by the heads of households.
BIRTHPLACES
Table 14 shows, by sex, the number of persons born in each of the counties of
Northern Ireland, other parts of the British Isles, Commonwealth Countries, etc.,
foreign countries and at sea.
Of the total population of the County and County Borough

    (i.e., 155,540) persons bom in the County or County Borough numbered 124,297 (79,9 per
cent), and altogether 137,411 (88.3 per cent) were born in some part of Northern Ireland.
&,998 persons (6.4 per cent) were born in the Irish Republic as compared with 10,274
(7.2 per cent) in 1937.
Only 1,021 persons (0*7 per cent) were born outside the
3ritish Isles, of whom 420 were born in Commonwealth Countries, 592 iti foreign countries,
and 8 at sea.
No birthplace was given for one person.
The percentage representing persons born in the County or County Borough (79.9) has
decreased since 1937 (80.8); the percentage of persons born in adjoining Counties of
Antrim and Tyrone decreased slightly from 5.6 to 5.4.
Table XII gives comparative figures for Censuses since 1861.

The figures given relate to the County and County Borough as constituted at the date of each Census.
A
portion of the County of Londonderry was transferred to the County of Antrim in 1915, consequent on the
extension of Portrush Urban District,
It will be noted that the percentage of the total population born in Northern

    Ireland decreased steadily between 1861 and 1926, viz., from 97.50 to 87.97.
Between

    1926 and 1937 there was an increase to 88.82 but this fell to 88.34 in 1951.
Likewise
over the same period 1861 to 1926 the percentage born in the Irish Republic steadily-
increased (1.-92 to 7.87);

    betv/een 1926 and 1937 there was a decrease to 7.20 and a
further decrease to 6.43 in 1951 which is the lowest point for the past sixty years.
The percentage born in England and Wales stands at 2.94 the highest figure i*eached during
the ninety years 1861 - 1951;
that born in Scotland increased for each intercensal
period during the span 1861 - 1926 but thereafter a decrease is recorded at each Census.

visitors to Northern Ireland and divided into five religious groups.

•The figures given relate to the County and County Borougji as constituted at the date of each Census.
A portion
of Londonderry was transferred to the County of Antrim In 1915, consequent on the extension of Portrush Urban
District.

    /Including Church of England and Episcopal Church of Scotland, numbering 2,453 and 4, respectively. In 1951.
A scrutiny of Table XIV reveals that Roman Catholics reached their highest percent-
age (49.5) of the total population in 1951 having increased by 1.0 per cent for each of
the intercensal periods since 1926; prior to that the percentage fluctuated - in 1861

    it was 45.3 and in 1881 reached its lowest point, i.e., 44.4 from which it has increased
at each enumeration.
Presbyterians stood at 35.1 of the total population in 1861 but
at each succeeding Census a decrease has been recorded, reaching its lowest percentage,
i,e., 25.8 in 1951.
The Church of Ireland gained 0.1 per cent between 1937 and 1951

    (from 20.2 to 20.3) and at the latter year stood 3.4 per cent higher than in 1861.
Methodists also increased by 0.1 per cent of the total population between 1937 and 1951;

    the figure for 1951 (1.4) being the same as for 1911 and 1926.
"Other and not stated
Denominations" show a percentage of 3.0 which is an increase of 0.2 on the 1937 figure.
A comparison of the 1951 figures with those for 1937 shows that there were, numerical
increases in all five groups, viz., Roman Catholics 7,730, Presbyterians 1,407, Church

    of Ireland 2,770, Methodists 289, others 608.
Table 16 shows the population by sex for each County District and Ward of the
County Borough under the five religious groups, viz., Roman Catholic, Presbyterian,
Church of Ireland (including the Church of England and the Episcopal Church of Scotland),
Methodist, and all other (or not stated) denominations.
The list of descriptions of
"Other Denominations" given at the foot of the table refers to the County and County
Borough as a whole; the names of denominations are restricted to those having not less
than ten adherents.
Of the "Other Denominations" the two ^categories which show the highest increases as

    compared with 1937 are: Baptist + 183 and Congregationalist + 120.
The number of per-
sons who omitted to state their denomination has increased from 91 to 470.
Table 17 gives details for the County and County Borough under five religious cate-
gories and age groups (individual years to age 21, and quinquennial age groups up to
69 years).
The comparative Table in the 1937 Report gave the population by individual
ages up to 20, and by two religious groups - Roman Catholics and other denominations.
It is not therefore possible to make a complete comparison between the 1937 figures and
those published in this Report, but it is observed that the overall increase in the

    population of Roman Catholics up to the age of 20 is 4,790 or 16.3 per cent, while the
increase in the population of all other denominations up to that age is 2,034 or 7,4 per
cent.
The following are the percentage increases or decreases in the various age

    groups up to 19 years and of the population aged 20 and over between 1937 and 1951:-

    A comparison of Tables 15 and 16 shows that in the County and County Borough 10.4
per cent of Roman Catholics, 9*7 per cent of Presbyterians, 16.2 per cent of members of
the Church of Ireland, 21.8 per cent of Methodists, and 13.9 per cent of other and not
stated denominations were born outside Northern Ireland.
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
It will be seen from the volumes "Classification of Occupations" and "Classifica-
tion of Industries" referred to in Part I of this Report that the separate terms under
which workers are classified run into many thousands.
This has occasioned modifica-
tions to bring census data within manageable dimensions, and thus generally speaking
the arrangement of headings is according to class of materials worked with, commodities
produced or services rendered.
Special attention is drawn to the following changes since the 1926 Census which is
the last occasion on which occupational and industrial enquiries were included in the
Northern Ireland Census Schedule:-

    (1) The 1951 Tables deal with persons aged 14 years and over, whereas in 1926 the
a^e of 12 was adopted as the minimum.
For Table XV the 1926 figures were adjusted
to the 1951 age grouping to allow proper comparison but see footnote to that table*
See footnote to Table XVI also.
(2) A revision in the Classification of Occupations since 1926 brings together
the bulk of the unskilled workers in a single group - Order XXVI.
Occupations; Table XV sets out the numbers and percentages of persons occupied
(i.e., persons whose normal occupation was stated) in the age group from 14 years and
upwards, for the Censuses 1926 and 1951*
The term "occupied" includes persons temporarily out of work.
The number returned

    as temporarily out of work in 1926 for the County and County Borough was 5,210 (4,143
males and 1,067 females)o

    In 1951, the figure amounted to 6,164 (5,035 males and
1,129 females).

The above table shows that for both sexes the percentage of occupied to t o t a l

    number of persons aged 14 years and over i s lower for 1951 than for 1986.
In 1926,
however, males aged 65 years and over comprised 12,0 per cent of the male population

    aged 14 years and upwards, whereas in 1951 the percentage was 12,9 and of females aged
    14 years and upwards the percentage aged 65 and over increased from 13.1 in 1926 to
13.7 in 1951.
I t i s probable also that the percentage of occupied persons - both male
and female - of the age group 65 and over decreased considerably between the Censuses.
(14667)

## Page 292

Though the proportions have decreased since 1926, the actual number occupied has

    increased from 60,400 in 1926 to 65,140 in 1951 - an increase of 4,740 persons (3,434
males and 1,306 females).

    In both 1926 and 1951 there were 42 occupied females to every 100 occupied males.
Table XVI shows the percentages of occupied to the total population, including
children under 14 years of age.

on the number for the whole of Northern Ireland it is necessarily small.
From Table XVI it •will be seen that the occupied represented 43.3 per cent of the

    population of both sexes and all ages in 1926 and in 1951 the percentage was 41.9.
In
Co. Antrim the decrease was only by 0.1 per cent but in Belfast County Borough it was
1.1 per cent and in County Down 1.6.
The figures for occupied persons as a percentage
of the total population (both sexes) of all ages in 1911 were - males 30. 6; females
11.8; persons 42.4.
The increase recorded for females between 1911 and 1926 was not
maintained in the later intercensal period and in fact a lower percentage were employed
in 1951 than in 1926.
For males the percentage decrease was only 0.2 between 1911 and
1926 but for the subsequent period the drop was by 0.9.
Table 18 gives the full list of occupations of persons of 14 years of age and over
and Table XVII (overleaf) shows the proportions per 1,000 of the occupied males and
females under the numerically important Orders and also for numerically important single
or grouped occupations.
32.5 per cent of the occupied males are engaged in Agricultural Occupations; 9.3
per cent in Transport and Communications; 8.3 per cent in Commercial, Finance and Insur^
ance Occupations (excluding Clerical); and 8.0 per cent are engaged as workers in
Building and Contracting etc.
37.5 per cent of occupied females are employed on the
manufacture of Textile Goods and Articles of Dress; 23.1 per cent are in Personal
Service; 9.0 per cent are Clerks, Typists, etc. and 8.3 are in Commercial, Finance and
Insurance Occupations.
The changes since 1926 in the Classification of Occupartions and
especially the introduction of Order XXVI bringing together groups of unskilled workers
not provided for elsewhere (formerly included in various Orders) make it impracticable
to give a general comparison between 1926 and 1951 figures.
It is, however, possible
to give comparative figures in some instances.
The following are those which show the
greatest variation in numbers and which are broadly similar to the 1926 classification:-

## Page 294

XXVI 1
Industries: The industrial classification has not undergone widespread changes
since 1926 as was the case in the classification of occupations.
The 1926 and 1951
figures, however, are not comparable as the 1926 figures for the occupied population

    include persons out of work (i.e., 4,143 males and 1,067 females) whereas the 1951
figures exclude persons out of work, in accordance with the practice throughout the
United Kingdom for this Census.
Table XVIII shows the distribution for 1951 according to certain main industrial
groupings for the County and County Borough.

The-Manufacturing Industries (used in a wide sense) provide employment for 34 per
cent of the occupied population.
In 1926 the percentage was 28.
Agriculture and
Fishing provide 24 per cent and the Distributive Trades and Finance (including Insur-

    ance and Banking) 13 per cent; in 1926 the percentages were 36 and 12 respectively.
Table XVIII also shows that the Males outnumber the Females in all groups except
the Professions and Miscellaneous Services.
The former group includes Teachers and
Nurses and the latter, persons employed in Private Domestic Service, Restaurants,
Entertainment, etc.
The same balance was recorded in respect of earlier Reports for
1951.
For males Agriculture and Fishing provided employment for 34 per cent of the
occupied, and the Manufacturing Industries (mainly Building and Contracting and the
Manufacture of Foods and Drinks) 26 per cent; in 1926 the percentage for the former
was 46 and 21 for the latter.
The Manufacturing Industries (mainly Textiles and Clothing) gave employment to

    52 per cent of the occupied females; in 1926 the percentage was 42.
Table 21 gives the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, separate figures being given for each Order and suitable groupings thereunder.
(14667)

## Page 384

PREFACE
Three censuses of population have been taken since the Government of Northern
Ireland was established.
The first enumeration took place in 1926 and incorporated
questions relating to occupation and industry, orphanhood and infirmities.
The
seconii enumeration made in 1937 was of more limited scope and was intended to bridge
the gap between the census of^ 1926 and the census which it was proposed to take in
1941, but which had to be abandoned owing to the outbreak of war.
The census taken
as at mid-night of 8th-9th April, 1951, forms the basis of this report and like that
in 1926 questions were asked as to the occupations and industries of the population.
The length of time required to process the data collected at an enumeration before
it can be presented in the ultimate reports is necessarily considerable.
In order to
meet immediate requirements, however, two Preliminary Reports on the 1951 census were
published.
The first of these gave the population figures by administrative areas
and towns and villages, and by Counties and County Boroughs according to religious
profession.
The Second Report, which was restricted to Counties and County Boroughs,
gave the population by age groups.
The present Volume is the sixth of seven county volumes (the Belfast County
Borough and Counties of Antrim, Down, Londonderry and Armagh volumes having been
published already) and has been prepared (as were also the Preliminary Reports) in
accordance with the provisions of the Census Act (No'lrthern Ireland) 1951.
The
statistics now published supersede those shown in the Preliminary Reports and may be
accepted as final.
This volume is consistent in design and scope with the previous volumes and the
remaining County volume (Fermanagh) will be prepared in like manner.
A General Report for the whole of Northern Ireland will follow the county
volumesi
This will bring together, and in some respects show in greater detail, the
information contained in the county volumes.
Additional features will also be
introduced particularly in regard to the distribution of the people industrially by
age and marital condition.
Following the 1926 census a topographical index was published dealing with each
type of area for which statistics were supplied in the county series.
A supplement
to this index was published after the 1937 census and a further supplement in respect
of the 1951 census will be prepared in due course.
Census publications are obtainable either directly from H.M. Stationery Office,
Chichester House, Chichester Street, Belfast, or from any book-seller.
L. C. MULLIGAN,
REGISTRAR-GENERAL
General Register Office,
Fermanagh House,
BELFAST.
June, 1954.
(18165)

## Page 389

PART I
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF TERMS
USED IN THIS VOLUME
AREA
The acreages given are as meaaured by the Ordnance Survey (foreshore being
excluded), and relate to the several administrative areas as constituted at the date
of the 1951 census.
The acreage covered by the larger rivers and lakes is not
included in the body of Tables 4 and 5 but is given in footnotes thereto.
POPULATION
The population comprises all persons on land or in vessels on inland waters on
census night.
Members of the Forces of the Crown are also included.
ADMINISTRATIVE DIVISIONS
The County is divided into ten county districts (four urban districts and six
rural districts) comprising 145 district electoral divisions, eleven of which are
coterminous with entire urban districts or wards thereof (see table 4).
Wards, where
they exist in urban districts, are the units for the purpose of municipal elections.
In rural districts the unit is the district electoral division.
The County is also
divided into twenty-seven county electoral divisions, which are the units for the
purpose of county council elections (see table 5).
Owing to the abolition of the Boards of Guardians, details of the Poor Law Union
and Dispensary districts are not given in this volume.
These areas, however, form
respectively, superintendent registrars^ and registrars^ districts for the registration
of marriages, births and deaths, and consequently the census tabulations have been
prepared in a manner suitable for the extraction of data according to these areas
should such be required.
There are five parliamentary constituencies for elections to the Northern Ireland
Parliament.
For elections to the United Kingdom Parliament South Tyrone with County
Fermanagh forms one constituency and the remainder of County Tyrone with the
Magherafelt Rural District (County Londonderry) forms another constituency.
For each
of these constituencies there is one Member of Parliament (see note (e) below and
Table 9).
The Tyrone Education Area is coterminous with the County.
The changes made in the constitution or boiindaries of administrative divisions of
the County during the last intercensal period are as follows:-
(a) The boundary of Cookstown Urban District was extended (1st April, 1947).
(b) The boundary of Dungannon Urban District was extended (1st April, 1949).
(c) A portion of Cookstown Rural District was transferred to Cookstown Urban
District (1st April, 1947).
(d) A portion of Dungannon Rural District was transferred to Dungannon Urban
District (1st April, 1949).
(e) Under the provision of the Representation of the People Act, 1948 two
constituencies affecting Tyrone were created for elections to the United Kingdom
Parliament, viz., (l) Mid-Ulster, comprised of part of Co. Londonderry
(Magherafelt Rural District) and part of Co. Tyrone, and (2) Fermanagh and
South Tyrone, comprised of all Co. Fermanagh and the remaining part of Co. Tyrone.
(18165)


---

## Page 390

HOUSES
1.
The term !!Hou^' means
(a) each structurally separate dwelling (except institutions) having distinct
access to the street, common stair, or landing.
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanitorium, nursing home, asylum,
religious, welfare or charitable community, residential school, barracks or other
premises under Naval, Military, Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other
place of detention), regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts.
(c) each boat, caravan, tent or other movable dwelling if occupied on census
night but not otherwise.
2.
The term "Private House" means a dwelling other than an institution, hotel,
boarding house, premises occupied by a caretaker, vessel, movable dwelling, barn,
stable or outhouse occupied on census night.
The term includes premises comprising a
shop with dwelling attached, unless occupied by a caretaker.
3.
The terms "Boarding House" and "Lodging House"' cover habitations described as
such, and any private house containing three or more boarders or lodgers.
4.
Th,e term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons on census
night.
"Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those temporarily vacant
through the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Enumerators were instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in such an
advanced state of decay that there was little likelihood that they would again be
made fit for habitation.
ROOMS
1.
The rooms counted are the usual living rooms including bedrooms and kitchens but
excluding sculleries (i.e., compartments used only for washing, cleaning, or
cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets, bathrooms, or any warehouse, office
or shop rooms.
Z*
For the classification of households according to rooms occupied (see table 7),
shared rooms - i.e., those used in common by two or more households - have been
included in the number of rooms occupied by the main household in the dwelling. .In
cases of one room occupations, all persons living therein, whether related or not,
have been enumerated as one census household.
HOUSEHOLDS
1.
"Non-private Households"' -comprise all persons enumerated in hotels, boarding
houses, lodging houses, shops and institutions (see para. 1(b) under "Houses"'
definition above).
2.
"Private Households"' are comprised of single persons or groups of persons
occupying separately the whole, or part of any private dwelling (see para. 2
under "Houses" definition above).
Visitors and domestic servants are included in the
household with which they were enumerated.
Lodgers having or sharing separate
accommodation were enumerated on separate returns and consequently are regarded as
separate private households.
It should be specially noted that a household or private household for census
purposes includes any group of persons, whether related or not, who live together in
separate house or room accommodation.
RATEABLE VALUATION
The figures shown are those for the year ended 31st March, 1952, and have been
supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Subject to the provisions of the Local Government (Rating and Finance) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1929, agricultural land and agricultural buildings are completely
(18165)


---

## Page 391

derated and industrial hereditaments and freight-transport hereditaments are partially
derated, the consequent deficiency in the yield of rates being made good by way of
General Exchequer contributions.
The figures in Tables 4 and 5 are the totals of the valuations in the various
areas before reduction on account of derating, but exclude in certain cases the valua-
tions of electricity, gas and water mains, telegraphs, telephones, fisheries, water-
works and tramways.
The total of such excluded valuations for the county is given in
a foot-note to the table.
USUAL RESIDENCE
The persons who have been regarded as having their usual residence in the area of
enumeration (i.e., the County) include:-
(1)
Those enumerated in their own houses;
(2)
Persons having settled residence in private lodgings;
(3)
Resident domestic servants;
(4)
Scholars, students, etc., living during term tim.e in residential schools,
etc.;
(5)
Inmates of institutions when the nature of the institution suggested
permanent residence;
(6)
Crews of vessels if usually living on shipboard.
BIRTHPLACES
The birthplaces of a small number of persons were not stated on the census return;
it was assumed that these persons were born in the county or county borough of enumera-
tion, except when they were stated to be visitors to the household, or bore foreign
names.
After further enquiry in the latter cases it was found possible to classify
them under definite headings.
RELIGIONS
The classification of religions includes a separate heading for each religion,
the adherents of which number 10 or more (see Table 1 6 ) .
The heading "Presbyterian"
includes those who call themselves by this name without qualification, together with
those who describe themselves as members of the Church of Scotland.
Other Presbyterian
denominations are shown at the foot of Table 16. Members of the Church of England and
the Episcopal Church of Scotland have been included under the heading "Church of
Ireland".
The latter group includes all persons returned as "Church" or "Episcopalian"
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
There are two categories into which the population may be grouped in their working
capacity, namely, occupation and industry.
The sole criterion for determining the
occupational classification is the type of work performed.
On the other hand,
industry has to do with the kind of business with which the occupation is associated.
Thus a carpenter working for a firm of shipbuilders would be classified under
"Carpenter" for occupation but under "Shipbuilding" for industry.
The 1951 Censuses were carried out simultaneously in all parts of the British
Isles and this afforded the first opportunity for Northern Ireland to adopt both
occupational and industrial classifications*on exactly the same lines as those used
for the rest of the United Kingdom.
* Classification of Occupations, 1950.
H.M.S.O.
Price £1 10s. Od. net.
Census 1951, Classification of industries.
H.M.S.O.
Price 8s. 6d. net.
(18165)


---

## Page 392

In the preparation of Tables 19 and 21 the range of Orders, Sub-Orders and
Occupational and Industrial unit groups have been modified by combining closely
related groups where the nximbers were small and the preservation or usefulness of
the data was not harmed.
TABLES CONTAINING DOTS (.) AND DASHES (-)
Throughout this volume dots (.) are used in tabular matter to indicate "None",
and dashes (-) are used to indicate that figures are not available or that the
column headings are not relevant to the lines in which the dashes appear.
CORRECTION OF CENSUS DATA
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the census data.
The process of correction began with the enumera-
tors, who received full instructions as to the action to be taken in cases of error or
omission, and it was continued by a system of check and further enquiry where necessary
applied at each stage of the office work.
The process was completed by an intensive
scrutiny and inter-comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny and comparison
revealed a certain number of incongruities, which, when material, were investigated
and corrected in the light of the facts disclosed;
trifling errors were adjusted
arbitrarily.
(18165)


---

## Page 393

PART II
STATISTICAL NOTES
The definitions, etc, given in the preceding section apply equally to these
notes and to the tables in Part III.
AREA
Descriptions and figures are given for areas as constituted at the date of the
1951 census unless otherwise stated.
The area of Tyrone County exclusive of the larger rivers and lakes, is 1,218
square miles or 23.2 per cent of the area of Northern Ireland.
POPULATION
The total population of the County was 132,082.
During, the intercensal period

    1937-1951 there was an increase in population of 4,496 persons but for each intercensal
period between 1841 and 1937 a decrease was registered.
Table 2 shows that censuses
were taken decennially between 1821 and 1911 and thereafter at irregular intervals;
this should be remembered when comparing data of one enumeration with another.
During
the whole range of enumerations the highest point was reached in 1841 when the figure

    was 313,011 and the lowest point in 1937 when the population was only 127,586. The
    two periods between 1821 and 1841 showed a combined increase of 51,146 persons but in
    the following decade (1841-1851) there was a decrease of 57,291 and although this was
the greatest intercensal decrease (18.3 per cent) the decreases for the periods 1881-

    1891 and 1891-1901 were also high being 13.3 and 12.2 per cent respectively.
Natural Increase; The following table sets out figures of the natural increase
and actual increase or decrease of population in each of the intercensal periods since
1871, and shows the net loss by migration.

Comparable figures of natural increases due to the excess of births over deaths,
in respect of the County are not available for periods prior to 1871, but it is evident

    that in each of the intercensal periods from 1841 to 1871, as well as from 1871 to 1937,
there was a net outward movement of such dimensions as not only to counterbalance the
natural increase, but to cause a marked decrease in population.
During the 1937-1951
period, however, although an increase in population of 4,496 is shown the outward
movement was as high as 11,045.

* The figures given relate to each area as constituted at the date of the 1951 Census.
f Estimated figure.
As the administrative divisions of the County are constituted at present, 108,777
live in rural areas and 23,305 in urban districts.
The urban population constitutes

    17.6 per cent of the whole as compared with 14.6 in 1937, 13.3 in 1926, 12.2 in 1911,
and 11.3 in 1901 - the areas being as constituted at the date of each census.
It
will be seen that all divisions except Castlederg and Clogher Rural Districts show
increases during the fourteen years 1937-1951.
The most marked percentage increases

    are in the urban districts of Strabane (18.2 per cent or 1,019 persons), Omagh (17.7
    per cent or 1,016 persons) and Dungannon (14.7 per cent or 729 persons).
The total
increase in the urban population is 3,193 or 15.9 per cent and the overall increase in
the rural population is 1,303 or 1.2 per cent - the areas being the same for both
censuses.
For the whole course of fifty years (1901 ~ 1951) all urban areas show an
increase since the commencement of the period:- Cookstown (313 or 8.0 per cent),

    Dungannon (1,360 or 31.5 per cent), Omagh (1,968 or 41.1 per cent) and Strabane (1,586
or 31.5 per cent).
All rural districts show substantial decreases, notably Clogher
(3,628 or 25.3 per cent).
Dungannon had the lowest proportionate decrease (3,168
persons or 11.2 per cent).
Amongst the urban areas population density is highest in Dungannon (7.3 persons
per acre) and lowest in Strabane (3.6 persons per acre).
In rural districts the rates
vary between 0.1 and 0.2 persons per acre.
For the whole County the rate is 0.2
persons per acre.
Tables 4 and 5 provide detailed information regarding area, population, housing
and accommodation for the various sub-divisions of the County and incorporate figures
of rateable valuation.
The rateable valuation of the County in 1951 was £594,879
before reduction on account of derating and £242,030 after reduction on account of
(18165)


---

## Page 395

derating.
In 1937 the corresponding valuations of the County were £548,698 and
£196,349 respectively.
Table 4 includes information regarding towns and villages containing 50 houses and
upwards with no legally defined boundaries.
Parliamentary Constituencies; Table 9 sets out the Parliamentary Constituencies
of the County for the Parliaments of both the United Kingdom and Northern Ireland
together with the figures of population, number of electors and electors per 1,000 of
the population, distinction being shown in respect of electors with residence
qualification.
Under the provisions of the Elections and Franchise Act (Northern Ireland), 1946,
a person is entitled to be registered as a parliamentary^ elector for any constituency
returning a member to the Parliament of Northern Ireland if he or she is a British
subject, 21 or more years of age, not subject to any legal incapacity, and was born in
Northern Ireland (or alternatively, has resided continuously in the United Kingdom for
a period of seven years ending on the qualifying date) and either:-
(a) has the requisite residence qualification of three months; or
(b) has occupied for three months prior to the qualifying date business premises
of at leasHi £10 valuation; or
(c) is the wife or husband of a person entitled to be registered in respect of
the above business premises qualification;
or
(d) was on the qualifying date a member of the Forces or a merchant seaman and who,
but for such service, would have been residing in Northern Ireland.
The electorate in the Queen's University constituency comprises all graduates of
the University who are British subjects of full age, not subject to any legal
incapacity.
Under the provisions of the Representation of the People Ac't (1949), a person is
entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any Northern Ireland con-
stituency returning a member to the Parliament at Westminster if that person is a
British subject or citizen of the Irish Republic of twenty-one or more years of age and
not subject to any legal incapacity to vote, is resident at an address in that con-
stituency on the qualifying date, and has resided at that address (or at any other
address or addresses in Northern Ireland) for a period of three months ending on the
qualifying date.
In addition, a person who would orcjinarily have been resident at a specified
address in a constituency in Northern Ireland, may be registered in respect of a
Service qualification if;
(i) he or she is a member of the Forces; or
(ii) he or she is a person employed in the service of the Crown in a post outside
the United Kingdom;
or
(iii) she is the wife of a person having a Service qualification and is residing
outside the United Kingdom to be with her husband.
Education Area; The Tyrone Coiinty Council is the Education Authority for the
whole area under its control, and all figures given in this Volume for the County apply
equally to the Tyrone Education Area.
HOUSING - BUILDINGS• ROOMS, HOUSEHOLDS, ETC.
Houses: The total number of houses, including hotels, institutions, etc., in the
County normally used for dwelling purposes was 32,934 of which 30,773 houses were
occupied on Census night.
The number of occupied houses at the 1937 Census was 30,241,
an increase therefore of 532, or 1.8 per cent iinder this category took place in the
intercensal period.
Table III includes all houses, private and non-private.
The
number of non-private houses was 164 of which 70 were boarding houses or hotels.
The

The total number of houses was the highest recorded since 1911.
The number of
houses under construction (607) was outstanding; the next highest census figure was
that of 86 for the 1926 census.
The average number of persons (exclusive of inmates of institutions) per house,

    steadily decreased from 1851 to 1937 (from ^.61 to 4.17) and now stands at 4.25.
Table IV shows the changes in the numbers of houses in each County district during
the intercensal period 1937-1951.
The increase of 444 houses for the County should be
considered with the figure for uninhabited houses, which shows a drop from 2,196 in

    1937 to 1,554 in 1951; 86 per cent of the houses unoccupied on Census night in 1951
were in rural areas; it was observed in County Armagh that 79 per cent were in rural
areas.

the

INHABITED PRIVATE HOUSES
Number of Houses and Inhabitants; The following statement sets out the figures
for inhabited private houses, persons therein and average number per house for censuses

    1926, 1937 and 1951, for the County.

The average annual increase in the number of inhabited private houses for the

    period 1926-1937 was 18 and for the period 1937-1951 it was 47, but this must be viewed
in the light of variations in the population in these dwelling houses - there was a
decrease of population (4,527 persons) in the first period, whereas in the latter there
was an increase of 4,520.
The relative changes are seen more clearly from the figures

    for ''persons per house" the ratios having dropped for the period 1926 - 1937 by 0.17
    and increased for 1937-1951 by 0.05.
    Of the total of 30,609 inhabited private houses, 30,364 (99.2 per cent) were
occupied by single private households, the remainder of 245 were shared by two or mpr^
households.

    Table V shows for the County the position at each census 1926, 1937 and 1951 for
inhabited private houses of various sizes and the extent to which they were shared by
two or more households.

The number of houses with three or less rooms fell during the 1926-1937 and
1937-1951 intervals.
The number of houses with four or five rooms increased steeply
over the whole period, while the number of houses with six or more rooms has also
increased, though at a slower rate.
The percentage of shared accommodation fell between 1926 and 1937 in each type of
house; between 1937 and 1951 it remained the same as regards houses of one to three
rooms and increased in each of the other categories.
Rooms 2 From Table 6 it will be observed that the total number of rooms in

    inhabited private houses was 130,912 (t^e corresponding figure for 1937 was 123,670)•
    The average number of rooms per house was 4.28 compared with 4.13 in 1937.

Information incorporated in Tables V and .VI for 1951 can be readily obtained for

PRIVATE HOUSEHOLDS
Households; The population of 128,096 occupying private houses was comprised of
30,910 households within the definitions of "Private Households"* given in Part I.
In
1937 there were 30,076 such households.
The increase of 834 households (2«8 per cent)
was at a somewhat lower rate than the rate of increase in population comprising them
(population increase was 4,520 -3.7 per cent).
It will be appreciated that the house-
hold factor has an important bearing on the question of housing.
In the period 1926 -
1937 the percentage increase in households was 0.35, whereas the population comprising
them decreased by 4,527.
Households of all sizes increased in number during the
period 1937 - 1951 with the exception of households of three, nine and ten persons each.
Table 7, in which the measurement of households according to the number of persons
in each household is detailed, shows that the number of households rises from 3,307
households of one person each, to 5,613 households of the two-person group; from
thence there is- a decrease to 5,356 of the three-person group from which there is steep
decline.
The two-person group comprises 18.2 per cent of all households, the three-
person group 17.3 per cent and the four-person group 15.9 per cent - these three
categories make up over 51 per cent of the total niomber of private households.

    The comparable figures for 1937 were 18.3 for the two-person, 18.2 for three-
person households and 15.7 for the four-person group, making over 52 per cent in all.
The average size of household was 4.1 persons in 1951 and also in 1937.
DENSITIES
Table 7 gives the composition of private households, the number of persons com-
prising each household, the number of rooms occupied, and the density of occupation.
Private Households; There were 272 households living at densities of more than
four persons per room and 903 households at more than three persons per room.
3,093
households or 10.0 per cent fall within the over two person per room category which
has been assumed to indicate overcrowding.
As shown earlier in these notes the household of two occurs most frequently; and
the most common combinations consist of the households of two and three, each occupying
four rooms.
Of the 12,014 households in the 1-3 room group, 2,146 comprise only one
person each.
There are 669 households (1,622 persons) living in one-roomed accommoda-

    tion, but of these 305 are persons living alone - the figures in 1937 were 577 house-
holds (1,209 persons) living in one-roomed accommodation.
The number of households of

    two or more persons occupying one room decreased from 454 in 1926 to 280 in 1937, but
rose to 364 in 1951.
The particulars of households in two and three-roomed accommodation are:-

    Of the 30,609 inhabited private houses 245 or 0,8 per cent were found to be
occupied by two or more households - 209 of these accommodated two households and 36
accommodated three or more households.
Persons; Of the total of 128,096 persons in private houses 81.3 per cent
(104,154 persons) were living not more than two persons per room;
in 1937 the percent-
age was 80.6, so that a slight mitigation of general overcrowding is indicated.
Table 7 shows that, taking the total number of persons in private households and
the total number of rooms occupied, there is an average of 0.98 persons for each room.

    In 1926 the corresponding figure was 1.07 and that for 1937 was 1.00, so that there has
been an improvement in room accommodation during both intercensal periods.

• Table VII» dealing with persons per household was restricted to Wards in the Belfast County Borough Volume.
It will be seen that the proportions of persons Itving at various densities of
over two persons per room decreased between 1926 and 1937 and^further decreased between
1937 and 1951 although in each of the two categories coxnprising the over ,three persons
per room densities thei'e was a slight increase in the 1937-1951 period.
County Districts; In the county divisions the proportion of hpuseholdB occupying
one room each is greatest in Castlederg Rural District where the percentage is 5*81
(131 households comprised of 468 persons).
Strabane Rural District comes next for
which the percentage is 3.62.
Table IX shows, by districts, the population in private households living at two
persons and less per room and at over two persons per room, also persons per 1,000
living at these densities, and for comparison, the 1926 and 1937 figures for persons
per 1,000 living at a density of over two persons per room are included.
It will be noted that in the County as a whole 187 persons per 1,000 live at a

    density of over two persons per room (in 1937 the figure was 194 and in 1926 it was 232
persons per 1,000 of the population).
Increases in the nuniber of persons living at
this density occurred in the rural districts of Castlederg, Dungannon and Strabane in
the 1937-1951 period.
Decreases - some substantial -- took place in all other
districts.
Between 1926 and 1937 all areas except Dungannon and Strabane Urban
Districts recorded a decrease.
The greatest overcrowding is in the rural districts

    of Castlederg, Strabane and Omagh in which the persons per 1,000 are 289, 240 and 212
respectively.
The smallest proportions of overcrowding are in Diingannon and Omagh
Urban Districts in each of which the figure is 95 persons per 1,000.

The 1937 Report showed that the number of hotels and boarding houses had decreased
by about 25 per cent since 1926 and their occupants similarly.

been a drop in every area except Dungannon Urban District, where the number did not
(18165)

## Page 401

    change. The Total for the County dropped to 70, from 171 in 1937.
Omagh Urban
District contains the highest number of this class of building and accounts for 24 per
cent of the total.

    Betweea 1937 and 1951 the number of inmates in institutions decreased by 69^ but
the total population (i.e., inmates and staff) of these institutions increased by 7.
USUAL RESIDENCE
Table 10 discloses that 98.2 per cent of the enumerated population of the County
were normally resident in the County and of the remainder, 1,610 persons ware usually
resident in some other part of Northern Ireland.
Allowing for 1,522 persons usually
resident in the County but enumerated elsewhere in Northern Ireland the adjustment to
obtain the usual residence figure represented 0.7 per 1,000 of the population.
In
1937, when the "Usual Residence" question was first included in the Census Return, the
adjustment figure was 1.4 per 1,000 of the total enumeration.
Only 702 persons were usually resident outside the Province.
SEX, AGE AND MARITAL CXDNDITION
Table 11 shows the population of the County according to sex, age (individual
years and quinquennial groups) and marital condition.
Table XI although restricted to
certain age groups, provides a convenient basis for comparison of the 1937 position
with that of 1951.

## Page 402

Sex Proportions; Since 1821, when the first Census affecting Northern Ireland was
tkken, each enumeration up to and including 1901 showed that the number of females in
the County exceeded t^e number of males.
The greatest disparity was in 1821 when
there were 1,062 females for every 1,000 males.
At each census from 1911 to 1951
(inclusive) males were in the majority.
The number of females per 1,000 males

    decreased from 98d in 1911 to 978 in 1926.
    In 1937 the figure stood at 943 per 1,000
    males, but in 1951 there wa^ an increase to 956 females per 1,000 males.
Males were
in the majority in 1951, in the quinquennial age groups comprising 0-49 years and at

    all ages within these groups, except at ages, 1, 3, 12, 25, 26, 29 and 33, at which
females were numerically superior.

    In the age groups 50-64, 70-74, 85-94, and 100
years and upwards females outnumbered males.
It will be noted from Table 12 that in all the urban areas there is a preponder-
ance of females over males whereas in all the rural districts males outniomber females.
Ages: The average age of the population of the County decreased between 1937 and
1951.

    At the 1911 Census the average was 32.4 years; it fell to 31.7 in 1926 and
    went up to 33.8 in 1937, falling to 32.3 in 1951.
    In 1937 the 10-14 age group accounted for the largest number of the population of
all quinquennial age groups, containing 9.4 per cent of the enumerated population, as
compared with 8.8 in 1951.
In 1951 the age group from birth to four years exceeded
all others with 10.9 per cent of the population as compared with 9.0 in 1937.
Since

    1937 decreases are shown in the age groups between 10 and 29, between 55 and 69, and
also in the 95-99 group.
Increases were recorded for males in the quinquennial age

    groups comprising 0-9 years, 30-54 and 75-94 and for females in the age groups com-
    prising 0-9 years, 25-54, 70-94 and 100 years and upwards.
The following chart shows the distribution of the population for the County in the

    four main age groups for 1901, 1937 and 1951*

## Page 403

XXI

    It will be seen that betwe-en 1901 and 1937 the age group 0-14 decreased by 0.6
per cent,, but between 1937 and 1951 an increase of 2.2 per cent was recorded.
The
15-44 group showed a decrease of 3. 6 per cent in the former period and in the latter a
decrease of 1.1 per cent.
The gain of 0.5 per cent in the 45-64 years category between

    1901 and 1937 was more than lost by thS drop to 18.5 in 1951.
The 65 years and over

    group rose from 7.7 per-cent in 1901 to 11.2 per cent in 1937 and remained at the
latter figure in 1951.
The proportion of the population aged 45 years and upwards is
shown to have fallen between 1937 and 1951 by 1.1 per cent.
Marital Condition; Since 1937 the number of persons in the County aged 14 and

    over increased by 246; males decreased by 190 and females increased by 436. The
    number of single persons in this age group decreased by 4,088 (1,962 males and 2,126
females), while the number of married persons increased by 4,576 (2,160 males and
2,416 females).
Single males^ aged 14 and over-accounted for 52.7 pe,r cent of the male population of

    the same age iii 1951 (56*5 per cent in 1937) and married men for 42.6 per cent (38.0 in
1937).

    In the same age group, single females represented 42.6 per cent (47,6 in 1937)
    and married women 45.8 per cent (41.0 in 1937).
For both sexes the only quinquennial groups of married persons to show-a decrease

    during the 1937-1951 intercensal period were males 55-69 and 90-99 a M females 55-59
and 65-69.
Widowers decreased by 405 whilst widows increased by liQ. The number of persons
wtfose marriage* had been dissolved and who had not remarried, increased from 4 tn 1937

    to 48 in 1951 - divorced men numbered 20 as compared with 3 in 1937, and divorced
women 28 as against 1.
EDUCATION
Table 13 summarises the information furnished regarding attendance at educational
establishments.
It will be seen that for the County of children aged 5-13 inclusive
6.0 per cent are stated not to be attending school*
In the 14-17 age groups, i.e.,
amongst persons over the age for compulsory school attendance but of normal ag^ for
attending grammar school, 71. 7 per cent were not attending educational establishments.
Persons of all ages attending educational establishments part-time were shown to
number 101. As the total number of part-time students enrolled in the institutions of
further education under the management of the County Tyrone Education Authority during

    the year ended 31st July, 1951, numbered 2,073, it is apparent that in many cases the
information regarding partt-time education wa^ not furnished on th<e census returns.
Much the same result was disclosed by the earlier census reports.
It would appear
that the significance of the question was not appreciated by the heads of households.
BIRTHPLACES
Table 14 shows, by sex, the number of persons bom in each of the counties of
Northern Ireland, other parts of the British Isles, Commonwealth Countries, etc..
Foreign Countries and at sea. Of the total population of the County (i.e., 132,082)

    persons bom in the County numbered 107,844 (81,6 per cent), and altogether 120,847
(91.5 per cent) were bom in some part of Northern Ireland.*
6,599 persons (5.0 per
cent) were bom in the Irish Republic as compared with 6,228 (4.9 per cent) in 1937.
Only 748 persons (0*6 per cent) were bom outside the British Isles, of whom 315 were
bom in Commonwealth Countries, 431 in Foreign Countries, and 2 at sea.
The percentage representing persons bom in the County viz,., (81.6) has decreased
since 1937 when it stood at 84.4.
Table XII gives comparative figures for Censuses since 1861.
It will be noted that the percentage of the total population bom in Northern

    Ireland decreased steadily between 1861 and 1926, viz., from 97.06 to 9g.26.
Between

    1926 and 1937 there was an increase to 92.61 but this fell to 91.50 in 1951.
Over
the period 1861 to 1926 the percentage bom in the Irish Republic steadily increased

    (2.37 to 5.02); between 1926 and 1937 there was a decrease to 4.88, but in the last
int^rcensal period there was an increase to 5.00. The percentage bom in England and

    Wales stands at 1.70, the highest figure reached during the ninety years 1861-1951;

* Including Church of England and Episcopal Church of Scotland, numbering 970 and 1 respectively.
A scrutiny of Table XIV reveals that the percentage Roman Catholic of the whole
population was at its highest point (56.5) in 1861.
The lowest figures recorded were

    54.6 in 1891 and 54.7 in 1901.
At other enumerations it fluctuated between 55.3 and

    55.6 and stood at 55.4 in 1951.
The percentage Presbyterian remained at the same

    figure, 18.6, in 1911, 1926 and 1937 and increased to 18.7 in 1951.
The highest
figure for this denomination was 19.7 per cent registered in 1901.
The Church of

    Ireland decreased by 0.6 per cent between 1937 and 1951 (from 22.4 to SI.8) and at the
latter year stood at its lowest point.
Methodists increased by 0.2 per cent of the

    total population between 1937 and 1951 and the figure for 1951 (2.2) is the highest
recorded.
Other and Not Stated Denominations"' have increased at each enumeration

    since 1901 and show a percentage of 1.9, which is an increase of .0.2 on the 1937
figure.
A comparison of the 1951 figures with those for 1937 shows that there were
numerical increases in all groups, viz., Roman Catholics 2,537, Presbyterians 1,038,

    Church of Ireland 202, Methodists 390, others 329.
Table 16 shows the population by sex for each County District under the five
religious groups, viz., Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church of Ireland (including
the Church of En-gland and the Episcopal Church of Scotland) , Methodist and all other
(or not stated) denominations.
The list of descriptions of "Other Denominations"
given at the foot of the table refers to the County as a whole; the names of
denominations are restricted to those having not less than ten adherents.
Of the "Other Denominations" the two categories which show the greatest variation

    as compared with 1937 are: Baptist 356 (an increase of lOO) and Brethren 1,254 (a
decrease of 136).
The number of persons who omitted to state their denomination has
increased from 97 to 338.
Table 17 gives details for the County under five religious categories and age
groups (individual years to age 21, and quinquennial age groups up to 69 years).
The
comparative Table in the 1937 Report gave the population by individual ages up to 20,
and by two religious groups - Roman Catholics and other denominations.
It is not
therefore possible to make a complete comparison between the 1937 figures and those
published in this Report, but it is observed that the overall increase in the popula-
tion of Roman Catholics up to the age of 20 is 2,415 or 8.9 per cent, while the
increase in the population of all other denominations up to that age Is 1,089 or 5.2
per cent.
The following are the percentage increases or decreases in the various

    age groups up to 19 years and of the population aged 20 and over between 1937 and
1951:-

A comparison of Tables 15 and 16 shows that in the County 7.7 per cent of Roman
Catholics, 8.2 per cent of Presbyterians, 10.6 per cent of members of the Church of
Ireland, 9.7 per cent of Methodists, and 10.1 per cent of other and not stated
denominations were born outside Northern Ireland.
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
As will be seen from the voliomes "Classification of Occupations" and "Classifica-
tion of Industries" referred to in Part I of this Report the separate terms under which
workers are classified run into many thousands.
This has occasioned modifications to
bring census data within manageable dimensions, and thus generally speaking the
arrangement of headings is according to class of materials worked with, commodities
produced or services rendered.
Special attention is drawn to the following changes since the 1926 Census which
is the last occasion on which occupational and industrial enquiries were included in
the Northern Ireland Census Schedule:-

    (1) The 1951 Tables deal with persons aged 14 years and over, whereas in 1926
the age of 12 was adopted as the minimum.
For Table X7 the 1926 figures were
adjusted to the 1951 age grouping to allow proper comparison but see footnote to
that table.
See footnote to Table XVI also.
(2) A revision in the Classification of Occupations since 1926 brings together
the bulk of the unskilled workers in a single group - Order XXVI.
Occupations; Table XV sets out the numbers and percentages of persons occupied
(i.e., persons whose normal occupation was stated) in the age group from 14 years and
upwards, for the Censuses 1926 and 1951.
The term "occupied" includes persons temporarily out of work.
The number

    returned as temporarily out of work in 1926 for the County was 3,183 (2,224 males and
959 females).

    In 1951, the figure amounted to 4,078 (3,289 males and 789 females).

    aged 14 years and upwards, whereas in 1951 the percentage was 15.3 and of females aged

I t is probable also that the percentage of occupied persons - both male
and female - of the age group 65 and over decreased considerably between the Censuses.

## Page 407

XXV
The number of occupied males has decreased by 2,335 since 1926 and the number of
occupied females by 1,280, making an overall decrease of 3,615 in the number of
occupied persons.

    In 1926 there were 29 occupied females to every 100 occupied males, while in 1951
there were 28 occupied females to every 100 occupied males.

From Table XVI it will be seen that the occupied represented 42. 7 per cent of the

    population of both sexes and all ages in 1926 and in 1951 the percentage was 40.2.
In County Armagh the decrease was by 2.4 per cent and in County Londonderry 1.4 per
cent.
The figures for occupied persons as a percentage of the total population (both

    sexes) of all ages in 1911 were - males 33.3; females 10.2; persons 43.5.
Decreases
accordingly were recorded for both males and females between 1911 and 1926 and also
between 1926 and 1951.
Table 18 gives the full list of occupations of persons of 14 years of age and
over and Table XVII (overleaf) shows the proportions per 1,000 of the occupied males
and females under the numerically important Orders and also for niimerically important
single or grouped occupations.
51.2 per cent of the occupied males are engaged in Agricultural Occupations;
7.0 per cent in Commerical, Finance and Insurance Occupations (excluding Clerical);
6.4 per cent are engaged as workers in Building and Contracting, etc., and 5. 7 per cent
in Transport and Communications.
21.8 per cent of occupied females are engaged in
Personal Service;
14.9 per cent are employed as Textile workers; 13.2 per cent on the
manufacture of Textile Goods and Articles of Dress and 12.1 per cent are in Professional
and Technical Occupations (excluding Clerical).
The changes since 1926 in the Classification o f Occupations and especially the
introduction of Order XXVI bringing together groups of \inskilled workers not provided
for elsewhere (formerly included in various Orders) make it impracticable to give a
general comparison between 1926 and 1951 figures.
Jt is, however, possible to give
comparative figures in some instances.
The following are those which show the
greatest variation in numbers and which are broadly similar to the 1926 classification:-

## Page 409

Industries; The industrial classification has not undergone widespread changes
since 1926 as was the case in the classification of occupations.
The 1926 and 1951
figures, however, are Jiot Strictly comparable as the 1926 figures for the occupied
population include persons out of work (i.e., 2,224 males and 959 females) whereas
the 1951 figures exclude persons out of work, in accordance with the practice
throughout the United Kingdom for this Census.
Table XV'III shows the distribution for 1951 according to certain main industrial

Agriculture and Fishing provide employment for 43 per cent of the occupied popula-
tion.
In 1926 the percentage was 57.
The Manufacturing Industries (used in a wide
sense) provide 26 per cent and the Distributive Trades and Finance (including Insurance

    and Banking) 10 per cent; in 1926 the percentages were 16 and 8 respectively.
Table XVIII also shows that the males outnumber the females in all groups except
the Professions and Miscellaneous Services.
The former group includes Teachers and
Nurses and the latter, persons employed in Private Domestic Service, Restaurants,
Entertainments, etc.
The same balance was recorded in respect of earlier Reports for
1951.
Foi* males Agriculture and Fishing provide employment for 52 per cent of the
occupied, and the Manufacturing Industries (mainly Textiles, Building and Contracting

    and Manufacture of Foods) 22 per cent; in 1926 the percentage for the former was 67
and 13 for the latter.
The Manufacturing Industries (mainly Textiles and Clothing) give employment to

    39 per cent of the occupied females; in 1926 the percentage was 26.
Table 21 gives the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, separate figures being given for each Order and suitable groupings thereunder.

The entire town contains 265 persons.

(a)
The remaining part of Mid-Ulster is comprised of the Rural District of Magjierafelt in the County of
Londonderry, the figures for which a r e : -
Population, 31,206;
No. of Electors (All Qualifications),
19,434.
(b)
The remaining part of this constituency i s Co. Fermanagh, the figures for which a r e : -
Population, 53,044;
No. of Electors (All Qualifications), 34,437.
(c)
For .all Mid-Ulster there are 67,832 electors with Residence Qualifications and in Fermanagh and South Tyrone
there are 66,609 such electors.
There is one Member of Parliament for the whole constituency of Mid-Ulster and one for Fermanagh and
South Tyrone.
